**Step 3 — Static Head** = +5 m (lift).

**Step 4 — Total Head** the pump must supply:

$$
H_{pump} = (z_{2}-z_{1}) + \frac{v_{2}^{2}-v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + h_{f} = 5 + 0.476 + 3.5 = 8.976 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic Power** delivered to water:

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho g \dot V H_{pump} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02 \times 8.976 = 1761 \text{ W} \approx 1.76 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Electrical Input Power** with efficiency $\eta = 0.70$:

$$
P_{elec} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta} = \frac{1761}{0.70} = 2516 \text{ W} \approx 2.52 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{electrical} \approx 2.52 \text{ kW}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — Energy Equations Module 1**
> - The **First Law of Thermodynamics** is the only physical principle behind every energy equation.
> - Use **enthalpy $H = U + pV$** for flow processes; use **internal energy $U$** for closed/batch systems.
> - Always pick **one reference state** ($T_{ref}$, $H_{ref} = 0$) and stay consistent — relative enthalpies are what matter.
> - For a **heat exchanger** at steady state with no phase change: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}C_{p}\Delta T$.
> - For a **compressor/turbine** at steady state, adiabatic: $W_{s} = \dot{m}(H_{2}-H_{1})$.
> - The **mechanical energy balance** (Bernoulli + friction + shaft work) is for **kinetic, potential, pressure, and friction** terms only — it does **not** include internal energy.
> - The **general differential energy equation** contains four terms: accumulation, convection, conduction, and work.
> - The **substantial derivative** $\frac{DT}{Dt} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \vec v\cdot\nabla T$ tracks a fluid particle, not a fixed point.
> - Always watch out for **units**: 1 kW = 1 kJ/s; 1 kJ/h = 1/3600 kW.
> - For an **adiabatic reactor**: $\sum \dot{m}_{out}H_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}H_{in} = -\Delta H_{rxn}$.
> - For **reversible isothermal compression** of an ideal gas: $W_{rev} = \dot{n}RT\ln(p_{2}/p_{1})$.
> - For **reversible adiabatic** (isentropic) compression: $W_{rev} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\dot{n}R T_{1}\!\left[\left(\tfrac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}-1\right]$.
> - The **isentropic efficiency** of a compressor is $\eta_{isen} = W_{isen}/W_{actual}$.
> - Latent heat + sensible heat problems (e.g., vaporization + superheat): split into **two sequential** energy integrals.
> - **Negative work** = work done ON the system (pump, compressor); **positive work** = work done BY the system (turbine, engine).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is a fundamental Partial Differential Equation (PDE) that mathematically expresses the **Principle of Conservation of Mass** for a flowing fluid (or any continuous medium) within a defined control volume. In the context of Process Modelling and Simulation, it serves as the foundational mass-balance building block from which nearly all transport phenomena models (momentum, energy, species) are derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT631, Module 1):**
> The Continuity Equation is the mathematical statement of the law of conservation of mass applied to a fluid in motion. It states that the rate of change of mass within a control volume is equal to the net rate of mass flow into the volume plus the net rate of mass generation (which is **zero** for a non-reacting single-component system).

The general vector (differential) form of the continuity equation is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0
$$

where:
- $\rho$ is the fluid density (kg/m³)
- $t$ is time (s)
- $\vec{v}$ is the velocity vector (m/s)
- $\nabla \cdot$ is the divergence operator

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **garden hose connected to a large overhead water tank**. Open the tap, and water rushes out. The amount of water inside the hose never magically disappears or appears from nowhere — what flows in from the tank **must equal** what flows out of the nozzle (plus any tiny accumulation inside the hose wall if pressure builds). That is conservation of mass in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Highway Traffic Analogy":**
> Think of cars flowing on a six-lane highway. If the density of cars (cars/km) on a stretch of road **increases** over time, it is *only* because more cars entered that stretch than left it. Conversely, if the density is dropping, more cars are exiting than entering. The Continuity Equation is just the mathematical translation of this accounting identity for a fluid, where "cars" are replaced by "mass," "highway" by "control volume," and "entry/exit" by "mass flux."

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fluid density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | Mass per unit volume |
| Velocity vector | $\vec{v}$ | **m/s** | Fluid motion field |
| Mass flux | $\vec{J} = \rho \vec{v}$ | **kg/(m²·s)** | Mass crossing unit area per unit time |
| Specific volume | $v$ | **m³/kg** | Reciprocal of density |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Quick-Fact:** For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ is a **constant** ($\rho \neq f(x,y,z,t)$). This is the single most important assumption examiners test, because it collapses the Continuity Equation to its simplest physical form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — Conservation of Mass

The Continuity Equation is built upon the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)**, which links system (Lagrangian) and control-volume (Eulerian) perspectives. The four-pillar logical flow is:

1. **Mass can neither be created nor destroyed** in a non-reacting, non-nuclear system (postulate of classical mechanics).
2. **Rate of accumulation** of mass inside a control volume (CV) is governed by the difference between **inflow and outflow**, plus any volumetric generation.
3. For a **single chemical species without reaction**, the generation term is **identically zero**.
4. The RTT mathematically converts the system-based derivative into a CV-based integral, producing the **Integral Form** of the continuity equation.

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Forms

The Continuity Equation is conventionally written in **three equivalent forms**, each valuable in different engineering contexts:

- **Integral Form** — Used for macroscopic balances over reactors, pipes, mixers.
- **Differential Form** — Used for CFD and detailed field analysis.
- **Simplified Special Form** — Used for hand-calculation problems in KTU exams.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Mathematical Form

#### A. Integral (Macroscopic) Form

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

- **Why use it?** When you only care about total mass entering/leaving a piece of equipment (e.g., a stirred-tank reactor), this is the most efficient and intuitive form.

#### B. Differential (Microscopic) Form

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

- **Why use it?** When you need a point-by-point field description (velocity, density at every $(x,y,z,t)$), this is the workhorse of CFD solvers.

#### C. Incompressible Simplified Form

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0
$$

- **Why use it?** Water, oils, and low-speed gas flows are treated as incompressible. The density drops out of the equation, leaving a clean divergence-free velocity field.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. They are the highest-weightage formulas in this module for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Form / Case | Equation | Key Condition | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Vector (Differential)** | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ | None (most general) | CFD, compressible flow |
| 2 | **Cartesian Expansion** | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cartesian coordinates | Pipe flow, channel flow |
| 3 | **Cylindrical Expansion** | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cylindrical coordinates | Tubular reactors, rotating machinery |
| 4 | **Spherical Expansion** | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial (r^2 \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r \sin\theta}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta \sin\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r \sin\theta}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\phi)}{\partial \phi} = 0$ | Spherical coordinates | Droplet evaporation, packed beds |
| 5 | **Steady-State Form** | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ | $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$ | Continuous processes at design operation |
| 6 | **Incompressible Form** | $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ | $\rho = \text{const}$ | Liquids, low-Mach gas flow |
| 7 | **1-D Pipe (Integral)** | $\frac{d}{dt}(\rho A L) = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$ | Uniform cross-section | Plug flow reactors |
| 8 | **Substantial Derivative Form** | $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0$ | Material derivative | Compressible flow analysis |

> **Notation Safeguard:** The symbol $\vert$ for absolute value is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to preserve table integrity (e.g., $\vert v \vert$ for speed magnitude).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Continuity Equation is the silent workhorse behind:
- **Chemical Reactor Design** — Tracking species mass in CSTR/PFR.
- **Distillation Columns** — Mass balance across trays.
- **Piping Network Design** — Bernoulli's equation derivation begins here.
- **CFD Solvers (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, COMSOL)** — Solved alongside Navier-Stokes equations.
- **Atmospheric and Oceanic Modelling** — Weather prediction, ocean current simulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Integral Form of the Continuity Equation

**Starting Premise (Law of Conservation of Mass):**

$$
\binom{\text{Rate of mass}}{\text{accumulation in CV}} = \binom{\text{Net mass flow}}{\text{into the CV}}
$$

**Step 1: Express the accumulation term.**
The mass contained within the control volume at any instant is:

$$
m_{CV}(t) = \int_{CV} \rho(\vec{r}, t) \, dV
$$

Hence the rate of accumulation is:

$$
\frac{d m_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV
$$

**Step 2: Express the net mass flow across the control surface.**
The mass flow rate across a differential surface element $dA$ is $\rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$, where $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal. The net inflow is the negative of the outflow integral:

$$
\dot{m}_{net,in} = - \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA
$$

**Step 3: Equate accumulation and net flow (no generation).**

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

> This is the **Integral Form** of the Continuity Equation. It is a *scalar* equation valid for any arbitrary control volume.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Form via the Divergence Theorem

**Step 1: Apply the Divergence Theorem to the surface integral.**

By the Divergence (Gauss) Theorem:

$$
\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the integral form.**

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 3: Combine integrals over the same CV.**

$$
\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0
$$

> *(Note: Since the CV is fixed in space, the time derivative moves inside the integral, becoming a partial derivative.)*

**Step 4: Apply the "arbitrary volume" argument.**
Because the integral equals zero for *any* arbitrary control volume, the integrand itself must vanish at every point in the flow field:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cartesian Expansion

Expanding the divergence operator in Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

> This is the form most often reproduced in KTU derivations and problem statements.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Incompressible Special Form

**Assumption:** $\rho = \text{constant}$ (independent of $x, y, z, t$).

**Step 1:** Differentiate the constant-density identity.

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Expand $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$ using the **product rule for divergence**:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho
$$

**Step 3:** Since $\rho$ is constant, $\nabla \rho = 0$, giving:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v})
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the general equation and divide by $\rho$:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0
$$

In Cartesian components:

$$
\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Meaning of $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$:** "What flows in must flow out." The volume expansion rate of a fluid element is zero for incompressible flow.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Cylindrical Coordinate Expansion

Using the standard divergence operator in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r A_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial A_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}
$$

Apply this to $\vec{A} = \rho \vec{v}$:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}
$$

Final cylindrical continuity equation:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Water (incompressible, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) flows steadily through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D_1 = 0.10 \text{ m}$ at velocity $v_1 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$. The pipe contracts to a nozzle of diameter $D_2 = 0.05 \text{ m}$. Find the exit velocity $v_2$.

**Solution using the 1-D incompressible continuity equation:**

**Step 1:** Incompressible 1-D continuity:

$$
A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the areas.

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $v_2$.

$$
v_2 = \frac{A_1 v_1}{A_2} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.0}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Halving the diameter reduces the area by a factor of 4, so the velocity must increase by a factor of 4 — a direct consequence of the Continuity Equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Conservation Law to Solver-Ready Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Principle: Conservation of Mass] --> B[Select Control Volume CV]
    B --> C{Geometry of CV}
    C -->|Pipes, ducts, channels| D[Cartesian Coordinates]
    C -->|Tubes, reactors, shafts| E[Cylindrical Coordinates]
    C -->|Droplets, packed beds| F[Spherical Coordinates]
    D --> G[Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Divergence Theorem]
    H --> I[General Differential Continuity Equation]
    I --> J{Assumption Check}
    J -->|rho = constant| K[Incompressible Form: div v = 0]
    J -->|Steady state| L[Steady Form: div rhov = 0]
    J -->|1D, uniform A| M[1D Form: A v = constant]
    K --> N[Solver Ready PDE]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Logical Flow: Derivation Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Define mCV integral] --> S2[Step 2: Express mass flux across CS]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Equate accumulation and net flow]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Divergence Theorem]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Arbitrary CV argument]
    S5 --> S6[Final PDE: d rho dt plus div rhov = 0]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Control Volume with Mass Flux

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CV[Control Volume CV bounded by Control Surface CS]
        D1[Inlet Stream: mdot in, rho in, v in] -->|Mass flux entering| M[Fluid Element inside CV]
        M -->|Mass flux leaving| D2[Outlet Stream: mdot out, rho out, v out]
        M -->|Accumulation rate| ACC[dmCV dt = partial rho partial t dV]
    end
    ACC --> EQ[Continuity: Accumulation = In - Out]
    D1 --> EQ
    D2 --> EQ
```

### 4.4 Form-Selection Decision Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need pointwise field description?} -->|Yes| Q2{Compressible flow?}
    Q1 -->|No, only total flow| R1[Use Integral Form]
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[General Differential Form]
    Q2 -->|No, rho = const| R3[Incompressible: div v = 0]
    R1 --> END[Apply to Problem]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**State and explain the general continuity equation in differential form. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**

The general continuity equation in differential form is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0
$$

**Physical Significance:**
- The first term, $\partial \rho / \partial t$, represents the **local (unsteady) rate of change of density** at a fixed point in space.
- The second term, $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$, represents the **net convective mass flux divergence** — i.e., the net rate at which mass leaves a unit volume per unit time.
- The sum being zero is the **pointwise statement of mass conservation**: any local increase in density must be exactly balanced by a net inflow of mass, and vice versa.

> **Examiner's Note (3 marks):** Award 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for identifying each term, and 1 mark for the conservation statement. Sketches earn bonus half-marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**For an incompressible fluid, the continuity equation simplifies to $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$. Justify this simplification with the necessary assumption.**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumption:** The fluid density $\rho$ is **constant in space and time** ($\rho \neq f(x, y, z, t)$).

**Justification (3-step logic):**
1. The general equation is $\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$.
2. Since $\rho = \text{const}$, the first term vanishes: $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$.
3. The product rule for divergence gives $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$. Since $\nabla \rho = 0$, this reduces to $\rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0$, and dividing by the non-zero $\rho$ yields $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$.

> **Examiner's Note:** Failure to state the assumption is the most common reason for losing 1 mark here.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (PECHT631):** Module 1 internal choice is provided. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **integral form** of the continuity equation for a fluid flowing through an arbitrary control volume. State clearly all assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1:** No mass generation (single-component, non-reacting).
**Assumption 2:** Mass is conserved (classical mechanics).
**Assumption 3:** The fluid is a continuum (valid at length scales much larger than molecular mean free path).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Mass inside the control volume:

$$
m_{CV}(t) = \int_{CV} \rho \, dV
$$

Rate of accumulation:

$$
\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Mass flux across a differential element of the control surface is $\rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$. Net outflow is the positive integral (since $\hat{n}$ points outward):

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Conservation statement: Accumulation + Net Outflow = 0 (with no generation):

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** This is the **Integral Form of the Continuity Equation**, valid for any fixed control volume.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Starting from the integral form, **derive the differential (point) form** of the continuity equation in Cartesian coordinates.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the Divergence Theorem:

$$
\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute back into the integral equation and combine:

$$
\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Since the integrand must vanish for *any* arbitrary CV, the bracket itself must be zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Expand in Cartesian $(x, y, z)$:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write the **continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates** and explain the physical meaning of each term.

**Model Solution:**

The continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Term-by-term physical meaning (7 marks, 1.5 marks each + 1 for assembly):**

- $\partial \rho / \partial t$: **Local (unsteady) accumulation** of mass per unit volume.
- $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r}$: **Radial mass flux divergence** — accounts for the cylindrical geometry (the factor $1/r$ arises from the surface area of a cylindrical shell being $2\pi r L$).
- $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta}$: **Azimuthal (angular) mass flux divergence**.
- $\frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}$: **Axial mass flux divergence** — dominant term in tubular reactors and pipe flow.

**Engineering Application:** This form is the standard governing equation for **tubular chemical reactors**, **rotating annular space**, and **helical coil heat exchangers**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **2-cm diameter pipe** carries an incompressible liquid at a steady flow rate of $4 \text{ L/s}$. The pipe branches into three identical parallel pipes of diameter $1 \text{ cm}$ each. Determine the velocity in each branch pipe.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Main pipe: $D_1 = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $Q_1 = 4 \text{ L/s} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.
- Each branch: $D_2 = 1 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$, number of branches = 3.
- Steady, incompressible flow.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Continuity for incompressible steady flow: $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$.

$$
Q_1 = 3 Q_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_2 = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 1.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Cross-sectional area of each branch:

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.01)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Velocity in each branch:

$$
v_2 = \frac{Q_2}{A_2} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} = 16.98 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Result:

$$
\boxed{v_2 \approx 17.0 \text{ m/s}}
$$

> **Dimensional check:** $Q_2 / A_2$ has units $\text{m}^3/\text{s} \div \text{m}^2 = \text{m/s}$. ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Continuity Equation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "No Generation" assumption explicitly** — Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** if you do not state that there is no mass source/sink for a single-component non-reacting system.
> 2. **Confusing the substantial derivative $D\rho/Dt$ with $\partial \rho/\partial t$** — The former follows the fluid particle, the latter is taken at a fixed point. This is a classic **2-mark trap**.
> 3. **Wrong sign in the integral form** — The surface integral $\int \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) dA$ represents **outflow** (because $\hat{n}$ points outward). Writing it as inflow without the negative sign will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Skipping the "arbitrary volume" argument** — When deducing the differential form from the integral form, you must state explicitly that the equation holds for *any* CV, therefore the integrand vanishes. Examiners dock **1–2 marks** for skipping this.
> 5. **Mixing up incompressible vs. steady-state** — These are **independent** assumptions. A flow can be steady but compressible (e.g., flow in a converging nozzle at constant mass flow rate), or unsteady but incompressible (e.g., oscillating water in a U-tube).
> 6. **Forgetting the $1/r$ and $r^2$ geometric factors** in cylindrical/spherical forms — this is the most common error in coordinate-system conversions, costing **2–3 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Your Exam):**

- ✅ The Continuity Equation is the **mathematical statement of conservation of mass** for a flowing fluid.
- ✅ The **general (vector) form** is $\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$.
- ✅ The **integral form** uses a control volume + control surface: $\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$.
- ✅ The **differential form** is derived from the integral form by applying the **Divergence Theorem** and the **arbitrary-volume argument**.
- ✅ For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho = \text{const}$), the equation collapses to $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$.
- ✅ For **steady flow**, $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$, giving $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$.
- ✅ **Cartesian** form has three linear terms; **Cylindrical** form has the $1/r$ geometric factor; **Spherical** form has both $1/r$ and $1/r^2$ factors.
- ✅ The 1-D pipe-flow simplification is $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ (for incompressible flow), or $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$ (for compressible flow).
- ✅ **Three core assumptions** to always state: (i) no chemical reaction, (ii) single-component system, (iii) continuum assumption valid.
- ✅ The equation is a **scalar PDE** (one equation, one dependent variable $\rho$ when $\vec{v}$ is known, or two unknowns when both $\rho$ and $\vec{v}$ must be solved).
- ✅ It is the **first equation solved** in any CFD simulation — typically alongside the Navier-Stokes (momentum) equations.
- ✅ Real-world check: doubling the pipe diameter reduces velocity by a factor of 4 (for constant flow rate) — derived directly from the continuity equation.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Continuity Equation**
*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECHT631 — Process Modelling and Simulation.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equation of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation of Motion (EOM):** A mathematical statement of **Newton's Second Law of Motion** applied to a continuum fluid element. It expresses the principle of **conservation of linear momentum** for a fluid in motion, relating the rate of change of momentum to the sum of forces (pressure, viscous, gravity, and external body forces) acting on the fluid element.

In the context of **Process Modelling and Simulation (PECHT631)**, the Equation of Motion is derived through a **shell momentum balance** on a thin fluid shell, followed by letting the shell thickness tend to zero, yielding the general differential form of the momentum balance.

For a Newtonian fluid with constant density $\rho$ and constant viscosity $\mu$, the Equation of Motion in vector notation reduces to the famous **Navier–Stokes Equation**:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Cross-Reference (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> The Equation of Motion is also referred to as the **Momentum Balance Equation** or the **Cauchy Equation of Motion** (inviscid form). The full viscous form for compressible Newtonian fluids is the **Navier–Stokes Equation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a thick, sticky ball of honey across a table with your finger.
- The **pressure force** is like the push of your finger (a normal stress).
- The **viscous force** is the honey's internal resistance to deformation (it sticks to your finger and the table).
- The **gravity force** is the honey's own weight pulling it downward.

The **Equation of Motion** is essentially the accounting ledger that balances these three forces (plus any external body force) against the honey's *acceleration* — i.e., how fast its velocity is changing. If you stopped pushing, the honey would decelerate due to viscosity. If you added more honey (more mass), it would decelerate faster. That exact balancing act is what the EOM does mathematically for *any* flowing fluid.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants You Must Memorize:**
> - Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Density of water at 4 °C: $\rho_{H_2O} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C: $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

### 1.3 Place in the KTU Hierarchy of Transport Equations

The Equation of Motion is one of the **three fundamental conservation laws** in transport phenomena:

| Conservation Law | Resulting Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Mass | Continuity Equation | Fluid is neither created nor destroyed |
| **Momentum** | **Equation of Motion** | **Newton's 2nd law for a fluid element** |
| Energy | Energy Equation | 1st Law of Thermodynamics for a fluid element |

These three, together with an equation of state, form the **closed set** needed to solve a vast majority of chemical engineering flow problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation Strategy — The Shell Momentum Balance

KTU examiners love testing the *logic* of the shell balance. The standard procedure is:

1. **Choose a coordinate system** (rectangular, cylindrical, or spherical) depending on the geometry.
2. **Define a fluid element** (a thin shell of thickness $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$, $\Delta z$).
3. **Write Newton's 2nd Law** in the chosen direction:
   $$\sum F = m a = \rho \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z \frac{D v_x}{Dt}$$
4. **Enumerate all forces**:
   - Pressure forces on the 6 faces.
   - Viscous (shear) forces on the 6 faces.
   - Gravity force (a body force).
5. **Divide by the volume** $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$.
6. **Take the limit** as $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z \to 0$ to obtain the differential form.
7. **Simplify** assuming constant $\rho$ and $\mu$ to obtain the Navier–Stokes equation.

### 2.2 The General Equation of Motion (Vector Form)

For a Newtonian fluid with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$$

where the **substantial (material) derivative** is:

$$\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Full Navier–Stokes (vector) | Newtonian, constant $\rho,\mu$ |
| 2 | $\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \dfrac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial z^{2}} \right) + \rho g_x$ | Navier–Stokes in $x$-direction (rectangular) | Newtonian, constant $\rho,\mu$ |
| 3 | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$ | Euler's Equation (inviscid flow) | Frictionless flow |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} + P + \rho g z = \text{constant}$ | Bernoulli's Equation (mechanical energy balance) | Steady, inviscid, incompressible, along a streamline |
| 5 | $\sum F_{x} = \rho V \dfrac{D v_x}{Dt}$ | Shell momentum balance (rectangular $x$-direction) | Any fluid, before limiting process |
| 6 | $\tau_{yx} = \mu \dfrac{d v_x}{d y}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity (1-D shear stress) | Newtonian fluid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** All terms in the Navier–Stokes equation must reduce to $\text{N/m}^{3}$ (force per unit volume). The LHS is inertial; the RHS terms are pressure gradient, viscous, and body force — all in $\text{N/m}^{3}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of chemical reactors and piping**: Predicting pressure drop, velocity profiles, and pump requirements.
- **Boundary layer analysis**: Flow over flat plates, around submerged objects (aerodynamics, ship hulls).
- **Non-Newtonian flow in food/polymer industries**: Modified EOMs account for yield stress and shear-thinning.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)**: The Navier–Stokes equation is the cornerstone of every commercial CFD solver (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, COMSOL).
- **Microfluidics and biomedical devices**: Predicting blood flow in capillaries and drug delivery systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the x-Component of the Equation of Motion (Rectangular Coordinates)

This is the **most exam-relevant derivation** in KTU Module 1.

**Step 0 — Statement of Newton's Second Law for a fluid element of volume $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$:**

$$\sum F_x = m a_x = \rho (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z) \frac{D v_x}{Dt}$$

**Step 1 — Express the substantial derivative:**

$$\frac{D v_x}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure forces in the x-direction (acting on the 6 faces):**

Force at face $x$ (entering): $P \mid_{x} \Delta y \Delta z$
Force at face $x + \Delta x$ (exiting): $P \mid_{x+\Delta x} \Delta y \Delta z$

The **net pressure force in $x$** is:

$$F_{P,x} = \left[ P \mid_{x} - P \mid_{x+\Delta x} \right] \Delta y \Delta z$$

**Step 3 — Viscous (shear) forces in the x-direction:**

Four shear stresses act on the four faces perpendicular to the $y$ and $z$ axes: $\tau_{yx}$ and $\tau_{zx}$.

At face $y$: $\tau_{yx} \mid_{y} \Delta x \Delta z$
At face $y+\Delta y$: $\tau_{yx} \mid_{y+\Delta y} \Delta x \Delta z$
At face $z$: $\tau_{zx} \mid_{z} \Delta x \Delta y$
At face $z+\Delta z$: $\tau_{zx} \mid_{z+\Delta z} \Delta x \Delta y$

The **net viscous force in $x$** is:

$$F_{V,x} = \left[ \tau_{yx} \mid_{y} - \tau_{yx} \mid_{y+\Delta y} \right] \Delta x \Delta z + \left[ \tau_{zx} \mid_{z} - \tau_{zx} \mid_{z+\Delta z} \right] \Delta x \Delta y$$

**Step 4 — Gravity (body) force in the x-direction:**

$$F_{G,x} = \rho (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z) g_x$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all forces into Newton's 2nd Law:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z) \frac{D v_x}{Dt} &= \left[ P \mid_{x} - P \mid_{x+\Delta x} \right] \Delta y \Delta z \\
&\quad + \left[ \tau_{yx} \mid_{y} - \tau_{yx} \mid_{y+\Delta y} \right] \Delta x \Delta z \\
&\quad + \left[ \tau_{zx} \mid_{z} - \tau_{zx} \mid_{z+\Delta z} \right] \Delta x \Delta y \\
&\quad + \rho (\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z) g_x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} &= -\frac{ P \mid_{x+\Delta x} - P \mid_{x} }{\Delta x} \\
&\quad -\frac{ \tau_{yx} \mid_{y+\Delta y} - \tau_{yx} \mid_{y} }{\Delta y} \\
&\quad -\frac{ \tau_{zx} \mid_{z+\Delta z} - \tau_{zx} \mid_{z} }{\Delta z} \\
&\quad + \rho g_x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Take the limit** as $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z \to 0$:

$$\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x$$

**Step 8 — Substitute Newton's Law of Viscosity** (for a Newtonian fluid):

$$\tau_{yx} = \mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}, \qquad \tau_{zx} = \mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}$$

**Step 9 — Final result (x-component Navier–Stokes):**

$$\boxed{\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial z^{2}} \right) + \rho g_x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> By cyclic permutation of $x \to y \to z \to x$, we obtain the $y$ and $z$ components.

### 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Equation (Inviscid Limit)

Setting $\mu = 0$ in the Navier–Stokes equation:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$$

For **steady, inviscid, incompressible flow along a streamline**, integrate from point 1 to point 2:

$$\int_{1}^{2} \rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} \cdot d\vec{s} = -\int_{1}^{2} \nabla P \cdot d\vec{s} - \int_{1}^{2} \rho g \, dz$$

Using the identity $\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} \cdot d\vec{s} = \dfrac{D}{Dt}\left(\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}\right) dt$ and integrating:

$$\frac{P_{1}}{\rho} + g z_{1} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho} + g z_{2} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2}$$

This is the famous **Bernoulli Equation**.

### 3.3 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) flows steadily down a vertical pipe. The velocity profile is fully developed and given by $v_z(r) = v_{\max}\left(1 - \dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)$. Using the z-component of the Navier–Stokes equation, find the pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dz}$.

**Step 1 — Identify applicable form of EOM:**

For steady ($\partial / \partial t = 0$), fully developed, axisymmetric flow with $v_r = v_\theta = 0$, only $v_z \neq 0$:

$$\rho \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + \rho v_r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \rho \frac{v_\theta}{r} \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + \rho v_z \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial \theta^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial z^{2}} \right] + \rho g_z$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using the conditions:**

All terms on the LHS vanish: $\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} = 0$, $v_r = v_\theta = 0$, $\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Substitute $v_z(r)$ and compute derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} = -\frac{2 v_{\max} r}{R^{2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) = \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( -\frac{2 v_{\max} r^{2}}{R^{2}} \right) = -\frac{4 v_{\max}}{R^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the simplified EOM:**

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dz} + \mu \left( -\frac{4 v_{\max}}{R^{2}} \right) + \rho g_z$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the pressure gradient** (with $g_z = -g$ for downward flow, or $+g$ for upward):

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = \rho g_z - \frac{4 \mu v_{\max}}{R^{2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validity check:** For a downward flow ($z$ pointing down, $g_z = +g$) with no driving pressure gradient, we expect $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\rho g R^{2}}{4 \mu}$, which matches **Hagen–Poiseuille's** average result.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Anatomy of the Equation of Motion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fluid Element of Volume dV] --> B[Apply Newton's Second Law: Sum F equals m times Dv by Dt]
    B --> C1[Pressure Forces on 6 Faces]
    B --> C2[Viscous Shear Forces on 4 Faces]
    B --> C3[Body Force: Gravity rho g]
    C1 --> D[Net Force Vector in x, y, z directions]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Divide by dV and Take Limit as Dimensions go to Zero]
    E --> F[Substitute Newton's Law of Viscosity: tau equals mu times dv by dy]
    F --> G{Fluid Type and Assumptions}
    G -->|Newtonian, Constant rho and mu| H[Full Navier Stokes Equation in Vector Form]
    G -->|mu equals 0 Inviscid| I[Euler Equation of Motion]
    G -->|Steady, Inviscid, Incompressible, Streamline| J[Bernoulli Equation]
    H --> K[Final Differential Equation of Motion]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Shell Momentum Balance Element (Rectangular)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FL["Fluid Element with Dimensions Delta x, Delta y, Delta z"]
        FACE1["Face at x: Pressure P sub x acts in plus x direction"]
        FACE2["Face at x plus Delta x: Pressure P sub x plus Delta x acts in minus x direction"]
        FACE3["Face at y: Shear tau sub yx acts in plus x direction"]
        FACE4["Face at y plus Delta y: Shear tau sub yx plus Delta y acts in minus x direction"]
        FACE5["Face at z: Shear tau sub zx acts in plus x direction"]
        FACE6["Face at z plus Delta z: Shear tau sub zx plus Delta z acts in minus x direction"]
        BODY["Body Force: rho g sub x times volume acts downward or in plus x direction"]
    end
    FACE1 --> NETF["Net Force in x equals m times Dv sub x by Dt"]
    FACE2 --> NETF
    FACE3 --> NETF
    FACE4 --> NETF
    FACE5 --> NETF
    FACE6 --> NETF
    BODY --> NETF
    NETF --> LIMIT["Take limit Delta x Delta y Delta z to zero: yields differential EOM"]
```

### 4.3 Flowchart: Decision Tree for Choosing the Correct EOM Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Given Flow Problem} --> Q2{Is Viscosity Significant?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, Newtonian| Q3{Is Density Constant?}
    Q2 -->|No Negligible| EULER["Use Euler Equation: rho Dv by Dt equals minus grad P plus rho g"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is Flow Steady?}
    Q3 -->|No Compressible| COMP["Use General Compressible Navier Stokes: include variable rho and mu effects"]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5{Geometry Type?}
    Q4 -->|No Unsteady| UNS["Include partial v by partial t term in Navier Stokes"]
    Q5 -->|Pipe or Channel| PIPE["Use cylindrical coordinates, derive Poiseuille profile"]
    Q5 -->|Flat Plate| PLATE["Use boundary layer approximation, derive Blasius solution"]
    Q5 -->|Complex Geometry| CFD["Solve Navier Stokes numerically using CFD package"]
    EULER --> Q6{Integrable along Streamline?}
    Q6 -->|Yes Steady Inviscid Incompressible| BERNOULLI["Use Bernoulli: half rho v squared plus P plus rho g z equals constant"]
    Q6 -->|No| ROT["Include vorticity, use Crocco equation"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Equation of Motion. State its general vector form for a Newtonian fluid with constant density and viscosity.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Equation of Motion** is a mathematical statement of Newton's second law applied to a fluid element, expressing the principle of conservation of linear momentum. It states that the rate of change of momentum of a fluid element equals the sum of pressure, viscous, and body forces acting on it.
>
> **General vector form** for a Newtonian fluid with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$:
>
> $$\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$$
>
> where $\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}$ is the substantial derivative, $\nabla P$ is the pressure gradient, $\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{v}$ is the viscous term, and $\rho \vec{g}$ is the gravitational body force. **(3 Marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between Euler's equation of motion and the Navier–Stokes equation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Euler's Equation** is the equation of motion for an **inviscid (frictionless)** fluid. It is obtained by setting the viscosity $\mu = 0$ in the Navier–Stokes equation:
>
> $$\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$$
>
> - **Navier–Stokes Equation** is the equation of motion for a **viscous Newtonian fluid** with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$:
>
> $$\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$$
>
> The key difference is the presence of the **viscous term** $\mu \nabla^{2}\vec{v}$ in Navier–Stokes, which is absent in Euler's equation. Euler's equation is useful for ideal flow problems, while Navier–Stokes governs all real viscous flows. **(3 Marks)**

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from Newton's second law, derive the **x-component of the Navier–Stokes equation** for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant density and viscosity, using the shell momentum balance approach. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fluid flows steadily between two large parallel horizontal plates separated by a distance $2h$. The velocity profile is given by $v_x(y) = v_{\max}\left(1 - \dfrac{y^{2}}{h^{2}}\right)$. Using the Navier–Stokes equation, derive the relationship between the pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ and the maximum velocity $v_{\max}$. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Apply Newton's second law to a fluid element of volume $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$:
> $$\sum F_x = \rho \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z \frac{D v_x}{Dt}$$
>
> Enumerate forces:
> 1. **Pressure forces** on faces at $x$ and $x+\Delta x$: net force $= [P\mid_x - P\mid_{x+\Delta x}] \Delta y \Delta z$. **(1 Mark)**
> 2. **Viscous forces** on faces at $y$, $y+\Delta y$, $z$, $z+\Delta z$: net force $= [\tau_{yx}\mid_y - \tau_{yx}\mid_{y+\Delta y}] \Delta x \Delta z + [\tau_{zx}\mid_z - \tau_{zx}\mid_{z+\Delta z}] \Delta x \Delta y$. **(1 Mark)**
> 3. **Gravity force**: $\rho \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z \, g_x$. **(1 Mark)**
>
> Substituting and dividing by $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$:
>
> $$\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} = -\frac{P\mid_{x+\Delta x} - P\mid_x}{\Delta x} - \frac{\tau_{yx}\mid_{y+\Delta y} - \tau_{yx}\mid_y}{\Delta y} - \frac{\tau_{zx}\mid_{z+\Delta z} - \tau_{zx}\mid_z}{\Delta z} + \rho g_x$$
>
> Take the limit as $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z \to 0$:
>
> $$\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$
>
> Substituting Newton's law of viscosity $\tau_{yx} = \mu \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}$ and $\tau_{zx} = \mu \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}$:
>
> $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial z^{2}} \right) + \rho g_x$$
>
> **(1 Mark)** — final equation. **(1 Mark)** — statement of assumptions (constant $\rho, \mu$, Newtonian fluid, incompressible).

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**
> For steady ($\partial/\partial t = 0$), fully developed ($v_x = v_x(y)$ only), unidirectional ($v_y = v_z = 0$) flow between horizontal plates, the Navier–Stokes equation in the $x$-direction reduces to: **(1 Mark)**
>
> $$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial y^{2}}$$
>
> (Gravity acts in $z$-direction since plates are horizontal, so $g_x = 0$.) **(1 Mark)**
>
> Substituting $v_x(y) = v_{\max}\left(1 - \dfrac{y^{2}}{h^{2}}\right)$:
>
> $$\frac{d v_x}{d y} = -\frac{2 v_{\max} y}{h^{2}}$$
>
> $$\frac{d^{2} v_x}{d y^{2}} = -\frac{2 v_{\max}}{h^{2}}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)**
>
> Plugging back:
>
> $$0 = -\frac{dP}{dx} + \mu \left( -\frac{2 v_{\max}}{h^{2}} \right)$$
>
> $$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{2 \mu v_{\max}}{h^{2}}}$$
>
> The negative sign indicates that **pressure decreases in the direction of flow**. **(1 Mark)** for the physical interpretation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Bernoulli equation** from Euler's equation of motion for steady, inviscid, incompressible flow along a streamline. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water at 20 °C ($\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$) flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter 5 cm at a volumetric flow rate of $Q = 2 \text{ L/s}$. If a pressure gauge reads 150 kPa at section 1 and 120 kPa at section 2 (which is 4 m downstream, with the pipe diameter unchanged), estimate whether the **inviscid (Bernoulli) assumption** is valid. The dynamic viscosity of water is $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Euler's equation for inviscid flow:
>
> $$\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)** for invoking Euler's equation.
>
> **Assumptions** (state all): **(2 Marks)**
> 1. Steady flow.
> 2. Inviscid ($\mu = 0$).
> 3. Incompressible (constant $\rho$).
> 4. Integration along a streamline.
>
> For flow along a streamline parameterized by arc length $s$, the tangential component is:
>
> $$\rho v \frac{dv}{ds} = -\frac{dP}{ds} - \rho g \frac{dz}{ds}$$
>
> Rearranging:
>
> $$\frac{d}{ds}\left( \frac{P}{\rho} + gz + \frac{v^{2}}{2} \right) = 0$$
>
> **(1 Mark)**
>
> Integrating between points 1 and 2 on the streamline:
>
> $$\frac{P_{1}}{\rho} + g z_{1} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho} + g z_{2} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)**
>
> Multiplying through by $\rho$ and rewriting:
>
> $$P + \rho g z + \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} = \text{constant along a streamline}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)**
>
> This is the **Bernoulli equation**.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**
> **Step 1: Compute the average velocity.**
>
> $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^{2}}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$
>
> $$\langle v \rangle = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.019 \text{ m/s}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)**
>
> **Step 2: Compute the Reynolds number.**
>
> $$Re = \frac{\rho \langle v \rangle D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 1.019 \times 0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 5.08 \times 10^{4}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)**
>
> **Step 3: Compute the predicted pressure drop from Bernoulli.**
>
> Since $z_1 = z_2$ and $v_1 = v_2$ (same diameter, incompressible):
>
> $$\Delta P_{\text{Bernoulli}} = P_1 - P_2 = 150 - 120 = 30 \text{ kPa}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)**
>
> **Step 4: Compute the actual viscous pressure drop using Hagen–Poiseuille.**
>
> $$\Delta P_{\text{viscous}} = \frac{32 \mu L \langle v \rangle}{D^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 1.019}{(0.05)^{2}} \approx 52.2 \text{ Pa} = 0.0522 \text{ kPa}$$
>
> **(2 Marks)**
>
> **Step 5: Compare and conclude.**
>
> $$\frac{\Delta P_{\text{viscous}}}{\Delta P_{\text{Bernoulli}}} = \frac{0.0522}{30} \approx 0.00174 = 0.174\%$$
>
> The viscous pressure drop is **negligible** (less than 0.2%) compared to the Bernoulli-predicted pressure difference. However, the absolute drop of 30 kPa is unusual for an inviscid, constant-area horizontal pipe (Bernoulli would predict **zero** pressure drop). This means **most of the observed 30 kPa drop is due to elevation change, fittings, or other effects not captured in the simple Bernoulli model**, not viscous losses. The inviscid assumption is mathematically **invalid** here because the observed pressure drop contradicts Bernoulli for this geometry. **(2 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the substantial derivative expansion** — Writing $\rho \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t}$ instead of $\rho \dfrac{D v_x}{Dt}$ costs you **2 marks** instantly. KTU examiners are strict about this.
> 2. **Missing the gravity direction** — For horizontal flows, students often write $g_x \neq 0$ when in fact $g_x = 0$ and $g_z = -g$. Read the geometry carefully.
> 3. **Sign errors in the pressure term** — The correct form is $-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}$, not $+\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}$. High-pressure fluid pushes *into* the low-pressure region.
> 4. **Confusing Euler with Bernoulli** — Euler's equation is a vector PDE; Bernoulli is its integrated scalar form along a streamline. KTU questions often test this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — Every EOM you write must come with a clear list of assumptions (Newtonian, constant $\rho$, constant $\mu$, etc.). Missing this can cost **1–2 marks** even if the math is correct.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equation of Motion (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Newton's 2nd law applied to a fluid element; conservation of linear momentum.
- **Substantial derivative:** $\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}$ — must appear in the LHS.
- **Navier–Stokes (general):** $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$ — valid for Newtonian, constant $\rho$, constant $\mu$.
- **Euler's equation:** Set $\mu = 0$ → $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$.
- **Bernoulli equation:** Integral of Euler's equation along a streamline for steady, inviscid, incompressible flow → $P + \rho g z + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} = \text{constant}$.
- **Shell balance steps:** Choose coordinates → write $\sum F = ma$ → enumerate pressure, viscous, body forces → divide by volume → take limit → substitute Newton's law of viscosity.
- **Three coordinate systems to know:** Rectangular $(x, y, z)$, cylindrical $(r, \theta, z)$, spherical $(r, \theta, \phi)$. KTU tests all three.
- **Common special cases:** Hagen–Poiseuille (pipe flow), Couette flow (between plates), Stokes flow (low Re), Bernoulli (ideal flow).
- **Units check:** Every term in the EOM must reduce to $\text{N/m}^{3}$ — always verify dimensional consistency in your derivation.
- **Engineering applications:** Pump sizing, reactor design, CFD, boundary layer analysis, microfluidics, biomedical devices.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Momentum in = Momentum out + Accumulation"* — write this at the top of every momentum balance question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Transport Equations: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

A **transport equation** is a partial differential equation (PDE) that mathematically describes the conservation of a macroscopic property (mass, momentum, energy, or chemical species) as it is transported through a continuous medium by **convection** (bulk motion) and **diffusion** (molecular motion), while being generated or consumed by **sources/sinks**.

In the unified vector-tensor form used throughout chemical engineering simulation, the generalized transport equation for any property $\phi$ transported in a fluid of density $\rho$ and velocity vector $\mathbf{v}$ is expressed as:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{v}) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** All four fundamental transport equations (Continuity, Momentum, Energy, Species Mass) of a viscous, Newtonian fluid are special cases of this single master equation. A student who masters the $\phi$-framework can reproduce every governing equation in CFD and reactor analysis simply by substituting $\phi = 1, v_i, T, C_A$.

### Symbol Glossary

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\phi$ | Generic transported scalar property | — |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | kg/m³ |
| $\mathbf{v}$ | Velocity vector field | m/s |
| $\Gamma_\phi$ | Diffusivity (diffusion coefficient) for property $\phi$ | varies |
| $S_\phi$ | Volumetric source/sink of $\phi$ | unit-dependent |
| $t$ | Time | s |

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The River Bank Ledger"**
> Imagine a river flowing past a town. The river carries with it: **water** (mass), **boats** (momentum), **warmth** (heat), and **pollution** (species). At any stretch of the river, four things happen to each of these "cargoes":
> 1. **Storage** — how much is piled up in that stretch right now (the $\partial/\partial t$ term).
> 2. **Conveyor belt in/out** — how much the flowing river brings in minus what it carries away (the $\nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{v})$ term).
> 3. **Spreading** — turbulent or molecular spreading from high to low concentration (the $\nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi)$ term).
> 4. **Factories & drains** — local sources (factories dumping heat) and sinks (a drain removing pollutant), the $S_\phi$ term.
>
> The transport equation simply states: **What flows in + What is produced = What flows out + What is stored + What is consumed**. It is a mathematical "bank ledger" for every physical quantity.

## The Four Classical Transport Equations (Preview)

By choosing $\phi$ appropriately, the master equation collapses into:

- $\phi = 1$ → **Continuity equation** (mass conservation, $S_\phi = 0$).
- $\phi = v_x, v_y, v_z$ → **Navier-Stokes equations** (momentum conservation).
- $\phi = T$ → **Energy equation** (heat transport with viscous dissipation).
- $\phi = C_A$ → **Species continuity** (concentration of chemical species).

The constants **g = 9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration) and **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant) frequently appear in the source terms of these equations when buoyancy or compressibility is included.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Physical Meaning of Each Term

The generalized equation:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{v}) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

decomposes the transport of any property into **four fundamental mechanisms**:

1. **Unsteady (Transient) Term** $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t}$:
   - Represents *accumulation* (or depletion) of $\phi$ inside the control volume per unit time.
   - If this term is zero, the process is **steady-state** — KTU exam favourite.

2. **Convective Term** $\nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{v})$:
   - Represents transport of $\phi$ due to bulk fluid motion.
   - This is the reason a river carries boats downstream even without wind.

3. **Diffusive (Transport) Term** $\nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi)$:
   - Represents transport of $\phi$ due to a *gradient* (Fick's law, Fourier's law, Newton's law of viscosity).
   - Always acts from high to low concentration — it's the universe's tendency to erase gradients.

4. **Source / Sink Term** $S_\phi$:
   - Represents volumetric generation (e.g., chemical reaction, viscous heating) or destruction (e.g., radioactive decay).
   - Can be a constant, a function of $\phi$, or a function of position.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for Module 1 numericals. Memorize every entry — KTU board questions frequently test whether students can correctly identify $\Gamma_\phi$ and $S_\phi$ for a given $\phi$.

| Property Transported ($\phi$) | $\rho \phi$ (Capacity) | $\Gamma_\phi$ (Diffusivity) | $S_\phi$ (Source) | Equation Name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $\rho$ | **0** | **0** | Continuity equation |
| $v_x$ | $\rho v_x$ | $\mu$ | $-\partial p/\partial x + \rho g_x + F_x$ | x-momentum (N–S) |
| $v_y$ | $\rho v_y$ | $\mu$ | $-\partial p/\partial y + \rho g_y + F_y$ | y-momentum (N–S) |
| $v_z$ | $\rho v_z$ | $\mu$ | $-\partial p/\partial z + \rho g_z + F_z$ | z-momentum (N–S) |
| $T$ | $\rho C_p T$ | $k$ (thermal conductivity) | viscous dissipation + reaction | Energy equation |
| $C_A$ | $\rho C_A$ | $\rho D_{AB}$ (mass diffusivity) | $r_A$ (reaction rate) | Species continuity |

**Boundary Condition Library (essential for KTU 14-mark problems):**

| Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet (1st kind)** | $\phi = \phi_0$ at wall | Value is prescribed | No-slip wall: $v = 0$ |
| **Neumann (2nd kind)** | $\partial \phi / \partial n = q_0$ | Flux is prescribed | Insulated wall: $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ |
| **Robin / Cauchy (3rd kind)** | $-k \partial \phi/\partial n = h(\phi - \phi_\infty)$ | Convection at boundary | Heat loss to ambient |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CFD Software** (ANSYS Fluent, COMSOL, OpenFOAM): the entire solver kernel solves the discretized version of this generalized equation for $\phi = 1, v_i, T, C_A$ on a mesh.
- **Chemical Reactor Design**: PFR → plug-flow form (1-D, no $\nabla$ in transverse directions); CSTR → well-mixed limit (drop spatial gradients).
- **Heat Exchanger Modelling**: energy equation with convective + conductive terms.
- **Environmental Engineering**: species equation tracks pollutant dispersion in rivers and atmosphere.
- **Biomedical Devices**: drug delivery through tissue uses the species equation with source term $S_\phi$ representing absorption.

## Classification of Transport PDEs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Classify the given PDE."* The classification is decided by the dominant physical mechanism.

| Type | Temporal Term | Spatial Order | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parabolic** | Present | 2nd order spatial | Unsteady heat conduction, transient diffusion |
| **Elliptic** | Absent ($\partial/\partial t = 0$) | 2nd order, mixed derivatives | Steady-state conduction, Laplace/Poisson equation |
| **Hyperbolic** | Absent | 2nd order, dominant convective | Wave-like flows, inviscid flows |

When the **Peclet number** $\mathrm{Pe} = \rho v L / \Gamma_\phi > 1$, convection dominates → hyperbolic tendency. When $\mathrm{Pe} < 1$, diffusion dominates → parabolic/elliptic. This dictates the choice of numerical scheme (upwind vs. central differencing).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — The Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

### Setup

Consider an **infinitesimal fluid element** (control volume) of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ fixed in space (Eulerian frame). Let $\rho(x, y, z, t)$ be the fluid density.

### Step 1 — Mass Flux In (x-direction)

Mass entering the face at $x$ (per unit area, per unit time) is $\rho v_x$ evaluated at $x$:

$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{in}} = (\rho v_x)\big|_{x} \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 2 — Mass Flux Out (x-direction)

Mass leaving the face at $x + dx$ is $\rho v_x$ evaluated at $x + dx$. Using a **Taylor expansion**:

$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} = \left[(\rho v_x) + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} dx\right]_{x} dy \, dz$$

### Step 3 — Net Mass Flow in x-direction

$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{net}} = \dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{x,\text{in}} = \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 4 — Repeat for y and z Directions

By identical reasoning:

$$\dot{m}_{y,\text{net}} = \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} dx \, dy \, dz$$

$$\dot{m}_{z,\text{net}} = \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 5 — Total Net Outflow

$$\dot{m}_{\text{out,total}} = \left[\frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}\right] dx \, dy \, dz$$

This is precisely $\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) \, dV$.

### Step 6 — Rate of Mass Accumulation

Mass inside the element at time $t$ is $\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$. The rate of accumulation is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz) = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 7 — Apply Conservation (In − Out = Accumulation)

Since there is no source/sink of mass in a non-nuclear flow:

$$-\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) \, dV = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dV$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector expansion form**: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$. For an **incompressible flow** ($\rho = $ const.), this reduces to $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$.

---

## Derivation 2 — Equation of Motion (Navier–Stokes, x-component)

### Setup

Apply **Newton's second law** ($\Sigma F = m a$) to the same Eulerian fluid element of mass $\rho \, dV$.

$$\sum F_x = \rho \, a_x \, dV = \rho \frac{D v_x}{D t} dV$$

where $\dfrac{D}{Dt}$ is the **material derivative**, accounting for both local change and convective transport.

### Step 1 — Identify the Four Forces

**(a) Pressure force on the element:** Net pressure thrust in x-direction:

$$F_{p,x} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} dV$$

(negative because pressure pushes from high to low).

**(b) Viscous (shear) force:** For a Newtonian fluid, the net viscous force on the element is:

$$F_{\mu,x} = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right)\right] dV = \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla v_x) \, dV$$

**(c) Body force (gravity):**

$$F_{g,x} = \rho g_x \, dV$$

**(d) External force per unit volume $F_x$** (e.g., electromagnetic): added directly.

### Step 2 — Substitute into Newton's Law

$$\rho \frac{D v_x}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla v_x) + \rho g_x + F_x$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Material Derivative

$$\frac{D v_x}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla) v_x$$

### Step 4 — Write in Vector Form

$$\rho \left[\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{v}\right] = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla \mathbf{v}) + \rho \mathbf{g} + \mathbf{F}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For constant $\mu$, the term $\nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla \mathbf{v})$ becomes $\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v}$, the classical Navier–Stokes equation. This is the most-tested 14-mark derivation in Module 1.

---

## Derivation 3 — Energy Equation (for a Newtonian Fluid with Viscous Dissipation)

### Step 1 — First Law of Thermodynamics

Rate of change of internal + kinetic energy inside the element equals net heat added + net work done:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left[\rho \left(e + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2\right)\right] + \nabla \cdot \left[\rho \left(h + \tfrac{1}{2}v^2\right)\mathbf{v}\right] = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \dot{q} + \Phi$$

where $e$ = internal energy, $h = e + p/\rho$ = enthalpy, and $\Phi$ = **viscous dissipation function** (mechanical energy degraded to heat).

### Step 2 — Viscous Dissipation Function

For incompressible flow with constant $\mu$:

$$\Phi = \mu \left[2\left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\right)^2 + 2\left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y}\right)^2 + 2\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right)^2\right]$$

### Step 3 — Final Form (for constant $\rho$, $C_p$, $k$)

$$\boxed{\rho C_p \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla T\right) = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi + \dot{q}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this form, $T$ plays the role of $\phi$, $\Gamma_\phi = k$, $S_\phi = \Phi + \dot{q}$. The viscous dissipation $\Phi$ is often negligible for gases at low velocity but crucial for high-speed flows and polymer processing.

---

## Derivation 4 — Species Mass Transport Equation

### Step 1 — Mass Balance for Species A

For chemical species $A$ with concentration $C_A$ (kg/m³) and reaction rate $r_A$ (kg/m³·s):

$$\frac{\partial (\rho C_A)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho C_A \mathbf{v}) = \nabla \cdot (\rho D_{AB} \nabla C_A) + r_A$$

### Step 2 — Identify Parameters in Master Form

Setting $\phi = C_A$:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \phi \mathbf{v}) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

yields the identifications: $\Gamma_\phi = \rho D_{AB}$ and $S_\phi = r_A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note carefully that the **diffusion coefficient** in species transport is $\rho D_{AB}$ (not just $D_{AB}$) because the convective term already carries $\rho$. This subtlety is a common KTU board-exam trap.

### Step 3 — Incompressible Constant-$\rho$ Form

Dividing through by $\rho$:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla C_A = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A + \frac{r_A}{\rho}$$

This is **Fick's second law of diffusion** with convection and reaction — the workhorse equation of reactor design and pollutant dispersion.

---

## Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Continuity | Momentum (N–S) | Energy | Species |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\phi$ | 1 | $v_i$ | $T$ | $C_A$ |
| $\Gamma_\phi$ | 0 | $\mu$ | $k$ | $\rho D_{AB}$ |
| Order of PDE | 1st | 2nd | 2nd | 2nd |
| Non-linearity | Linear | Quasi-linear (in $\mathbf{v}$) | Linear in $T$ | Linear in $C_A$ |
| Source cause | None | Pressure, gravity | Reaction, dissipation | Reaction |
| Analytical solutions exist for | Many | Few (Couette, Poiseuille) | Many (1-D rod, fin) | Many (1-D slab, sphere) |
