We will derive Eq. (2) of the cheat sheet from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the reacting liquid control volume.

### Step 0 — Starting Equation
The general unsteady-state energy balance for an open system is:

$$\frac{dU_{\text{sys}}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s + \sum \dot{n}_i^{\text{in}} h_i - \sum \dot{n}_i^{\text{out}} h_i$$

where $\dot{Q}$ is the heat added to the system, $\dot{W}_s$ is the shaft work (here $= 0$), and the last two terms are enthalpy flow in and out.

### Step 1 — Simplify for the Reactor

- **Shaft work** $\dot{W}_s = 0$ (no agitator work counted in internal energy balance, or treated separately).
- **Heat input** has two parts:
  - Heat from reaction: $\dot{Q}_{\text{rxn}} = (-\Delta H_R)\,V\,k C_A$ — positive if exothermic.
  - Heat removed by jacket: $\dot{Q}_{\text{jacket}} = U A_j (T_j - T)$ — *into* the system, so we write it as $+ U A_j (T_j - T)$.
- **Inlet enthalpy flow:** $F \rho C_p T_0$ (per unit volume basis using $F$ in m³/s).
- **Outlet enthalpy flow:** $F \rho C_p T$.
- **System internal energy:** $U_{\text{sys}} = V \rho C_p T$ (for incompressible liquid).

So the balance becomes:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(V \rho C_p T) = F \rho C_p T_0 - F \rho C_p T + (-\Delta H_R)\,V\,k C_A + U A_j (T_j - T)$$

### Step 2 — Expand the LHS
Since $V$, $\rho$, $C_p$ are constant in time:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_R)\,V\,k C_A + U A_j (T_j - T)$$

### Step 3 — Divide Through by $V \rho C_p$

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(T_0 - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k\,C_A}{\rho C_p} + \frac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T_j - T)$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange the Last Term

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(T_0 - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k\,C_A}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T - T_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **exactly** the master temperature ODE. The minus sign on the heat-removal term is now in its KTU-board form. Always double-check this sign in the exam — it is the **#1 most common sign-error trap** in energy balance derivations.

### Step 5 — Limiting Case Check (Sanity Test)

If the reactor is **adiabatic and batch** ($F=0$, $U=0$):
$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k\,C_A}{\rho C_p}$$

Combined with the batch mole balance $\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k C_A$, we can eliminate time by dividing:

$$\frac{dT}{dC_A} = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)}{\rho C_p}$$

Integrating with initial conditions $C_A = C_{A0}$, $T = T_0$:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)}{\rho C_p}\,(C_{A0} - C_A) = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}\,X$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slope of $T$ vs. $C_A$ is **constant** in the adiabatic batch case. This is the well-known **adiabatic temperature–concentration line** used in process safety diagrams. The maximum possible temperature rise is the **adiabatic temperature rise**:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}$$

---

## 3.2 The Coupled System — Matrix Form for Numerical Solution

KTU 2024 scheme questions frequently ask for the *coupled system* in vector/matrix form suitable for **Runge–Kutta 4th-order** integration. Here it is in standard ODE form $\dfrac{d\mathbf{y}}{dt} = \mathbf{f}(t, \mathbf{y})$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} C_A \\ T \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{F}{V}(C_{A0} - C_A) - k(T)\,C_A \\[2mm] \dfrac{F}{V}(T_0 - T) + \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k(T)\,C_A}{\rho C_p} - \dfrac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T - T_j) \end{bmatrix}$$

with initial conditions:

$$\begin{bmatrix} C_A(0) \\ T(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{A0} \\ T_0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** This vector form is what you should hand-write at the start of any Part-B numerical question, before doing the RK-4 iterations. Examiners look for the *formulation step* specifically — it carries 2 to 3 marks even before any computation.

---

## 3.3 Steady-State Solution (Useful for Exam Shortcuts)

At steady state, $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = 0$ and $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = 0$. The two coupled algebraic equations become:

$$0 = \frac{F}{V}(C_{A0} - C_A) - k(T)\,C_A \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_A = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + k(T)\,\tau}$$

$$0 = \frac{F}{V}(T_0 - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k(T)\,C_A}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T - T_j)$$

Substituting the first into the second yields a **single nonlinear algebraic equation in $T$** that can have 1, 2, or 3 solutions depending on $\beta$ and $\gamma$ (the Arrhenius number). This is the famous **multiple-steady-state** phenomenon.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often tests the **operating-point stability** by asking: *"At which steady state would you operate the reactor? Justify with a sketch of $Q_{\text{gen}}$ vs. $Q_{\text{rem}}$."* — Always choose the *lower* stable steady state (safer) for exothermic reactions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Outline (No Code — Hand-calc Style)

**Problem (sample):** $C_{A0} = 0.5$ mol/L, $T_0 = 350$ K, $T_j = 380$ K, $F/V = 0.1$ s$^{-1}$, $A = 10^{10}$ s$^{-1}$, $E/R = 10000$ K, $-\Delta H_R = 50000$ J/mol, $\rho C_p = 4 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K), $U A_j/(V) = 2 \times 10^5$ J/(m³·K·s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Approach (no code, by hand):** Use **Euler's method** or **RK-4** with a step size $\Delta t = 0.5$ s, iterating from $t=0$ to a target time $t = 5$ s. Tabulate $C_A$, $T$, $k$ at each step.

**Step 1 (at $t=0$):** $k(350) = 10^{10} e^{-10000/350} = 10^{10} \cdot e^{-28.57}$. **Hand-calc tip:** $e^{-28.57} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-13}$, so $k \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$.

**Step 2:** $C_A(0.5) \approx 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot [0.1 \cdot 0 + 0.0034 \cdot 0.5] \cdot 0.5 \approx 0.4996$ mol/L.

**Step 3:** $T(0.5) \approx 350 + 0.5 \cdot [0.1(350-350) + \frac{50000 \cdot 0.0034 \cdot 0.5}{4 \times 10^6} - \frac{2 \times 10^5}{4 \times 10^6}(350 - 380)]$
$= 350 + 0.5 \cdot [0 + 0.02125 - 0.05 \cdot (-30)]$
$= 350 + 0.5 \cdot [0.02125 + 1.5] = 350 + 0.761 = 350.76$ K.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **dominant** term here is the jacket heat-removal: $-1.5$ K/s initially. As $T$ rises, $k$ explodes, and the reaction term $\frac{(-\Delta H_R) k C_A}{\rho C_p}$ will start to dominate, causing $T$ to *accelerate upward*. This is the start of a runaway if unchecked. The numerical pattern observed will be: $T$ slowly rises, then rapidly accelerates — the *S-shaped* $T(t)$ curve.

**Step 4 (next step):** Use $T = 350.76$ K to get $k(350.76) = 10^{10} e^{-10000/350.76} = 10^{10} e^{-28.510}$. The change in the exponent from $-28.57$ to $-28.51$ multiplies $k$ by $e^{0.06} \approx 1.062$ — a 6% increase from just 0.76 K of temperature rise. This demonstrates the **Arrhenius sensitivity** quantitatively.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Coupled Model

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **information flow** between the three pillars (Mole, Energy, Arrhenius) and the jacket boundary. This is the schematic you should reproduce in your KTU answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedStream["Feed Stream F, CA0, T0"]
    ReactorCV["Reactor Control Volume V, rho, Cp"]
    JacketWall["Jacket Wall UA"]
    SteamRes["Steam Reservoir constant Tj = Tsat"]
    MoleOut["CA of solution ODE"]
    EnergyOut["T of solution ODE"]
    RateOut["k of solution Arrhenius"]
    RK4["RK4 Integrator dt = 0.5 s"]
    PlotOut["CA vs t and T vs t"]

    FeedStream --> ReactorCV
    SteamRes -->|constant Tj| JacketWall
    JacketWall -->|heat removal UAj T minus Tj| ReactorCV
    ReactorCV --> MoleOut
    ReactorCV --> EnergyOut
    MoleOut --> RateOut
    EnergyOut --> RateOut
    RateOut --> MoleOut
    RateOut --> EnergyOut
    MoleOut --> RK4
    EnergyOut --> RK4
    RK4 --> PlotOut
```

---

## 4.2 Heat-Flow Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reaction Exothermic Qrxn = minus delta H V k CA"]
    B["Jacket Removal Qrem = UAj T minus Tj"]
    C["Inlet Stream Enthalpy F rho Cp T0"]
    D["Outlet Stream Enthalpy F rho Cp T"]
    E["Accumulation V rho Cp dT by dt"]
    A --> E
    B --> E
    C --> E
    D --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** All four arrows point **into** the accumulation term. If the net sum of (Reaction + Jacket + Inlet − Outlet) is positive, $T$ rises; if negative, $T$ falls. The jacket term switches sign exactly when $T$ crosses $T_j$ — this is what allows the reactor to find a self-regulating steady state.

---

## 4.3 Steady-State Operating-Point Stability Diagram (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Qgen["Qgen versus T exponential curve from Arrhenius"]
    Qrem["Qrem versus T straight line slope UAj intercept UAj Tj"]
    Intersection["Intersection Points 1 to 3"]
    Stable1["Lower Steady State Stable"]
    Unstable["Middle Steady State Unstable"]
    Stable2["Upper Steady State Stable but hazardous"]
    Runaway["Runaway if line tangent to exponential"]

    Qgen --> Intersection
    Qrem --> Intersection
    Intersection --> Stable1
    Intersection --> Unstable
    Intersection --> Stable2
    Intersection --> Runaway
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** This "S-curve" sketch is worth 3–4 marks by itself in any question that asks *"Discuss reactor stability"*. Always label the three regions: ignition region (lower branch unstable), runaway region (upper branch hazardous), and the safe operating window.

---

## 4.4 Process Flow Schematic (Physical Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Steam Supply line at Pjacket"]
    S2["Condensate Drain line"]
    R1["Reactor body with impeller"]
    J1["Jacket annulus surrounding wall"]
    IN["Feed inlet with CA0 and T0"]
    OUT["Product outlet with CA and T"]
    TC["Temperature sensor inside reactor"]
    PI["Pressure indicator on jacket"]

    S1 --> J1
    J1 --> R1
    J1 --> S2
    IN --> R1
    R1 --> OUT
    TC --> R1
    PI --> J1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** In questions on *control and instrumentation* of jacketed reactors (a popular Module-2 / Module-3 bridge topic), you must show the **temperature sensor inside the reacting liquid** and the **pressure indicator on the jacket** — these are the two control loops. Do not place the temperature sensor in the jacket — that is a common student error.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a "non-isothermal reactor". Why is a steam-jacketed reactor treated as having a constant jacket temperature in process modelling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **non-isothermal reactor** is one in which the reaction temperature varies with time (and/or position) due to the heat released or absorbed by the chemical reaction; consequently, the energy balance must be solved **simultaneously** with the mole balance to predict the system behaviour. (1.5 marks)

In a **steam-jacketed** reactor, the heat-transfer fluid in the jacket is **condensing saturated steam**. Because condensation occurs at a *fixed* saturation temperature $T_{\text{sat}}$ corresponding to the jacket operating pressure (assumed constant), the jacket-side temperature $T_j$ remains constant as long as liquid condensate is being drained and fresh steam supplied. Hence, $T_j = T_{\text{sat}}(P_j) = $ **constant**, and a separate energy balance on the jacket side is unnecessary — the jacket acts as a *thermal reservoir*. (1.5 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is the "adiabatic temperature rise" $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ for an exothermic reaction? Write its expression and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **adiabatic temperature rise** is the maximum possible increase in the temperature of the reacting mixture if *all* the heat released by the reaction is retained (i.e., no heat is lost to the jacket and the feed is at $T_0$). (1 mark)

Its expression is:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,C_{A0}}{\rho\,C_p}$$

with units of **kelvin (K)**. (1 mark)

**Significance:** It represents the upper bound of temperature that can be reached at full conversion under adiabatic conditions. If $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ is large (e.g., > 50 K), the system is prone to *thermal runaway* and the operating conditions must be chosen carefully. The **Damköhler number** $\beta = \Delta T_{\text{ad}} / (T_j - T_0)$ compares this rise to the available cooling, and runaway is likely when $\beta \gtrsim 1$. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part-B Module 2 questions carry **14 marks** and offer an internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below. Sub-parts are (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]

**(a)** Develop the complete unsteady-state mathematical model of a **non-isothermal steam-jacketed CSTR** in which the liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ (irreversible, first-order, exothermic) takes place. Clearly state the **assumptions** and write down the three governing equations (mole balance, energy balance, rate law) along with initial conditions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the parameter values given below, determine whether the reactor is operating in a **safe region** or approaching **runaway**. Justify by sketching (qualitatively) the $Q_{\text{gen}}$ vs. $Q_{\text{rem}}$ curves. *(7 marks)*

- $C_{A0} = 0.8$ mol/L, $T_0 = 340$ K, $T_j = 380$ K
- $V = 1$ m³, $F = 0.05$ m³/s
- $A = 7 \times 10^{9}$ s$^{-1}$, $E/R = 9500$ K
- $-\Delta H_R = 60000$ J/mol
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K)
- $U = 800$ W/(m²·K), $A_j = 6$ m²

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Model Development (7 marks)**

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
1. Perfect mixing (well-mixed CSTR — uniform $C_A$ and $T$ inside).
2. Constant volume $V$, constant density $\rho$, constant $C_p$ (incompressible liquid).
3. Single liquid-phase reaction: $A \to B$, irreversible, first-order in $C_A$.
4. Jacket steam at constant saturation temperature $T_j$ (condensing steam assumption).
5. No shaft work; ideal-gas / ideal-liquid behaviour not required.
6. Inlet flow = outlet flow (steady volumetric flow, $F$ = constant).

**Three governing equations** (5 marks):

**1. Mole balance on A:**

$$V\,\frac{dC_A}{dt} = F(C_{A0} - C_A) - V\,k\,C_A$$

**2. Energy balance on reacting liquid:**

$$V\,\rho\,C_p\,\frac{dT}{dt} = F\,\rho\,C_p\,(T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_R)\,V\,k\,C_A - U A_j (T - T_j)$$

**3. Rate law (Arrhenius):**

$$k(T) = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

**Initial conditions:**

$$C_A(0) = C_{A0} = 0.8 \text{ mol/L}, \quad T(0) = T_0 = 340 \text{ K}$$

Dividing the first two equations by $V$ to obtain the standard coupled ODE system:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(C_{A0} - C_A) - k\,C_A$$

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(T_0 - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k\,C_A}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T - T_j)$$

> **Mark distribution (a):** Assumptions — 1 mark; mole balance — 1.5 marks; energy balance — 2 marks; rate law — 1 mark; initial conditions — 0.5 mark; vector form — 1 mark.

---

**(b) — Safety / Runaway Analysis (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the adiabatic temperature rise** (1 mark):

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,C_{A0}}{\rho C_p} = \frac{60000 \times 0.8}{1000 \times 4180} = \frac{48000}{4.18 \times 10^6} \approx 0.0115 \text{ K}$$

Wait — this is for a CSTR in mol/m³ units; convert: $C_{A0} = 0.8$ mol/L = 800 mol/m³. So:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{60000 \times 800}{1000 \times 4180} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^7}{4.18 \times 10^6} \approx 11.48 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Damköhler number $\beta$** (1 mark):

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{ad}}}{T_j - T_0} = \frac{11.48}{380 - 340} = \frac{11.48}{40} \approx 0.287$$

Since $\beta < 1$, the available cooling capacity *exceeds* the adiabatic temperature rise — the reactor is **likely operating in the safe region**, with a unique stable steady state.

**Step 3 — Sketch the curves qualitatively** (3 marks):

- **$Q_{\text{rem}}(T)$:** straight line, $Q_{\text{rem}} = U A_j (T - T_j) = 4800 (T - 380)$, slope = **4800 W/K**, $x$-intercept at $T = 380$ K.
- **$Q_{\text{gen}}(T)$:** S-shaped (exponential-then-saturating) curve, passing through origin at $T = 0$, rising sharply as $T$ increases.

The two curves intersect at **one point** (since $\beta < 1$), which is the unique operating steady state. (2 marks for the qualitative discussion and conclusion.)

**Conclusion:** The reactor is **not** in runaway danger under the given operating conditions; there is only one stable steady state, and the margin is comfortable ($\beta = 0.29$). However, if the *feed concentration* were increased to $C_{A0} > 2.8$ mol/L, $\beta$ would exceed 1, and multiple steady states (and runaway risk) would emerge.

> **Mark distribution (b):** $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ calculation — 1 mark; $\beta$ calculation — 1 mark; qualitative sketch description — 3 marks; final conclusion and engineering recommendation — 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **multiple steady states** in a non-isothermal CSTR with condensing-steam jacket. Show mathematically how **three** steady states can arise for a sufficiently exothermic reaction. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the exothermic liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ in a steam-jacketed CSTR, derive the expressions for **conversion** and **temperature** at steady state, in terms of the **Damköhler number** $\beta$ and the **Arrhenius number** $\gamma = E/RT_j$. Discuss the physical meaning of $\beta$ and $\gamma$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Multiple Steady States (7 marks)**

At steady state, the **rate of heat generation** by reaction must equal the **rate of heat removal** through the jacket plus the convective enthalpy balance:

$$Q_{\text{gen}}(T) = Q_{\text{rem}}(T) + Q_{\text{conv}}(T)$$

- $Q_{\text{gen}}(T) = (-\Delta H_R)\,V\,k(T)\,C_A(T)$ — **exponential in $T$** because $k \propto e^{-E/RT}$.
- $Q_{\text{rem}}(T) = U A_j (T - T_j)$ — **linear in $T$** with slope $U A_j$.

The sum of these two functions plotted against $T$ is an **S-shaped curve** intersecting the line $Q = 0$ at **up to three points** (if the curvature of $Q_{\text{gen}}$ is steep enough). These three intersections correspond to the three steady states:

- **Lower steady state** ($T_{\text{ss},1}$): low conversion, low $T$ → STABLE (perturbation decays).
- **Middle steady state** ($T_{\text{ss},2}$): intermediate $T$ → UNSTABLE (perturbation grows).
- **Upper steady state** ($T_{\text{ss},3}$): high $T$, near full conversion → STABLE but unsafe.

**Mathematical origin (3 marks):** The single algebraic equation for $T$ is:

$$\frac{F}{V}(T - T_0) + \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k(T)\,C_A(T)}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T - T_j) = 0$$

This is a *transcendental* equation in $T$ (because of $e^{-E/RT}$). For a strongly exothermic reaction (large $-\Delta H_R$) and a small activation energy denominator (high $T$), the function $f(T) = $ LHS is **non-monotonic** — it has a local maximum and a local minimum — leading to up to three roots by Rolle's theorem.

**Physical condition for multiplicity** (1 mark): the slope of $Q_{\text{gen}}$ at the inflection point must exceed the slope of $Q_{\text{rem}}$:

$$\left.\frac{dQ_{\text{gen}}}{dT}\right|_{T^*} > \frac{dQ_{\text{rem}}}{dT} = U A_j$$

This translates to $\beta > $ some critical value (typically $\beta_c \approx 0.2$–$0.3$ for typical kinetics).

> **Mark distribution (a):** Description of three steady states — 2 marks; physical sketch description — 2 marks; mathematical origin (transcendental equation, non-monotonicity) — 2 marks; critical condition for multiplicity — 1 mark.

---

**(b) — Derivation of Steady-State $X$ and $T$ in terms of $\beta$ and $\gamma$ (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define dimensionless groups** (1 mark):

- $\beta = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R)\,C_{A0}}{\rho C_p (T_j - T_0)}$ — heat-of-reaction parameter.
- $\gamma = \dfrac{E}{R T_j}$ — dimensionless activation energy.
- $\tau = V/F$ — space time.

**Step 2 — Steady-state mole balance** (2 marks):

$$0 = \frac{C_{A0} - C_A}{\tau} - k C_A \quad\Rightarrow\quad X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state energy balance** (1 mark):

$$0 = \frac{T_0 - T}{\tau} + \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k C_A}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T - T_j)$$

Substitute $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$ and rearrange:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,C_{A0}\,X}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A_j \tau}{\rho C_p V}(T - T_j)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce $\beta$ and simplify** (2 marks):

$$T - T_0 = \beta (T_j - T_0) X - \left(\frac{U A_j \tau}{V \rho C_p}\right)(T - T_j)$$

Define $\kappa = \dfrac{U A_j \tau}{V \rho C_p}$ (dimensionless heat-transfer parameter). Then:

$$\frac{T - T_0}{T_j - T_0} = \beta X - \kappa \left(\frac{T - T_j}{T_j - T_0}\right)$$

Define $\theta = (T - T_0)/(T_j - T_0)$ and $\theta_j = (T_j - T_0)/(T_j - T_0) = 1$. Then:

$$\theta = \beta X - \kappa (\theta - 1) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta(1 + \kappa) = \beta X + \kappa \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta = \frac{\beta X + \kappa}{1 + \kappa}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce $\gamma$ via the Arrhenius rate** (1 mark):

$$k(T) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{\gamma T_j}{T}\right)$$

So the rate constant at the operating temperature $T$ is a function of $\theta$ through $T = T_0 + \theta(T_j - T_0)$. Substituting this into the mole balance yields a single transcendental equation in $X$ (or $\theta$) parameterized by $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\tau A$.

> **Mark distribution (b):** Definition of dimensionless groups — 1 mark; $X$ in terms of $k\tau$ — 2 marks; $T$ equation in terms of $\beta$ — 2 marks; $\gamma$ introduction and final transcendental equation — 1 mark; physical meaning of $\beta$ and $\gamma$ — 1 mark.

**Physical meaning of $\beta$ and $\gamma$** (part of the 1 mark above):

- **$\beta$ (Damköhler-heat number):** measures the ratio of the *maximum possible temperature rise* (adiabatic, full conversion) to the *available temperature driving force* $(T_j - T_0)$. Large $\beta$ means the reaction wants to heat the mixture a lot compared to the cooling available → **runaway-prone**.
- **$\gamma$ (Arrhenius number):** measures the *temperature sensitivity* of the rate constant. Large $\gamma$ means the rate is very sensitive to $T$ (steep exponential) → highly **non-linear** behaviour and likely **multiplicity**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 mark-losing mistakes on this topic** (compiled from KTU 2019–2024 answer scripts):
>
> 1. **Sign error on the heat-removal term.** It MUST be $-UA_j(T-T_j)$ in the energy balance (heat is *removed* when $T > T_j$). Writing $+UA_j(T-T_j)$ is the #1 killer. **Penalty: 2 marks** every time it appears.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by $V \rho C_p$.** Students leave the LHS as $\frac{dU}{dt}$ and then can't solve. **Penalty: 1 mark** if you recover later, **2 marks** if you don't.
> 3. **Treating the jacket as a variable-temperature system.** The condensing-steam assumption is a *given* in this problem. Writing a separate jacket energy balance wastes time and is wrong. **Penalty: 3 marks** for full misread of problem.
> 4. **Using $T_0$ instead of $T_j$** in the heat-removal term. The jacket is at $T_j$, not the inlet feed. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Ignoring units of $C_{A0}$.** The value 0.5 mol/L is **NOT** 0.5 mol/m³. Convert correctly or use 500 mol/m³ in $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$. **Penalty: 1–2 marks** depending on context.
>
> **Bonus tip:** Always state the **three assumptions** (well-mixed, constant $V$/$\rho$/$C_p$, condensing steam) explicitly at the start. This single paragraph is worth **1–2 marks** and signals to the examiner that you know what you are doing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Steam-Jacketed Non-Isothermal Reactor:**

- [x] The model has **two coupled ODEs** (mole + energy) plus the **Arrhenius rate law** — three pillars: **MR–ER–AR**.
- [x] The **jacket temperature $T_j$ is constant** because of condensing-saturated-steam assumption — this is the *defining* modelling simplification.
- [x] The **master temperature equation** is:
$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(T_0 - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_R)\,k C_A}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A_j}{V \rho C_p}(T - T_j)$.
- [x] The **sign convention** is critical: jacket removal term carries a **minus** sign when written as $(T - T_j)$.
- [x] The **adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}$ is the upper bound of $T$ rise at full conversion.
- [x] The **Damköhler number** $\beta = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{ad}}}{T_j - T_0}$ predicts runaway: **$\beta > 1$ → multiple steady states and runaway risk**.
- [x] The **Arrhenius number** $\gamma = E/R T_j$ measures non-linearity: large $\gamma$ → multiple steady states more likely.
- [x] For **multiple steady states**, the $Q_{\text{gen}}$ curve is S-shaped and the straight-line $Q_{\text{rem}}$ can intersect it at **up to 3 points**.
- [x] The **lower and upper** steady states are **stable**; the **middle** one is **unstable** — operate at the **lower** (safer) one for exothermic reactions.
- [x] At **steady state**, the coupled ODEs become **two coupled algebraic equations**, with the elimination of $C_A$ yielding a *single transcendental equation in $T$*.
- [x] **Numerical solution** is by **RK-4** (preferred) or **Euler** (acceptable but less accurate) — step size $\Delta t \le 0.5$ s is typical.
- [x] **Vector form** of the ODE system must be written at the start of any numerical question — it is worth 2–3 marks on its own.
- [x] The **engineering applications** are: batch pharma reactors, polymerisation reactors, food processing kettles, and HAZOP/PSM safety analysis.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"A non-isothermal reactor is a mole balance, an energy balance, and an Arrhenius rate — tied together by the steam jacket's constant $T_j$. Master the three equations, and you master the question."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiling-Open and Enclosed Vessel
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boiling-Open and Enclosed Vessel

# Boiling – Open and Enclosed Vessel

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boiling** is a mode of heat transfer in which a liquid changes to vapor at the heated surface, accompanied by the formation, growth, and departure of vapor bubbles. When this phase change occurs at a heated surface submerged in a stagnant (or gently convecting) bulk liquid, it is termed **pool boiling**.

**Open Vessel Boiling:**
When the liquid is heated in a vessel that is exposed to the atmosphere, the system operates at approximately **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** pressure. The vapor generated escapes freely into the surroundings, and the liquid surface remains at atmospheric pressure.

**Enclosed (Closed) Vessel Boiling:**
When the liquid is heated in a sealed/pressurized vessel, the vapor accumulates above the liquid, creating a **two-phase liquid–vapor equilibrium system**. The operating pressure is dictated by the saturation temperature of the liquid, and the system may be under **saturated, subcooled, or pressurized conditions**.

> [!NOTE]
> In an **enclosed vessel**, the system is governed by the Clausius–Clapeyron relationship between saturation temperature and saturation pressure. Pressure is no longer a constant; it becomes a *function of temperature*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

- **Open Vessel:** Imagine boiling water in a kettle on a stove. Steam freely rises and escapes through the spout. The kettle lid is just for safety. Whatever happens inside, the pressure above the water is essentially **fixed at atmospheric pressure**.
- **Enclosed Vessel:** Think of a pressure cooker. As water boils, the steam has nowhere to escape (unless a weighted valve releases). Pressure builds up, raising the boiling point. Now the water boils at a **higher temperature than 100 °C**, and the pressure depends on how hot the system gets.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** (for radiation-dominated regimes): $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Standard surface tension of water at 100 °C:** $\sigma \approx 0.0589 \text{ N/m}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Boiling Modes

Boiling phenomena are classified based on two key parameters:
1. **Bulk liquid temperature relative to saturation temperature** ($T_{sat} - T_{bulk}$)
2. **Excess surface temperature** ($\Delta T_e = T_w - T_{sat}$)

| Boiling Mode | Condition | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Subcooled Boiling** | $T_{bulk} < T_{sat}$ | Bubbles form at surface but condense in bulk |
| **Saturated Boiling** | $T_{bulk} = T_{sat}$ | Bulk is at saturation; vigorous bubble formation |
| **Forced Convection Boiling** | External flow present | Combination of convection + bubble dynamics |
| **Pool Boiling** | No bulk flow | Static liquid; pure buoyancy-driven motion |

### 2.2 The Pool Boiling Curve (Nukiyama Curve)

The famous Nukiyama curve (1934) plots heat flux ($q''$) versus excess surface temperature ($\Delta T_e$):

- **Region A–B:** *Natural Convection* — No bubbles; heat transfer dominated by single-phase convection.
- **Region B–C:** *Nucleate Boiling (onset of nucleation)* — Isolated bubbles begin forming at preferred nucleation sites (cavities, scratches).
- **Region B–D:** *Partial Nucleate Boiling* — Bubble formation more frequent but still localized.
- **Region D–E:** *Fully Developed Nucleate Boiling* — Bubbles merge and form columns of vapor. **Most efficient region** for heat transfer.
- **Point E (CHF):** *Critical Heat Flux* — Peak heat flux; transition from nucleate to film boiling.
- **Region E–F:** *Transition Boiling (Partial Film Boiling)* — Unstable; vapor intermittently blankets the surface.
- **Point F:** *Leidenfrost Point / Minimum Heat Flux* — Stable vapor film established.
- **Region F–G–H:** *Film Boiling* — Heat transfer through a stable vapor blanket by conduction + radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** is a *design-limiting* parameter. Operating beyond CHF leads to catastrophic surface overheating (fuel rod burnout in nuclear reactors, heater burnout in process plants).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Correlation / Formula | Regime | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rohsenow (Nucleate Boiling) $q'' = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l}\,\Delta T_e}{C_{sf}\, h_{fg}\, Pr_l^n}\right]^3$ | Nucleate Boiling | $C_{sf}, n$ depend on surface–liquid pair |
| 2 | Critical Heat Flux (Zuber) $q''_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | CHF / Burnout | Independent of surface condition |
| 3 | Minimum Heat Flux (Berenson) $q''_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | Leidenfrost Point | Independent of surface condition |
| 4 | Film Boiling (Bromley) $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{k_v^3 \, \rho_v \, (\rho_l - \rho_v) \, g \, (h_{fg} + 0.4 c_{p,v}\,\Delta T_e)}{\mu_v \, L \, \Delta T_e}\right]^{1/4}$ | Stable Film Boiling | For horizontal cylinders/plates |
| 5 | Clausius–Clapeyron $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \, v_{fg}}$ | Vapour–Liquid Equilibria | Enclosed vessel saturation |
| 6 | Boiling Number $Bo = \dfrac{q''}{G \, h_{fg}}$ | Dimensionless | Compares boiling to sensible heating |
| 7 | Jakob Number $Ja = \dfrac{c_{p,l}\,\Delta T_e}{h_{fg}}$ | Dimensionless | Superheat-to-latent ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for the Boiling Curve:**
> - At $\Delta T_e = 0$: $q'' \to 0$ (no boiling occurs below saturation).
> - At CHF ($\Delta T_{e,CHF}$): $q''$ reaches maximum; derivative $\partial q''/\partial \Delta T_e = 0$.
> - At Leidenfrost point: $q''$ is *minimum* in film regime; film becomes stable.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Reactor Design:** CHF limits the maximum linear heat rate of fuel rods.
- **Petrochemical Reboilers:** Enclosed vessel boiling in distillation column reboilers determines tray efficiency.
- **Cryogenics:** Film boiling governs the cooldown of LNG storage tanks.
- **Electronics Cooling:** Subcooled boiling is exploited in high-heat-flux chip cooling.
- **Steam Generators:** Operating in saturated nucleate boiling maximizes heat transfer without burnout risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Rohsenow Correlation (Nucleate Boiling – Open Vessel)

Rohsenow (1952) developed an empirical correlation by modeling the bubble growth process using *bubble dynamics* under quasi-static equilibrium.

**Step 1: Bubble Departure Criterion**

A bubble departs the surface when buoyancy force exceeds surface tension holding force. Equilibrium at departure:

$$F_b = F_{\sigma}$$

**Step 2: Buoyancy Force**

$$F_b = \dfrac{\pi \, d_b^3}{6} \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)$$

**Step 3: Surface Tension Force (acting around circumference)**

$$F_{\sigma} = \pi \, d_b \, \sigma$$

**Step 4: Equating and solving for bubble departure diameter $d_b$**

$$\dfrac{\pi \, d_b^3}{6} \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v) = \pi \, d_b \, \sigma$$

$$d_b^2 = \dfrac{6 \, \sigma}{g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}$$

$$d_b = \left[\dfrac{6 \, \sigma}{g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2}$$

**Step 5: Energy Balance on Bubble**

The rate of heat transfer to the bubble equals the rate of latent heat absorbed during growth:

$$q'' \cdot A_s = \rho_v \, h_{fg} \, \dfrac{dV_b}{dt}$$

For a hemispherical bubble of radius $r$ growing at surface:

$$\dfrac{dV_b}{dt} = \pi \, r^2 \, \dfrac{dr}{dt}$$

**Step 6: Bubble Growth Rate (Rayleigh–Plesset type analysis)**

For a vapor bubble growing in a superheated liquid:

$$\dfrac{dr}{dt} = \dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_e \, \sqrt{k_l \, \rho_l \, c_{p,l}}}{\rho_v \, h_{fg} \, \sqrt{\pi \, t}}$$

This is the *asymptotic solution* for bubble growth in a thermal boundary layer.

**Step 7: Substituting back into energy balance**

After integration and dimensional analysis, Rohsenow proposed the form:

$$q'' = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, Pr_l^n}\right]^3$$

> **Final Rohsenow Correlation:**

$$\boxed{q'' = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, Pr_l^n}\right]^3}$$

**Where:**
- $C_{sf}$ = surface–fluid characteristic constant (e.g., water–copper mechanically polished: $C_{sf}=0.0130, n=1.0$)
- $n$ = empirical exponent, typically 1.0 for water and 1.7 for other liquids
- $\mu_l, c_{p,l}, Pr_l$ = liquid viscosity, specific heat, Prandtl number
- $\sigma$ = surface tension
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization

**Step 8: Solving for Heat Transfer Coefficient**

Since $q'' = h \cdot \Delta T_e$, an effective boiling heat transfer coefficient can be extracted from this correlation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Critical Heat Flux (Zuber, 1959)

Zuber used a **hydrodynamic instability** argument based on Taylor wavelength analysis.

**Step 1: Assume Vapor Column Model**

At CHF, vapor escapes as columns (jets) of diameter $D_c$ rising from the heated surface. The wavelength of instability for a cylindrical column of vapor rising in liquid is:

$$\lambda = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{3\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

**Step 2: Critical Vapor Velocity**

The vapor velocity required to sustain a Taylor-unstable column:

$$u_{v,crit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi \, g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{3 \, \rho_v^2}} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\rho_l^2}$$

For simplified form (Zuber's final result):

$$u_v = \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 3: Mass of Vapor Generated per Unit Area per Unit Time**

$$\dot{m}'' = \rho_v \cdot u_v = \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 4: Energy Required (Latent Heat Multiplied by Mass Flux)**

$$q''_{max} = h_{fg} \cdot \dot{m}'' = h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 5: Final Zuber Correlation with empirical coefficient (0.149)**

$$\boxed{q''_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}}$$

**Key Insight:** CHF depends only on **fluid properties**, not on the heater surface. This is why CHF is a *fundamental property* of the fluid–pressure system.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Berenson's Minimum Heat Flux

At the Leidenfrost point, a stable vapor film exists. Berenson modeled the vapor film as a stagnant layer and used an energy balance on it.

**Step 1: Vapor film conduction**

$$q''_{min} = \dfrac{k_v \, \Delta T_e}{\delta}$$

**Step 2: Film thickness from hydrodynamic stability (same Taylor wavelength analysis)**

$$\delta = \left[\dfrac{3 \mu_v \, \lambda}{2\pi \, \rho_v \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2}$$

After substitution and empirical fitting, Berenson obtained:

$$\boxed{q''_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2}\right]^{1/4}}$$

---

### 3.4 Open Vessel vs. Enclosed Vessel — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Open Vessel | Enclosed Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| **System Pressure** | Constant (= atmospheric) | Variable; governed by $T_{sat}$ |
| **Saturation Temperature** | Fixed (e.g., 100 °C for water) | Increases with pressure |
| **Vapor Escape** | Free to atmosphere | Confined; accumulates above liquid |
| **Heat Input Effect** | Drives boiling at constant $T_{sat}$ | Increases both $P$ and $T_{sat}$ |
| **Bubble Dynamics** | Bubbles freely rise and burst at surface | Bubbles may recondense if vapor is at lower $T$ |
| **Modeling Approach** | Steady-state heat flux vs. $\Delta T_e$ | Coupled mass + energy + momentum |
| **Application** | Open kettles, atmospheric reboilers | Pressure cookers, autoclaves, steam generators |
| **Phase Equilibrium** | None (vapor escapes) | Must satisfy $T_{system} = T_{sat}(P)$ |
| **Governing Equations** | Pool boiling correlations | Clausius–Clapeyron + Navier–Stokes + Energy |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A horizontal copper plate boils water at 1 atm. Properties:
- $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3$ J/kg
- $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³
- $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³
- $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Find:** Critical heat flux.

**Solution:**

$$q''_{max} = 0.149 \times h_{fg} \times \rho_v \times \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\dfrac{g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 0.0589 \times (957.9 - 0.5956)}{(0.5956)^2}$$

$$= \dfrac{9.81 \times 0.0589 \times 957.30}{0.3547} = \dfrac{553.0}{0.3547} \approx 1559.3$$

$$\left[1559.3\right]^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{1559.3}} = \sqrt{39.49} = 6.284$$

$$q''_{max} = 0.149 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 0.5956 \times 6.284$$

$$= 0.149 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 3.744 = 1.259 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q''_{max} \approx 1.259 \text{ MW/m}^2}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pool Boiling Curve – Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Heated Surface in Stagnant Liquid] --> B{Temperature Condition}
    B -->|T_w greater than T_sat| C[Nucleation Sites Active]
    B -->|T_w less than T_sat| Z1[No Boiling Region]
    C --> D[Region A-B: Natural Convection Only]
    D --> E[Region B-C: Onset of Nucleate Boiling]
    E --> F[Region B-D: Isolated Bubble Regime]
    F --> G[Region D-E: Fully Developed Nucleate Boiling]
    G --> H[Point E: Critical Heat Flux CHF Reached]
    H --> I[Region E-F: Transition Boiling Unstable]
    I --> J[Point F: Leidenfrost Point Minimum Heat Flux]
    J --> K[Region F-G: Unstable Film Boiling]
    K --> L[Region G-H: Stable Film Boiling]
    L --> M[Heat Transfer via Conduction plus Radiation in Vapor Film]

    subgraph Modeling Choices
        M1[Rohsenow: Nucleate Boiling]
        M2[Zuber: Critical Heat Flux]
        M3[Berenson: Minimum Heat Flux]
        M4[Bromley: Film Boiling]
    end

    G --> M1
    H --> M2
    J --> M3
    L --> M4
```

### 4.2 Open vs. Enclosed Vessel – Process Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Open Vessel System
        OV1[Heat Source] --> OV2[Liquid Pool at 1 atm]
        OV2 --> OV3[Vapor Bubble Formation]
        OV3 --> OV4[Bubble Rise and Burst]
        OV4 --> OV5[Free Escape to Atmosphere]
        OV5 --> OV6[Constant Pressure Boundary]
    end

    subgraph Enclosed Vessel System
        EV1[Heat Source] --> EV2[Liquid Pool in Sealed Vessel]
        EV2 --> EV3[Vapor Bubble Formation]
        EV3 --> EV4[Vapor Accumulation in Headspace]
        EV4 --> EV5[Pressure Increases]
        EV5 --> EV6[Saturation Temperature Increases via Clausius-Clapeyron]
        EV6 --> EV7[Equilibrium State Reached at New T_sat and P]
    end

    OV6 -. feedback .-> OV2
    EV7 -. feedback .-> EV3
```

### 4.3 Bubble Dynamics and Boiling Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Subcooled Liquid at Heater Surface] --> S2[Superheat Boundary Layer Forms]
    S2 --> S3[Nucleation Cavity Activated]
    S3 --> S4[Embryo Bubble Growth on Surface]
    S4 --> S5{Diameter Check}
    S5 -->|Less than Departure Diameter| S6[Continue Growing]
    S6 --> S5
    S5 -->|Greater than Departure Diameter| S7[Buoyancy Force Dominates]
    S7 --> S8[Bubble Detachment]
    S8 --> S9[Bubble Rise Through Liquid]
    S9 --> S10{Subcooled or Saturated Bulk}
    S10 -->|Subcooled| S11[Condensation and Collapse]
    S10 -->|Saturated| S12[Reaches Free Surface and Releases Vapor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 Model]**
Define **pool boiling** and **nucleate boiling**. How does nucleate boiling differ from film boiling in terms of heat transfer mechanism?

**Model Answer:**
Pool boiling is the boiling process that occurs on a heated surface submerged in a body of liquid that is essentially stagnant.

**Nucleate Boiling:** Bubbles nucleate at discrete sites on the heated surface, grow, and detach. Heat transfer is augmented by bubble-induced liquid agitation and latent heat transport.

**Film Boiling:** A continuous vapor film blankets the surface. Heat must conduct through this vapor layer, which is a poor thermal conductor. Hence, film boiling has *lower* heat transfer coefficients than nucleate boiling.

Nucleate boiling is **far more efficient** because it combines sensible and latent heat transport, while film boiling relies on conduction through a vapor layer (with radiation at high $\Delta T_e$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
What is the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** and why is it a critical parameter in boiling heat transfer?

**Model Answer:**
The Critical Heat Flux (CHF), also called burnout heat flux, is the maximum heat flux that can be sustained by a heated surface before the boiling regime transitions from nucleate to film boiling.

It is critical because:
- Beyond CHF, the heater surface temperature rises **dramatically** due to the insulating vapor film.
- This leads to **catastrophic surface failure** — fuel rod burnout in nuclear reactors, heater element melting, etc.
- CHF is a *design-limiting parameter* in reactor cores, boilers, and electronic cooling systems.
- It is essentially independent of surface conditions and depends primarily on **fluid properties and pressure**.

CHF is calculated using the **Zuber correlation**:
$$q''_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

---

### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **pool boiling curve** and identify the different regimes of boiling. Discuss the physical phenomena occurring in each region. (7 marks)

**(b)** Starting from fundamental principles, **derive the Rohsenow correlation** for nucleate boiling and discuss the role of the surface–fluid characteristic constant $C_{sf}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pool Boiling Curve:**

The Nukiyama curve plots heat flux $q''$ (y-axis) versus excess surface temperature $\Delta T_e = T_w - T_{sat}$ (x-axis) on a log scale.

| Region | Heat Transfer Mode | Description |
|---|---|---|
| A–B | Natural Convection | No vapor formation; heat transfer by single-phase convection only |
| B–C | Onset of Nucleate Boiling | First bubbles appear at preferred cavities |
| B–D | Partial Nucleate Boiling | Bubble density increases, more sites active |
| D–E | Fully Developed Nucleate Boiling | Vapor columns coalesce and rise; maximum efficiency |
| Point E | **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** | Peak of curve; transition to film boiling |
| E–F | Transition Boiling | Unstable; intermittent vapor blanketing |
| Point F | **Leidenfrost Point** | $q''_{min}$; vapor film becomes stable |
| F–G | Unstable Film Boiling | Film formed and collapsed repeatedly |
| G–H | Stable Film Boiling | Continuous vapor blanket; conduction + radiation |

At **point E**, the slope of the curve decreases sharply, indicating CHF. Beyond CHF, $\Delta T_e$ rises rapidly even as $q''$ drops — this is the *boiling crisis*.

**(b) Rohsenow Correlation Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.

Key steps:
1. Bubble departure diameter: $d_b = \left[\dfrac{6 \sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2}$
2. Bubble growth rate from Rayleigh–Plesset solution: $\dfrac{dr}{dt} \propto \dfrac{\Delta T_e}{h_{fg}}$
3. Energy balance: $q'' = h_{fg} \cdot \dot{m}''$
4. Final form:
$$q'' = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, Pr_l^n}\right]^3$$

**Role of $C_{sf}$:** The constant $C_{sf}$ accounts for the surface–liquid combination. It depends on surface wettability, roughness, and oxidation state. Higher $C_{sf}$ values yield *lower* heat fluxes for the same $\Delta T_e$, representing a "more difficult" boiling surface. For example:
- Water–Copper (mechanically polished): $C_{sf} = 0.0130, n = 1.0$
- Water–Stainless Steel (mechanically polished): $C_{sf} = 0.0132, n = 1.0$
- Water–Brass (mechanically polished): $C_{sf} = 0.0060, n = 1.0$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between boiling in an **open vessel** and an **enclosed vessel**, with the help of neat diagrams. Explain how the saturation temperature changes in each case. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Zuber correlation for Critical Heat Flux** and explain why CHF is independent of the heater surface material. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Open vs. Enclosed Vessel Boiling:**

**Open Vessel:**
- Operates at constant atmospheric pressure ($P \approx 1.01325$ bar).
- Vapor escapes freely; the boiling temperature remains fixed at 100 °C for water.
- Heat input drives the boiling process; vapor production rate increases with $q''$.
- Phase equilibrium is irrelevant — vapor is lost continuously.
- Mathematical model: $q'' = f(\Delta T_e)$ with $P = $ constant.

**Enclosed Vessel:**
- Operates at a pressure determined by system temperature via Clausius–Clapeyron.
- Vapor accumulates in the headspace; a **two-phase equilibrium** is established.
- As heating continues, both pressure and temperature rise together.
- Saturation temperature is given by: $T_{sat} = T_{sat}(P) = $ increasing function of $P$.
- Mathematical model: Coupled PDEs of mass, energy, and momentum balance.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Open Vessel | Enclosed Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | Fixed (atmospheric) | Variable |
| $T_{sat}$ | Constant | Rises with heating |
| Mass in system | Decreases (vapor escapes) | Conserved |
| Governing physics | Heat transfer | Heat + mass transfer + thermodynamics |
| Phase equilibrium | None | Clausius–Clapeyron applicable |

**Diagrams:** See **Section 4.2** of these notes for the process architecture flow diagram.

**(b) Derivation of Zuber Correlation:**

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes for the full derivation.

**Key Points:**
- Zuber used the **Taylor hydrodynamic instability** of vapor columns rising from a heated surface.
- The characteristic wavelength: $\lambda = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{3\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$
- Vapor velocity at instability: $u_v = \left[\dfrac{g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$
- CHF arises from mass flux × latent heat: $q''_{max} = h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \cdot u_v$
- Final Zuber correlation:
$$q''_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Why CHF is independent of surface material:**
- The derivation uses only **fluid properties** ($\rho_l, \rho_v, \sigma, h_{fg}$) and **gravity**.
- No surface roughness, wettability, or material properties appear.
- At CHF, the bubble dynamics are governed by **hydrodynamic instability** in the bulk liquid, not by surface wetting.
- This is experimentally verified for a wide range of surfaces (polished, rough, oxidized) at the same pressure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the *Leidenfrost point* with CHF. CHF is the **peak heat flux**; Leidenfrost point is the **minimum heat flux** in film boiling regime. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** In Rohsenow correlation, $C_{sf}$ is *not* dimensionless — it has units that ensure dimensional consistency. Forgetting to specify this loses a mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Open vessel boiling is NOT necessarily saturated boiling. If $T_{bulk} < T_{sat}$, subcooled boiling occurs even at atmospheric pressure.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In an enclosed vessel, do NOT assume pressure is constant. Pressure is a *dependent variable* determined by temperature via Clausius–Clapeyron.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** The Bromley equation applies only to *stable film boiling* (Region G–H), not to transition boiling.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Pool Boiling Curve (Nukiyama):** Six distinct regions from natural convection to stable film boiling.
- [x] **Nucleate Boiling:** Most efficient boiling regime; heat transfer via bubble agitation + latent heat.
- [x] **CHF (Zuber):** $q''_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Leidenfrost Point (Berenson):** $q''_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2}\right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Rohsenow (Nucleate):** $q'' = \mu_l h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l}\Delta T_e}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n}\right]^3$
- [x] **Bromley (Film Boiling):** $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{k_v^3 \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v) g (h_{fg} + 0.4 c_{p,v}\Delta T_e)}{\mu_v L \Delta T_e}\right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Open Vessel:** $P = $ atmospheric, $T_{sat} = $ fixed, vapor escapes.
- [x] **Enclosed Vessel:** $P = P(T_{sat})$ via Clausius–Clapeyron; vapor accumulates; two-phase equilibrium.
- [x] **CHF is a surface-independent property** — depends only on fluid properties and pressure.
- [x] **Boiling Number** $Bo = \dfrac{q''}{G h_{fg}}$ and **Jakob Number** $Ja = \dfrac{c_{p,l}\Delta T_e}{h_{fg}}$ are key dimensionless groups.
- [x] **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** governs enclosed vessel saturation: $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T v_{fg}}$
- [x] For KTU numericals, memorize the **empirical constants 0.149 (Zuber) and 0.09 (Berenson)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Batch Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Batch Distillation** is a steady-state unsteady physical separation process in which a finite quantity (charge) of a liquid mixture is charged into a reboiler (still pot) and subjected to vaporization, with the vapor being condensed and collected as discrete fractions over the course of operation. Unlike continuous distillation, the composition in the still pot changes continuously with time, making it inherently a **dynamic, transient, lumped-parameter process**.

The fundamental governing relationship is the **Rayleigh Equation** (1902), which mathematically couples the instantaneous liquid composition in the still pot ($x$), the vapor composition in equilibrium with it ($y^*$), and the ratio of the residual moles ($W$) to the initial moles ($F$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The core mathematical modelling of batch distillation in Module 2 revolves around the **Rayleigh Equation**, the assumption of **constant molar overflow (CMO)**, and the derivation of material balance expressions under both **constant distillate composition** and **constant reflux ratio** operating policies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tea Brewing Cup:**
> 
> Imagine you have a cup of *masala chai* (spiced tea) with milk and water. You gently heat the cup, and the more volatile aromas/essential oils (think of these as the *light key component*) escape as vapor first, leaving behind a stronger, more concentrated liquid. As heating continues, the liquid becomes progressively richer in the *heavy key component* (water/milk solids).
> 
> If you were to **condense and collect the vapor at different times**, each "shot" of condensed vapor would have a different composition:
> - **Early shots** → highly aromatic, volatile-rich (analogous to high purity distillate)
> - **Middle shots** → transitional mixture
> - **Late shots** → mostly water/milk residue vapor
> 
> This *time-wise* composition variation is the hallmark of **batch distillation**. The cup (still pot) is a *finite reservoir*, so the residual liquid composition drifts as the process unfolds. You cannot maintain a fixed distillate purity without *actively* manipulating the reflux ratio — just as you cannot keep brewing a perfectly identical cup of chai without changing the heat input.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard engineering metrics** characterize batch distillation:

- **Molar holdup of liquid in the still pot** ($W$, in **kmol**) — the residual liquid inventory at any time $t$.
- **Initial charge** ($F$, in **kmol**) — the total moles of mixture charged initially.
- **Residue fraction** ($q = W/F$, dimensionless) — the fraction of original charge remaining in the pot.
- **Distillate composition** ($x_D$, mole fraction) — the instantaneous composition of condensed overhead product.
- **Reflux ratio** ($R = L/D$, dimensionless) — the ratio of liquid returned to the column to the distillate withdrawn.
- **Relative volatility** ($\alpha_{LK,HK}$) — separation factor between the light and heavy key components (typically **$\alpha > 1$** for the LK).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** In continuous distillation, time is *not* a variable; the state is steady. In batch distillation, **time is the independent variable**, and all compositions and flows vary with $t$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Philosophy and Process Logic

Batch distillation is performed in two primary operating modes:

**Mode 1: Constant Reflux Ratio (CRR) Policy**
- The reflux ratio $R$ is held **fixed** throughout the batch.
- The distillate composition $x_D$ **decreases monotonically** as the still pot depletes.
- The operator collects the distillate in **cumulative cuts** (fractions).

**Mode 2: Constant Distillate Composition (CDC) Policy**
- The distillate composition $x_D$ is held **fixed** at a target specification.
- The reflux ratio $R$ is **continuously increased** to maintain purity as the pot gets depleted.
- This requires an **advanced control loop** (model-predictive or feedback).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework (Constant Molar Overflow Assumption)

Under the **CMO assumption** (latent heats of vaporization are essentially equal, and sensible heats + heat of mixing are negligible):

1. **Molar vapor rate** leaving the top of the column: $V$ (kmol/h), constant.
2. **Molar liquid rate** in the rectifying section: $L$ (kmol/h), constant.
3. **Distillate rate**: $D = V - L$ (constant under CMO).
4. **Reflux ratio**: $R = L/D$ (constant under CRR).
5. The **overall material balance** on the still pot is: $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = -D$.
6. A **component material balance** on the light key gives: $\dfrac{d(W x)}{dt} = -D x_D$.
7. Expanding using the product rule: $W \dfrac{dx}{dt} + x \dfrac{dW}{dt} = -D x_D$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Operating Mode** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rayleigh Equation (Differential Form) | $\dfrac{dx}{y^* - x} = \dfrac{dW}{W}$ | $x$ : pot liquid mole fraction; $y^*$ : equilibrium vapor mole fraction; $W$ : residual moles (kmol) | All modes |
| Rayleigh Equation (Integrated Form, Binary) | $\ln \left( \dfrac{F}{W} \right) = \displaystyle\int_{x_W}^{x_F} \dfrac{dx}{y^* - x}$ | $F$ : initial moles (kmol); $W$ : final moles (kmol); $x_F, x_W$ : initial/final pot compositions | All modes |
| Rayleigh Equation (Constant $\alpha$, Analytical) | $\ln \left( \dfrac{F}{W} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln \left( \dfrac{x_F}{x_W} \right) + \alpha \ln \left( \dfrac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F} \right) \right]$ | $\alpha$ : relative volatility (dimensionless) | Constant $\alpha$ |
| Overall Material Balance (Pot) | $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = -D$ | $D$ : distillate molar flow (kmol/h); $t$ : time (h) | CMO assumed |
| Component Balance (LK) | $W \dfrac{dx}{dt} + x \dfrac{dW}{dt} = -D x_D$ | $x_D$ : distillate mole fraction | CMO assumed |
| Reflux Ratio Definition | $R = \dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{V - D}{D}$ | $L$ : reflux flow (kmol/h); $V$ : vapor boil-up (kmol/h) | All modes |
| Pot Composition vs. Time (CRR) | $x(t) = \dfrac{x_F \cdot \exp\!\left[ -(\alpha - 1) D t \,/\, W \right] \cdot (\text{terms})}{1}$ | Implicit; solved via ODE integration | CRR |
| Distillate Average Composition | $\bar{x}_D = \dfrac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t_{\text{batch}}} D \cdot x_D(t) \, dt}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t_{\text{batch}}} D \, dt}$ | Cumulative average | Post-batch accounting |
| Minimum Reflux (CDC) | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y^*_{F}}{y^*_{F} - x_F}$ | At start of batch | CDC |
| Material Balance Tie-Up | $F x_F = W x_W + (F - W) \bar{x}_D$ | Global mass balance | All modes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** For absolute value expressions like $\vert x \vert$, use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` rather than the keyboard pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The same applies to set notations $\mid$ , $\vert$ and any norm expressions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Batch distillation is the **workhorse of low-volume, high-purity separation industries**:

- **Pharmaceuticals & Fine Chemicals:** Separation of heat-sensitive drug intermediates where thermal degradation prohibits continuous high-temperature processing.
- **Specialty Spirits & Flavors:** Production of whiskey, cognac, and essential oils where batch-to-batch character is preserved.
- **Petrochemicals:** Recovery of high-purity solvents (acetone, methanol, ethanol) from waste streams.
- **Pilot Plants & R&D:** Generation of small quantities of ultra-pure reference standards.

The **dynamic nature** of the process makes it an ideal case study for **process modelling and simulation** — it allows students to understand transient behavior, integration of differential equations, and the effect of operating policy on product purity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU Examinations:** The Rayleigh equation appears in nearly every KTU 2024 Scheme university exam paper under Module 2. Students are routinely asked to derive the integrated form, compute residue moles for a given target composition, or compare CRR vs. CDC operating policies. Mastery of the formula sheet above is non-negotiable for full marks.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Equation (Binary System)

**Starting Point:** Consider a binary mixture of light key (LK) and heavy key (HK) charged into a still pot.

**Step 1 — Overall Molar Balance at the Still Pot (over an infinitesimal time interval $dt$):**

Moles of LK entering the pot − Moles of LK leaving as distillate = Accumulation in pot.

Initially we have $W$ moles of liquid in the pot. In time $dt$, a small amount $dW$ of liquid is vaporized (note: $dW$ is *negative* since $W$ decreases). The vapor is in equilibrium with the residual liquid.

**Step 2 — Material Balance on the Light Key Component (LK):**

Moles of LK in the pot at time $t$: $W \cdot x$.

Moles of LK in the pot at time $t + dt$: $(W + dW)(x + dx)$.

Change in moles of LK in the pot: $d(Wx) = (W + dW)(x + dx) - W x$.

Expanding using the product rule:

$$
d(Wx) = W \, dx + x \, dW
$$

(neglecting the second-order term $dx \, dW$).

**Step 3 — Equating Accumulation to Vapor Leaving:**

The vapor leaving the pot has composition $y^*$ in equilibrium with the residual liquid. The moles of LK leaving in the vapor stream are $y^* \cdot (-dW)$ (since $-dW$ is the amount vaporized).

Therefore, the accumulation equals the moles of LK lost in vapor:

$$
W \, dx + x \, dW = y^* \, (-dW)
$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging into the Standard Rayleigh Form:**

Move the $x \, dW$ term to the right side:

$$
W \, dx = -y^* \, dW - x \, dW = -(y^* + x) \, dW
$$

Wait — this contains a sign error that we must correct carefully. Let us re-derive:

The moles of LK leaving in the vapor stream is $y^* \cdot (-dW)$. So:

$$
W \, dx + x \, dW = y^* \, (-dW)
$$

Bring $x \, dW$ to the right side:

$$
W \, dx = -y^* \, dW - x \, dW = -(y^* + x) \, dW
$$

Dividing both sides by $(y^* - x) \cdot W$:

$$
\frac{dx}{y^* - x} = -\frac{dW}{W}
$$

But since $W$ is *decreasing*, $dW$ is *negative*, so $-dW/W$ is *positive*. We can equivalently write:

$$
\frac{dx}{y^* - x} = \frac{d(-W)}{W}
$$

Integrating from the initial state ($W = F$, $x = x_F$) to the final state ($W = W$, $x = x_W$):

$$
\int_{x_F}^{x_W} \frac{dx}{y^* - x} = \int_{F}^{W} \frac{dW}{W} \cdot (-1) = \ln\left(\frac{W}{F}\right)
$$

Rearranging (note: $W < F$, so $W/F < 1$, hence $\ln(W/F) < 0$):

$$
\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} \frac{dx}{y^* - x}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting that the integration limits **flip** when we move $W/F$ to the LHS. The final form has the *upper* limit as $x_F$ (initial) and the *lower* limit as $x_W$ (final). Examine the sign carefully in every numerical problem.

### 3.2 Analytical Solution for Constant Relative Volatility

For an **ideal binary system** obeying Raoult's Law, the vapor-liquid equilibrium relation is:

$$
y^* = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}
$$

Substituting this into the Rayleigh integral:

$$
y^* - x = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x} - x = \frac{\alpha x - x[1 + (\alpha - 1) x]}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}
$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$
\alpha x - x - x(\alpha - 1) x = (\alpha - 1) x - (\alpha - 1) x^2 = (\alpha - 1) x (1 - x)
$$

Therefore:

$$
y^* - x = \frac{(\alpha - 1) x (1 - x)}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}
$$

Now, the Rayleigh integrand becomes:

$$
\frac{1}{y^* - x} = \frac{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}{(\alpha - 1) x (1 - x)}
$$

Performing **partial fraction decomposition**:

$$
\frac{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}{(\alpha - 1) x (1 - x)} = \frac{A}{x} + \frac{B}{1 - x}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $x(1-x)$:

$$
1 + (\alpha - 1) x = A(1 - x) + B x
$$

Setting $x = 0$: $1 = A$, so $A = 1$.

Setting $x = 1$: $1 + (\alpha - 1) = B$, so $B = \alpha$.

Therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{y^* - x} = \frac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \left[ \frac{1}{x} + \frac{\alpha}{1 - x} \right]
$$

**Integrating** with respect to $x$ from $x_W$ to $x_F$:

$$
\int_{x_W}^{x_F} \frac{dx}{y^* - x} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln(x) - \alpha \ln(1 - x) \right]_{x_W}^{x_F}
$$

Evaluating at the limits:

$$
= \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left\{ \left[ \ln(x_F) - \alpha \ln(1 - x_F) \right] - \left[ \ln(x_W) - \alpha \ln(1 - x_W) \right] \right\}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right) \right]
$$

Equating to the Rayleigh equation LHS:

$$
\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right) \right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This closed-form analytical solution is the **most exam-relevant** result in KTU Module 2. Students must be able to derive it from scratch under exam conditions and apply it to compute $W$ for a given $x_W$ (or vice versa).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A batch still is charged with **100 kmol** of a benzene-toluene mixture with $x_F = 0.60$ (mole fraction benzene). The relative volatility $\alpha_{B/T} = 2.47$ is constant. The batch is operated until the pot composition drops to $x_W = 0.30$. Determine:
- (i) The fraction of charge remaining in the still pot.
- (ii) The average distillate composition.
- (iii) The total moles of distillate collected.

**Solution:**

**(i) Compute $\ln(F/W)$ using the analytical Rayleigh equation:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \frac{1}{2.47 - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{0.60}{0.30}\right) + 2.47 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1 - 0.30}{1 - 0.60}\right) \right]
$$

Computing each term:

- $\ln(0.60/0.30) = \ln(2.00) = 0.6931$
- $\ln(0.70/0.40) = \ln(1.75) = 0.5596$
- $2.47 \times 0.5596 = 1.3823$
- Sum: $0.6931 + 1.3823 = 2.0754$
- Division by $(\alpha - 1) = 1.47$: $2.0754 / 1.47 = 1.4118$

Therefore:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = 1.4118 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F}{W} = e^{1.4118} = 4.1037
$$

$$
\frac{W}{F} = \frac{1}{4.1037} = 0.2437
$$

**The fraction of charge remaining in the pot is $W/F = 0.2437$ (24.37%).**

**(ii) Average Distillate Composition — Use the Global Mass Balance:**

$$
F \cdot x_F = W \cdot x_W + (F - W) \cdot \bar{x}_D
$$

Substituting values: $F = 100$ kmol, $W = 24.37$ kmol, $x_F = 0.60$, $x_W = 0.30$:

$$
100 \times 0.60 = 24.37 \times 0.30 + (100 - 24.37) \cdot \bar{x}_D
$$

$$
60.00 = 7.311 + 75.63 \cdot \bar{x}_D
$$

$$
75.63 \cdot \bar{x}_D = 52.689
$$

$$
\bar{x}_D = \frac{52.689}{75.63} = 0.6967
$$

**The average distillate composition is $\bar{x}_D = 0.6967$.**

**(iii) Total Moles of Distillate Collected:**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = F - W = 100 - 24.37 = 75.63 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Answer Summary:**
- $W/F = 0.2437$ → $W = 24.37$ kmol
- $\bar{x}_D = 0.6967$ mole fraction benzene
- $D_{\text{total}} = 75.63$ kmol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** The distillate composition (0.6967) is *between* the initial pot composition (0.60) and the equilibrium vapor composition at start ($y^* = \alpha x_F / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x_F] = 2.47 \times 0.60 / [1 + 1.47 \times 0.60] = 1.482 / 1.882 = 0.7875$). So $0.60 < 0.6967 < 0.7875$ — physically consistent. ✓

### 3.4 Derivation of Distillate Composition Under Constant Reflux Ratio (CRR)

Under CRR with CMO assumptions, the differential material balance on the still pot gives:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = -\frac{D}{W}(x_D - x)
$$

Since the distillate rate $D$ and the pot holdup $W$ are time-varying, and $x_D$ is determined by the operating line intersecting the equilibrium curve at the rectifying section, this is a **stiff ordinary differential equation** that typically requires **numerical integration** (e.g., Euler, RK4, or MATLAB's `ode45`).

The **analytical shortcut** for binary, constant $\alpha$ uses the Rayleigh equation directly with $x_D$ replaced by its average over the integration interval.

```mermaid
    node3 --> node4["CONDENSED LIQUID SPLIT"]

    node5 --> node7["REFLUX RETURN LINE"]
    node7 --> node1
    node1 --> node8["RESIDUE W WITHDRAWAL"]
    node1 --> node9["THERMAL ENERGY INPUT Q"]
    node1 --> node10["LIQUID COMPOSITION x T"]
    node3 --> node11["VAPOR COMPOSITION y STAR T"]
    node4 --> node12["REFLUX RATIO R CONTROL LOOP"]
    node12 --> node4
    node6 --> node13["DISTILLATE FRACTION CUTS F1 F2 F3"]
    node8 --> node14["HEAVY RESIDUE STORAGE"]

    node16 --> node12
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CHARGE F xF t0"] --> B["STILL POT W"]

    D -->|"Liquid L"| E["REFLUX DRUM"]

    E -->|"Distillate D"| F["PRODUCT CUT COLLECTOR"]

```

### 4.3 Operational Policy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["BATCH DISTILLATION OPERATION"] --> Q1{"TARGET PRODUCT PURITY?"}
    Q1 -->|"Fixed xD specification"| CDC["CONSTANT DISTILLATE COMPOSITION"]
    Q1 -->|"Variable xD acceptable"| CRR["CONSTANT REFLUX RATIO"]
    CDC --> R1["Reflux R increases with time"]
    CRR --> R2["xD decreases with time"]
    R1 --> OUT1["Multiple cuts of fixed purity collected"]
    R2 --> OUT2["Cumulative distillate has average xD bar"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["POT HOLDUP W x"] -->|"dx dW change"| P2["POT HOLDUP W dW x dx"]
    P2 -->|"Equilibrium Vapor y star"| P3["LK MOLE LOSS y star times minus dW"]
    P2 -->|"HK MOLE LOSS one minus y star times minus dW"| P4["HK MOLE LOSS y star times minus dW"]
    P3 --> RAYLEIGH["RAYLEIGH INTEGRAL"]
    P4 --> RAYLEIGH
    RAYLEIGH --> RES["F W relation via ln F W"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each Mermaid block here is a **functional architecture**, not a physical P&ID. For physical drawings (e.g., a column with trays, reboiler, condenser), the KTU standard recommends cross-referencing McCabe–Smith–Harriott or Geankoplis textbook schematics. The block diagrams above are fully sufficient for **modelling and simulation** discussions in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the Rayleigh equation for batch distillation and define all the terms involved. Under what assumption is the integrated form valid?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Rayleigh equation is:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} \frac{dx}{y^* - x}
$$

where:
- $F$ = initial moles of charge in the still pot (kmol)
- $W$ = residual moles of liquid in the pot at any time (kmol)
- $x_F$ = initial mole fraction of light key in the pot
- $x_W$ = final mole fraction of light key in the pot
- $y^*$ = equilibrium vapor mole fraction of light key
- $x$ = instantaneous mole fraction of LK in the pot

The integrated form is valid under the **assumption of constant molar overflow (CMO)** and **quasi-steady-state** behavior in the column, with negligible holdup of liquid on the trays compared to the reboiler. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare the constant reflux ratio (CRR) and constant distillate composition (CDC) operating policies in batch distillation. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Policy | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **CRR (Constant Reflux Ratio)** | Simpler control scheme; constant boil-up rate; easier to design and operate. | Distillate purity decreases with time; lower-quality late cuts may need to be discarded. |
| **CDC (Constant Distillate Composition)** | High-purity product throughout; no slop cuts (in ideal operation); higher product value. | Reflux ratio must be increased continuously; more energy-intensive; requires advanced control. |

(3 marks — 1 for comparison, 1 for CRR pair, 1 for CDC pair)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**  `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the Rayleigh equation for a binary mixture undergoing batch distillation, starting from a differential material balance on the light key component in the still pot. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch distillation column is charged with **150 kmol** of a methanol–water mixture. The initial methanol mole fraction is $x_F = 0.45$. The relative volatility $\alpha_{MeOH/H_2O} = 3.20$ is constant. The batch is operated under the Rayleigh equation (CRR mode) until the pot composition drops to $x_W = 0.20$. Calculate:
   1. The moles of residue left in the still pot. **(3 marks)**
   2. The total moles of distillate collected. **(2 marks)**
   3. The average distillate composition. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumption Statement (1 mark):**
- Binary mixture, constant molar overflow, quasi-equilibrium between vapor and pot liquid, negligible tray holdup, ideal VLE with constant relative volatility.

**Step 1 — Overall Molar Balance (1 mark):**
At time $t$, the pot contains $W$ kmol of liquid. In time $dt$, an amount $-dW$ is vaporized (note: $dW < 0$). Total mass balance: change in $W$ equals vaporization.

**Step 2 — Component (LK) Material Balance (2 marks):**
Moles of LK in pot at $t$: $W x$.
Moles of LK in pot at $t + dt$: $(W + dW)(x + dx)$.
Moles of LK leaving in vapor: $y^* \cdot (-dW)$.

Equating:

$$
d(Wx) = -y^* \, dW
$$

Expanding the LHS:

$$
W \, dx + x \, dW = -y^* \, dW
$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging to Rayleigh Form (2 marks):**

$$
W \, dx = -(y^* + x) \, dW
$$

Dividing by $W(y^* - x)$ and integrating from $F$ to $W$:

$$
\int_{x_F}^{x_W} \frac{dx}{y^* - x} = -\int_{F}^{W} \frac{dW}{W} = \ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Final Form (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} \frac{dx}{y^* - x}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Using the analytical Rayleigh equation with constant $\alpha$:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right) \right]
$$

**Substituting values (2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for evaluation):**
- $\alpha - 1 = 2.20$
- $\ln(x_F / x_W) = \ln(0.45/0.20) = \ln(2.25) = 0.8109$
- $\ln[(1 - x_W)/(1 - x_F)] = \ln(0.80/0.55) = \ln(1.4545) = 0.3747$
- $\alpha \times 0.3747 = 3.20 \times 0.3747 = 1.1990$
- Sum: $0.8109 + 1.1990 = 2.0099$
- Divide by $(\alpha - 1)$: $2.0099 / 2.20 = 0.9136$

Therefore:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = 0.9136 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F}{W} = e^{0.9136} = 2.4935
$$

**(1) Residue moles in the pot (1 mark):**

$$
W = \frac{F}{2.4935} = \frac{150}{2.4935} = 60.16 \text{ kmol}
$$

**(2) Total distillate collected (1 mark):**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = F - W = 150 - 60.16 = 89.84 \text{ kmol}
$$

**(3) Average distillate composition (1 mark):**

From global material balance:

$$
F x_F = W x_W + D_{\text{total}} \cdot \bar{x}_D
$$

$$
150 \times 0.45 = 60.16 \times 0.20 + 89.84 \cdot \bar{x}_D
$$

$$
67.50 = 12.032 + 89.84 \cdot \bar{x}_D
$$

$$
\bar{x}_D = \frac{55.468}{89.84} = 0.6174
$$

**Final Answers:** $W = 60.16$ kmol, $D_{\text{total}} = 89.84$ kmol, $\bar{x}_D = 0.6174$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**  `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **minimum reflux ratio** in batch distillation. Derive an expression for the minimum reflux ratio at the **start of the batch** using the operating line and equilibrium relationship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ethanol–water mixture ($x_F = 0.30$ mole fraction ethanol, $F = 80$ kmol) is batch-distilled in a column with the following VLE data at the reboiler composition:
   - At $x = 0.30$: $y^* = 0.575$
   - At $x = 0.20$: $y^* = 0.435$
   - At $x = 0.10$: $y^* = 0.245$

   The desired distillate purity is $x_D = 0.80$. Calculate:
   1. The minimum reflux ratio at the start of the batch using graphical/analytical interpolation. **(3 marks)**
   2. The actual reflux ratio if the operating reflux is taken as $R = 1.5 \cdot R_{\min}$. **(2 marks)**
   3. Comment on why the actual reflux ratio must be greater than the minimum. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Minimum Reflux Ratio Concept (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The minimum reflux ratio $R_{\min}$ is the *lowest* reflux ratio at which the desired separation can be achieved in a specified number of theoretical stages. Below $R_{\min}$, the operating line intersects the equilibrium curve, and an **infinite number of stages** would be required (a pinch point forms).

**Derivation at Start of Batch (5 marks):**

At the start of the batch, the still pot composition is $x_F$, and the corresponding equilibrium vapor composition is $y^*_F$.

The rectifying-section operating line equation (mass balance around the top of the column) is:

$$
y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}
$$

The **q-line** for the reboiler is the $45°$ line ($y = x$) at the pot composition.

The minimum reflux condition occurs when the operating line passes through the intersection of the **q-line** ($y = x = x_F$) and the **equilibrium curve** ($y = y^*_F$). At this pinch point:

$$
y_{\text{pinch}} = x_F, \quad y_{\text{pinch}} = y^*_F
$$

Substituting into the operating line equation at the pinch:

$$
x_F = \frac{R_{\min}}{R_{\min} + 1} \cdot x_F + \frac{x_D}{R_{\min} + 1}
$$

Solving for $R_{\min}$:

$$
x_F (R_{\min} + 1) = R_{\min} x_F + x_D
$$

$$
x_F R_{\min} + x_F = R_{\min} x_F + x_D
$$

$$
x_F = x_D - R_{\min}(x_D - x_F)
$$

Wait — reworking carefully:

$$
R_{\min}(x_D - x_F) = x_D - x_F
$$

Let me re-derive from scratch:

$$
y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}
$$

At the pinch, the operating line passes through the point $(x_F, y^*_F)$. So:

$$
y^*_F = \frac{R_{\min}}{R_{\min} + 1} \cdot x_F + \frac{x_D}{R_{\min} + 1}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $(R_{\min} + 1)$:

$$
y^*_F (R_{\min} + 1) = R_{\min} x_F + x_D
$$

$$
R_{\min} y^*_F + y^*_F = R_{\min} x_F + x_D
$$

$$
R_{\min}(y^*_F - x_F) = x_D - y^*_F
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}}
$$

This is the **classical minimum reflux formula** for batch distillation at the start of the batch. (5 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for algebra, 1 for final boxed result)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Interpolate VLE data to find $y^*_F$ at $x_F = 0.30$ (1 mark):**

The data point $(0.30, 0.575)$ is already provided directly, so $y^*_F = 0.575$. (If interpolation were needed, linear interpolation between $(0.20, 0.435)$ and $(0.40, ?)$ would be performed.)

**Step 2 — Apply the $R_{\min}$ formula (2 marks):**

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F} = \frac{0.80 - 0.575}{0.575 - 0.30} = \frac{0.225}{0.275} = 0.818
$$

**Answer: $R_{\min} = 0.818$ at the start of the batch.**

**Step 3 — Actual reflux ratio (2 marks):**

$$
R_{\text{actual}} = 1.5 \times R_{\min} = 1.5 \times 0.818 = 1.227
$$

**Answer: $R_{\text{actual}} = 1.227$ (rounded to 1.23).**

**Step 4 — Comment on why $R > R_{\min}$ is necessary (2 marks):**
