At $R = R_{\min}$, the operating line touches the equilibrium curve at a single pinch point, and the required number of theoretical stages becomes *infinite*. In practice, the column has a **finite number of trays** (say, $N = 10$ or $20$). To achieve a finite-stage separation, the reflux must be increased above $R_{\min}$, which steepens the operating line, pushing it away from the equilibrium curve and creating a finite driving force for mass transfer on every stage. The factor of 1.5 is a typical heuristic (similar to the 1.2 to 1.5 factor used in the **Gilliland correlation** for continuous distillation design). The trade-off is higher energy consumption (more reflux = more reboiler duty) versus a more compact, lower-capital-cost column.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors ($-2$ marks):** Many students confuse the sign of $dW$ in the derivation. Remember: $W$ is *decreasing*, so $dW < 0$, and the final form has $\ln(F/W)$ on the LHS (positive quantity, since $F > W$).
> 
> 2. **Integration Limit Reversal ($-2$ marks):** When the limit is $x_W \to x_F$, the integrand must be $dx/(y^* - x)$ with $x_F$ as the *upper* limit. Reversing the limits introduces a sign error.
> 
> 3. **Missing $\alpha$ in Partial Fractions ($-1$ mark):** In the partial fraction decomposition, the coefficient of $1/(1-x)$ is $\alpha$, not $1$. Forgetting this gives an incorrect integration.
> 
> 4. **Units Inconsistency ($-1$ mark):** Always quote moles in **kmol** and compositions as **mole fractions** (dimensionless). Mixing weight fractions or mass (kg) with mole fractions leads to wrong answers.
> 
> 5. **Confusing CRR and CDC ($-2$ marks):** Under CRR, $R$ is fixed and $x_D$ varies. Under CDC, $x_D$ is fixed and $R$ varies. Students frequently swap these in the final answer.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting the Global Mass Balance ($-1$ mark):** The average distillate composition *cannot* be read directly from the Rayleigh equation; it requires the overall material balance $F x_F = W x_W + (F-W) \bar{x}_D$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Batch Distillation (Module 2)**

- ✅ The **Rayleigh equation** is the foundational material balance for batch distillation: $\ln(F/W) = \int dx/(y^* - x)$.
- ✅ The **CMO (constant molar overflow)** assumption simplifies the column material balance and makes $D$ constant over time (under CRR).
- ✅ For **binary, constant $\alpha$** systems, the Rayleigh integral has a **closed-form analytical solution** involving $\ln(x_F/x_W)$ and $\alpha \ln[(1-x_W)/(1-x_F)]$.
- ✅ The **global mass balance** $F x_F = W x_W + (F - W) \bar{x}_D$ is *always* valid and is used to find the average distillate composition.
- ✅ **CRR** → fixed $R$, variable $x_D$, simpler operation, lower purity late in the batch.
- ✅ **CDC** → fixed $x_D$, variable $R$, higher product purity, more energy intensive.
- ✅ **$R_{\min}$ formula:** $R_{\min} = (x_D - y^*_F)/(y^*_F - x_F)$, derived from the operating line pinch at the start of the batch.
- ✅ **Tray holdup** is assumed negligible compared to pot holdup — this is the **lumped-parameter** assumption.
- ✅ The **Rayleigh equation does NOT apply to the column trays** — it applies *only* to the still pot (overall material balance).
- ✅ The relative volatility $\alpha$ must be **constant** (or VLE must be given as a data table) to use the analytical form.
- ✅ **Slop cuts** — intermediate distillate fractions that do not meet product specifications are recycled or blended.
- ✅ **Fenske equation** gives the minimum number of stages for batch distillation at total reflux; combined with $R_{\min}$, it is used in the **Gilliland correlation** to find the actual number of stages.
- ✅ **Time requirement** under CRR: $t_{\text{batch}} = (F - W)/D$, where $D$ is the distillate molar flow rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Closing Remarks:** Batch distillation is a *gateway topic* into the broader field of **process dynamics and control**. Mastery of the Rayleigh equation and its analytical solution is **mandatory** for the KTU End Semester Examination. The worked numerical example in Section 3.3 is representative of the difficulty level expected in the 14-mark Part B questions. Practice with **at least 5–7 additional numerical problems** from standard references to build fluency with the integration and algebraic manipulation steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal Binary Distillation Column - Simulation Using MATLAB/SciLab or Any Other Process Simulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ideal Binary Distillation Column - Simulation Using MATLAB/SciLab or Any Other Process Simulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Binary Distillation Column (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **Ideal Binary Distillation Column** is a multi-stage separation device in which a liquid mixture of two components (light key $LK$ and heavy key $HK$) is separated into a vapor product (distillate, $D$) enriched in the more volatile component and a liquid product (bottoms, $B$) enriched in the less volatile component, assuming **constant molar overflow (CMO)**, **theoretical stages (100% Murphree efficiency)**, and **binary Raoult's law equilibrium** at each stage.

In the context of the KTU **Process Modelling and Simulation** course, the *simulation* of such a column refers to predicting the steady-state composition profile $x_j$, $y_j$, temperature profile $T_j$, and flow profiles ($L_j$, $V_j$) across all $N$ stages by numerically solving the **MESH equations**:

- **M**ass balance (component & total)
- **E**quilibrium relation
- **S**ummation equation
- **H**eat (enthalpy) balance

For *ideal* columns, simplifications are invoked:
- Constant molar latent heat ⇒ **CMO holds** ($L_j = L$ and $V_j = V$ throughout the rectifying and stripping sections)
- Binary system ⇒ summation and component balance collapse into a single scalar equation per stage
- Raoult's law applies with constant relative volatility $\alpha$


## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Boiling-Pot Reflux Tower" Analogy**
> Imagine boiling salted water in a pot on a stove. The steam (vapor) rises, leaving most of the salt (heavy key) behind. If you condense that steam on a tilted cold lid and let the condensate trickle back into the pot, the returning liquid (reflux) washes the rising vapor, "scrubbing" the heavy component out of it. Stack 10 such lids in a vertical column and you have a **distillation column**. The *ideal* assumption says: every lid is perfectly efficient (the vapor leaving a lid is in perfect equilibrium with the liquid leaving it), and the heat supplied is so large that the liquid/vapor molar rates stay nearly constant on each side of the feed. Simulating this in MATLAB means solving the math that describes how the salt concentration changes from lid to lid (stage to stage) — from top to bottom.

## 1.3 Key Process Specifications and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Number of stages | $N$ | — | Includes reboiler and total condenser |
| Distillate flow | $D$ | **kmol/h** | Top product |
| Bottoms flow | $B$ | **kmol/h** | Bottom product |
| Reflux flow | $L$ | **kmol/h** | Liquid returned to column |
| Vapor boil-up | $V$ | **kmol/h** | Vapor from reboiler |
| Reflux ratio | $R = L/D$ | — | Design/operating parameter |
| Relative volatility | $\alpha$ | — | $>1$ for separable mixture |
| Feed quality | $q$ | — | Thermal condition of feed |
| Distillate composition | $x_D$ | **mole fraction** | Light key in $D$ |
| Bottoms composition | $x_B$ | **mole fraction** | Light key in $B$ |
| Murphree efficiency | $E_{MV}$ | — | $=1$ for ideal column |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MESH Equations — Decomposed

The complete steady-state model of a binary distillation column with $N$ theoretical stages consists of $N \times 4$ equations. For the **ideal** case, we eliminate enthalpy balances using the **CMO** assumption and reduce the equilibrium to a closed-form algebraic form.

### (a) Component Mass Balance (CMO form)
For stage $j$ (excluding feed, condenser, reboiler):

$$
L \, x_{j-1} + V \, y_{j+1} - L \, x_j - V \, y_j = 0
$$

### (b) Equilibrium Relation (Raoult's law, binary, constant $\alpha$)

$$
y_j = \frac{\alpha \, x_j}{1 + (\alpha - 1) \, x_j}
$$

### (c) Summation Equation

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{c} x_{i,j} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_j + (1 - x_j) = 1
$$

For binary, this is automatically satisfied; we track only $x_j$.

### (d) Overall Mass Balance (CMO)
In rectifying section: $V = L + D$, in stripping section: $\bar{V} = \bar{L} - B$, with $\bar{V} = V(1-q) + V_q$ discontinuity at feed.

### (e) Feed Stage Boundary (Fenske-Underwood-Gilliland framework)
Feed introduces discontinuity: $L_{\text{stripper}} = L_{\text{rect}} + qF$, $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$.


## 2.2 Why CMO Holds in Ideal Columns

- **Latent heats of both components are equal** ⇒ each mole vaporized condenses one mole on the next stage.
- **Sensible heat changes and heat of mixing are negligible** compared to latent heat.
- Therefore liquid and vapor molar flow rates are **piecewise constant** between feed points.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In problems, the examiner expects you to first verify the CMO assumption is applicable. State the three pre-conditions before writing simplified MESH equations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $y_j = \dfrac{\alpha \, x_j}{1 + (\alpha - 1) \, x_j}$ | Vapor-liquid equilibrium | Constant $\alpha$, binary |
| 2 | $y_{j+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} \, x_j + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Rectifying operating line (CMO) | Above feed |
| 3 | $y_{j+1} = \dfrac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} \, x_j - \dfrac{B \, x_B}{\bar{V}}$ | Stripping operating line (CMO) | Below feed |
| 4 | $q = \dfrac{\text{moles saturated liquid in feed}}{\text{total moles feed}}$ | Feed thermal condition | $q=0$ vapor, $q=1$ liquid |
| 5 | $y_q = \dfrac{q}{q-1} \, x_q - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | $q$-line (feed line) | Passes through $(z_F, z_F)$ |
| 6 | $V = (R+1) D$ | Total vapor flow (rectifying) | CMO |
| 7 | $\bar{L} = L + qF$ | Liquid flow (stripping) | CMO |
| 8 | $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$ | Vapor flow (stripping) | CMO |
| 9 | $L \, x_{j-1} - (L + V) x_j + V \, y_j = 0$ | Single-stage balance (excluding feed) | When substituting $y_{j+1} \to y_j$ after ELV step |
| 10 | $F = D + B$ | Total material balance | Steady state |
| 11 | $F z_F = D x_D + B x_B$ | Component balance | Steady state |
| 12 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$ | Minimum reflux (under $q$-line intersection) | Gilliland/Underwood |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the bare pipe `|`) inside any markdown table** to avoid breaking the KTU markdown renderer. All absolute-value / determinant expressions in the model use the symbol $\det$ or $\mid$ wrapped in display math outside tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of petroleum crude distillation units (CDU/VDU):** predicting number of trays.
- **Cryogenic air separation:** Argon/Oxygen/Nitrogen columns.
- **Pharma solvent recovery:** ethanol/water, methanol/water columns.
- **Process control:** rigorous models used in DMC (Dynamic Matrix Control) for inferential temperature control.
- **Digital twin implementation** in Industry 4.0 plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From General MESH to CMO Mass Balance

Starting from a general stage $j$ (with feed $F_j$, sidestream $S_j$):

$$
L_{j-1} \, x_{j-1} + V_{j+1} \, y_{j+1} + F_j \, z_j - (L_j + S_j) x_j - (V_j + S_j^V) y_j = 0
$$

**Step 1:** Apply **CMO** — $L_{j-1}=L_j=L$ and $V_{j+1}=V_j=V$ within each section. Sidestreams are zero for ideal column.

**Step 2:** Eliminate $S_j$ and $S_j^V$ (no side draws in ideal binary).

**Step 3:** We obtain:

$$
L \, x_{j-1} + V \, y_{j+1} - L \, x_j - V \, y_j = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V = L + D$ (rectifying) or $\bar{V} = \bar{L} - B$ (stripping) to derive the operating-line forms.

**Step 5:** Solving for $y_{j+1}$ in the rectifying section:

$$
y_{j+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_j + \frac{D}{V} x_D = \frac{R}{R+1} x_j + \frac{x_D}{R+1}
$$

This is the **rectifying operating line**.


## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form Equilibrium Composition (Solving for $x_j$)

The mass balance gives one equation in two unknowns $(x_j, y_j)$. The equilibrium gives a second:

$$
y_j = \frac{\alpha x_j}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_j}
$$

Substitute into the mass balance to obtain a single equation in $x_j$ alone. For a stage away from the feed:

$$
L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1} = L x_j + V \left[ \frac{\alpha x_j}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_j} \right]
$$

Multiply both sides by $[1 + (\alpha - 1) x_j]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1})[1 + (\alpha - 1) x_j] &= L x_j [1 + (\alpha - 1) x_j] + V \alpha x_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding the left side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1}) + (L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1})(\alpha - 1) x_j &= L x_j + L(\alpha - 1) x_j^2 + V \alpha x_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging into standard quadratic form $A x_j^2 + B x_j + C = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(\alpha - 1) \, x_j^2 &+ \Big[ L + V\alpha - (L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1})(\alpha - 1) \Big] x_j \\
&- (L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1}) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving the quadratic yields the liquid composition. The valid root is the one in $[0,1]$.


## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tridiagonal Matrix Form (Thomas Algorithm Foundation)

Define $A_j$, $B_j$, $C_j$, $D_j$ for stage $j$ in a generalized Thomas formulation. For CMO binary:

$$
A_j = L, \quad B_j = -(L + V), \quad C_j = V, \quad D_j = 0
$$

(Inside the feed stage, the $D$ term is $F z_F$.)

The stage equations form a **tridiagonal linear system** in $x_j$ once the $y$ terms are substituted with the equilibrium expression. After algebraic elimination, the system becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 x_1 + b_1 x_2 &= d_1 \\
a_j x_{j-1} + b_j x_j + c_j x_{j+1} &= d_j \quad (2 \le j \le N-1) \\
a_N x_{N-1} + b_N x_N &= d_N
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_j &= L \\
b_j &= -\Big[ L + V \cdot \frac{\alpha}{[1+(\alpha-1)x_j]^2} \Big] \\
c_j &= V \\
d_j &= -F_j z_j \quad \text{(non-zero only at feed stage)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is solved via the **Thomas Algorithm** (a Gaussian elimination specialized for tridiagonal matrices with $O(N)$ operations).

**Thomas Algorithm Pseudocode (for MATLAB/SciLab):**

1. Forward sweep: compute modified coefficients $\tilde{c}_j$, $\tilde{d}_j$
2. Back substitution: solve for $x_N$, $x_{N-1}, \ldots, x_1$


## 3.4 Derivation 4 — $q$-Line Equation

The feed stream enters at stage $f$ with vapor fraction $f_v = 1 - q$. Total balance at the feed stage:

$$
L_f + F = \bar{L}_f \quad \text{and} \quad V_f = \bar{V}_f + (1-q)F
$$

Component balance at feed stage:

$$
L_f x_{f-1} + F z_F + \bar{V}_{f+1} y_{f+1} = \bar{L}_f x_f + \bar{V}_f y_f
$$

Subtracting the two operating-line forms and using $\bar{L} = L + qF$, $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$:

$$
y_f = \frac{q}{q-1} x_f - \frac{z_F}{q-1}
$$

This passes through the point $(z_F, z_F)$ on the $y$–$x$ diagram.


## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection for KTU Problems

| Method | Equations Solved | Inputs Required | Outputs | When to Use (KTU) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| McCabe-Thiele (graphical) | CMO + equilibrium | $x_D, x_B, z_F, q, R, \alpha$ | $N_{\min}, R_{\min}, N_{\text{actual}}$ | Conceptual/design questions (3–7 marks) |
| Lewis-Matheson (stage-to-stage) | CMO + equilibrium | $x_D, x_B, z_F, q, R, \alpha$ | Stage-by-stage $x_j, y_j$ | Manual computation of tray profiles |
| Thiele-Geddes (component-by-component) | Full MESH, no CMO | $N, T_j, V_j, L_j$ | Compositions, $T$ profile | Rigorous simulation |
| Thomas Algorithm (matrix) | CMO linearized MESH | $N, L, V, F, \alpha, q$ | $x_j$ profile | Computational (MATLAB) questions |
| Newton-Raphson on MESH | Full MESH, non-CMO | All $N$ unknowns | All flows, $T$, compositions | Rigorous dynamic / non-ideal simulation |
| Inside-out (Boston/Sullivan) | Full MESH with inner/outer loops | Wide range of feeds | Converged rigorous profile | Industrial simulators (Aspen, HYSYS) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Simulation Algorithm

The following Mermaid diagram shows the step-by-step computational flow of simulating an ideal binary distillation column using MATLAB/SciLab (Thomas Algorithm / Newton iteration on MESH).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: User Inputs] --> B[Read N, alpha, F, zF, xD, xB, R, q]
    B --> C[Compute L, V, Lbar, Vbar from Material Balance]
    C --> D[Initialize xj equal to zF for all j]
    D --> E{Choose Solver}
    E -->|CMO Binary| F[Thomas Algorithm on Tridiagonal System]
    E -->|Non-CMO| G[Newton-Raphson on Full MESH]
    F --> H[Convergence Check: max abs xj_new minus xj_old less than 1e-8]
    G --> H
    H -->|Not Converged| I[Update xj and Iterate]
    I --> E
    H -->|Converged| J[Compute yj from Equilibrium Relation]
    J --> K[Compute Tj from Bubble Point Equation]
    K --> L[Generate Plots: xj vs j, yj vs j, Tj vs j]
    L --> M[Print Nmin, Rmin, Nactual, Composition Profile]
    M --> N[End: Export Results to .mat or .csv]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Stage-by-Stage MESH Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageJ["Stage j (general)"]
        In1["V sub j+1, y sub j+1"] --> StageNode["Liquid-Vapor Contact\n100 percent Murphree Efficiency"]
        In2["L sub j-1, x sub j-1"] --> StageNode
        In3["F sub j, z sub j (optional)"] --> StageNode
        StageNode --> Out1["V sub j, y sub j (vapor up)"]
        StageNode --> Out2["L sub j, x sub j (liquid down)"]
    end
    StageNode -. Equilibrium .-> EqNote["y sub j equals alpha x sub j over 1 plus alpha minus 1 times x sub j"]
    StageNode -. Summation .-> SumNote["x sub j plus x sub j heavy equals 1"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — $y$–$x$ Diagram with Operating Lines and $q$-Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["y equals x Diagonal Line"] --> P2
    P3["Equilibrium Curve y equals alpha x over 1 plus alpha minus 1 x"] --> P2
    P2["Stage Stepping Construction"] --> P4
    P5["Rectifying Operating Line Slope R over R plus 1, intercept xD over R plus 1"] --> P4
    P6["q-Line Slope q over q minus 1, passes through zF zF"] --> P4
    P7["Stripping Operating Line Slope Lbar over Vbar, intercept minus B xB over Vbar"] --> P4
    P4 --> P8["Count Stages from xB up to xD"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Coupling with MATLAB's `ode45` for Dynamic Simulation

For dynamic (transient) simulation of a binary column, the MESH equations become ODEs in time. The structure of the call to `ode45` in MATLAB:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Define Function dxdt equals columnODE t x u"] --> Q2
    Q2["Inside: Compute L, V, D, B from current x and u"] --> Q3
    Q3["Compute y from ELV using alpha and T from bubble point"] --> Q4
    Q4["Compute holdup derivative M d x sub j dt equals In minus Out plus Generation"] --> Q5
    Q5["Return dxdt"] --> Q6
    Q6["Call sol equals ode45 at columnODE tspan x0"] --> Q7
    Q7["Plot sol x time and sol y time"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the assumptions made in modelling an *ideal* binary distillation column under the constant molar overflow (CMO) approximation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Theoretical stages:** Each stage is an equilibrium stage, i.e., the vapor leaving is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the liquid leaving. Murphree vapor efficiency $E_{MV} = 1$.
2. **Constant molar overflow:** The molar heat of vaporization of both components is identical, and sensible heat changes and heat of mixing are negligible. Hence, the liquid and vapor molar flow rates are constant in each section of the column.
3. **Binary system:** Only two components are present, and one component balance plus the summation equation fully describe the compositions.
4. **Raoult's law / constant relative volatility $\alpha$:** The vapor-liquid equilibrium is described by $y_j = \alpha x_j / [1 + (\alpha-1)x_j]$.
5. **Adiabatic operation:** No heat loss to surroundings; reboiler and condenser are the only heat exchange points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Each assumption is worth 0.5–1 mark. Listing only "ideal stages" without naming the underlying physics loses 1 mark.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Write the four MESH equations for a general stage $j$ of a distillation column and identify which equation reduces to an algebraic form in the ideal CMO case.**

**Model Answer:**

The four MESH equations for stage $j$ are:

1. **M — Material balance (component $i$):** $L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} + F_j z_{i,j} - (L_j + S_j^L) x_{i,j} - (V_j + S_j^V) y_{i,j} = 0$
2. **E — Equilibrium:** $y_{i,j} = K_{i,j} x_{i,j}$
3. **S — Summation:** $\sum_i x_{i,j} = 1$
4. **H — Heat (enthalpy) balance:** $L_{j-1} h_{j-1} + V_{j+1} H_{j+1} + F_j h_{F,j} - (L_j + S_j^L) h_j - (V_j + S_j^V) H_j - Q_j = 0$

In the **ideal CMO case**, the **H (enthalpy) balance reduces to an algebraic form** giving constant $L_j$ and $V_j$ in each section. Therefore, only the first three M-E-S equations need to be solved (or just M + E for binary CMO, since S is automatically satisfied).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Many students write the H-balance but do not explicitly state that it is the *enthalpy* balance which collapses under CMO. This is where the 3 marks are awarded.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$) of 100 kmol/h containing 50 mol% benzene (LK) and 50 mol% toluene (HK) is to be separated in a column with a total condenser and partial reboiler to give a distillate of 95 mol% benzene and bottoms of 5 mol% benzene. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$ is constant. The reflux ratio is $R = 1.5 \, R_{\min}$.
**(i)** Determine $R_{\min}$ and the operating $R$.
**(ii)** Derive the rectifying and stripping operating-line equations.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Simulate the above column in MATLAB/SciLab using the Thomas algorithm. Show the input code skeleton, explain the tridiagonal matrix construction, and present a sample plot of the liquid composition profile $x_j$ vs stage $j$ for at least 10 stages.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Feed condition and intersection point**
For a saturated liquid feed, $q = 1$. The $q$-line is vertical at $x = z_F = 0.50$.

**Step 2: Find intersection of rectifying operating line with $q$-line**
The $q$-line passes through $(0.50, 0.50)$. The rectifying operating line is:

$$
y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}
$$

At the $q$-line intersection, $x = y = 0.50$. Substituting into the operating line:

$$
0.50 = \frac{R}{R+1} \cdot 0.50 + \frac{0.95}{R+1}
$$

Multiply by $(R+1)$:

$$
0.50(R+1) = 0.50 R + 0.95
$$

$$
0.50 R + 0.50 = 0.50 R + 0.95
$$

This gives a contradiction ($0.50 = 0.95$). Therefore, the intersection of the rectifying line with the $q$-line for $q=1$ actually occurs at $x = x_D$ when the operating line is tangent to the equilibrium curve or at the intersection of the operating line with $x = z_F$.

For $q = 1$ and saturated liquid feed, the $q$-line is the vertical line $x = 0.50$. The intersection with the operating line is at $x = 0.50$, and the corresponding $y$ from the operating line is:

$$
y_{int} = \frac{R}{R+1} \cdot 0.50 + \frac{0.95}{R+1}
$$

**Step 3: Use Underwood's condition for $R_{\min}$**
Underwood's equation for saturated liquid feed gives:

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{1}{(\alpha - 1) z_F} \left[ x_D - \alpha z_F (1 - x_D) \right] \quad \text{... (special case)}
$$

For a saturated liquid feed, the pinch occurs at the feed stage. Compute $y$ on the equilibrium curve at $x = 0.50$:

$$
y_{eq}(0.50) = \frac{2.47 \cdot 0.50}{1 + (2.47 - 1) \cdot 0.50} = \frac{1.235}{1 + 0.735} = \frac{1.235}{1.735} = 0.7118
$$

The rectifying operating line passes through $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ and the pinch point $(0.50, 0.7118)$:

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{0.95 - 0.7118}{0.95 - 0.50} = \frac{0.2382}{0.45} = 0.5293
$$

The slope equals $R/(R+1)$:

$$
\frac{R_{\min}}{R_{\min}+1} = 0.5293 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{\min} = 0.5293 / 0.4707 = 1.124
$$

**Operating R:**

$$
R = 1.5 \times 1.124 = 1.686
$$

**Step 4: Rectifying operating line**

$$
y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{1.686}{2.686} x + \frac{0.95}{2.686} = 0.6277 x + 0.3537
$$

**Step 5: Stripping operating line**
First compute material balance:

$$
D = F \cdot \frac{x_D - x_B}{x_D - x_B} \text{ (already normalized) — wait, by component balance:}
$$

$$
D = F \cdot \frac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.50 - 0.05}{0.95 - 0.05} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.45}{0.90} = 50 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
B = F - D = 100 - 50 = 50 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
L = R \cdot D = 1.686 \cdot 50 = 84.3 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
V = (R+1) D = 2.686 \cdot 50 = 134.3 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

For $q = 1$ (saturated liquid feed):

$$
\bar{L} = L + qF = 84.3 + 100 = 184.3 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F = 134.3 - 0 = 134.3 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

The stripping operating line passes through $(x_B, x_B) = (0.05, 0.05)$ and has slope $\bar{L}/\bar{V}$:

$$
\frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} = \frac{184.3}{134.3} = 1.372
$$

$$
\text{intercept} = \frac{B x_B}{\bar{V}} = \frac{50 \cdot 0.05}{134.3} = 0.01862
$$

$$
y = 1.372 x - 0.01862
$$

#### Model Solution for (b): MATLAB/SciLab Code Skeleton

```matlab
% IDEAL BINARY DISTILLATION COLUMN SIMULATION USING THOMAS ALGORITHM
clc; clear; close all;

% ---------- INPUTS (KTU 2024 Standard) ----------
F  = 100;          % Feed flow, kmol/h
zF = 0.50;         % Feed composition (benzene mole fraction)
xD = 0.95;         % Distillate composition
xB = 0.05;         % Bottoms composition
alpha = 2.47;      % Relative volatility (benzene/toluene)
q  = 1.0;          % Saturated liquid feed
R  = 1.686;        % Operating reflux ratio
N  = 12;           % Number of stages including reboiler (assumed)

% ---------- MATERIAL BALANCE ----------
D = F*(zF - xB)/(xD - xB);
B = F - D;
L = R*D;
V = (R+1)*D;
Lbar = L + q*F;
Vbar = V - (1-q)*F;

% ---------- INITIAL GUESS ----------
x = zF * ones(N, 1);  % Initial composition profile
x(1) = xD;            % Condenser (stage 1)
x(N) = xB;            % Reboiler (stage N)

% ---------- THOMAS ALGORITHM (tridiagonal solver) ----------
% After substituting y = alpha*x/(1+(alpha-1)*x) into mass balance,
% we obtain a non-linear tridiagonal system. Use Newton iteration.
% At iteration k: Ax = b, where A is tridiagonal.

maxIter = 200;
tol = 1e-9;

for iter = 1:maxIter
    a = zeros(N,1); b = zeros(N,1); c = zeros(N,1); d = zeros(N,1);
    for j = 2:N-1
        % Components for stage j in middle of column
        a(j) = L;
        c(j) = V;
        y_eq = alpha*x(j)/(1 + (alpha-1)*x(j));
        % Numerical derivative of equilibrium w.r.t. x
        dy_dx = alpha / (1 + (alpha-1)*x(j))^2;
        b(j) = -(L + V*dy_dx);
        d(j) = -(L*x(j-1) + V*y_eq - L*x(j) - V*alpha*x(j+1)/(1+(alpha-1)*x(j+1)));
    end
    % Stage 1 (condenser): V*y2 - (L+D)*y1 + L*x0 = 0  with x0=0, y1=y(xD)
    c(1) = V; b(1) = -(V*alpha/(1+(alpha-1)*xD)^2 + D); d(1) = -V*x(2)*alpha/(1+(alpha-1)*x(2)) + D*xD;
    a(1) = 0;
    % Stage N (reboiler): Lbar*x(N-1) - (Lbar - B)*x(N) - B*xB = 0
    a(N) = Lbar; b(N) = -(Lbar - B); d(N) = Lbar*x(N-1) - (Lbar-B)*x(N) - B*xB;
    c(N) = 0;
    
    % Thomas forward sweep
    for j = 2:N
        m = a(j)/b(j-1);
        b(j) = b(j) - m*c(j-1);
        d(j) = d(j) - m*d(j-1);
    end
    % Back substitution
    x_new = zeros(N,1);
    x_new(N) = d(N)/b(N);
    for j = N-1:-1:1
        x_new(j) = (d(j) - c(j)*x_new(j+1))/b(j);
    end
    
    % Convergence check
    err = max(abs(x_new - x));
    x = x_new;
    if err < tol
        fprintf('Converged in %d iterations. Error = %.2e\n', iter, err);
        break;
    end
end

% ---------- POST-PROCESSING ----------
y = alpha*x ./ (1 + (alpha-1)*x);

% ---------- PLOTS ----------
figure;
plot(1:N, x, 'b-o', 1:N, y, 'r-s', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
xlabel('Stage j'); ylabel('Mole fraction');
legend('x_j (Liquid)', 'y_j (Vapor)');
title('Ideal Binary Distillation Column: Composition Profile');
grid on;
```

**Expected Output Plot:** A monotonic decrease in $x_j$ from $x_1 = 0.95$ (condenser) to $x_N = 0.05$ (reboiler), with $y_j$ slightly above $x_j$ at every stage (due to lighter component enriching the vapor phase).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** A binary mixture with $\alpha = 2.0$ is distilled with a saturated vapor feed ($q = 0$) of composition $z_F = 0.40$, distillate $x_D = 0.90$, bottoms $x_B = 0.05$, and operating reflux ratio $R = 2.0$. Using the **McCabe-Thiele method**, determine:
**(i)** The rectifying and stripping operating-line equations.
**(ii)** The minimum number of stages $N_{\min}$ using the Fenske equation.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the use of `ode45` in MATLAB to simulate the **dynamic** start-up of the same distillation column, by formulating the dynamic MESH equations and writing the ODE function file.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline for Q-B:

**(a)(i):** For $q = 0$, the $q$-line is the horizontal line $y = z_F = 0.40$. Operating reflux $R = 2.0$ gives $V = (R+1)D = 3D$ and $L = 2D$.

Rectifying line passes through $(x_D, x_D) = (0.90, 0.90)$ with slope $R/(R+1) = 2/3$:

$$
y = \frac{2}{3} x + \frac{0.90}{3} = 0.6667 x + 0.30
$$

For stripping, compute $D$, $B$, $\bar{V}$, $\bar{L}$ using material balance. With $F = 100$ kmol/h (assume), $D = F(z_F - x_B)/(x_D - x_B) = 100 \cdot 0.35/0.85 = 41.18$ kmol/h, $B = 58.82$ kmol/h. $\bar{V} = V = 3D = 123.5$ (since $q=0$), $\bar{L} = L = 2D = 82.35$. Stripping line:

$$
y = \frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} x - \frac{B x_B}{\bar{V}} = \frac{82.35}{123.5} x - \frac{58.82 \cdot 0.05}{123.5} = 0.6667 x - 0.0238
$$

(Note: with $q=0$ and $R$ matching, rectifying and stripping have same slope, but different intercepts.)

**(a)(ii): Fenske equation:**

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\left[ \left(\frac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right) \left(\frac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right) \right]}{\ln \alpha}
$$

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\left[ \left(\frac{0.90}{0.10}\right) \left(\frac{0.95}{0.05}\right) \right]}{\ln 2.0} = \frac{\ln(9 \cdot 19)}{\ln 2} = \frac{\ln 171}{\ln 2} = \frac{5.142}{0.693} = 7.42
$$

So $N_{\min} = 8$ stages.

**(b): Dynamic MESH for `ode45`**
The dynamic mass balance for stage $j$ with holdup $M_j$ is:

$$
M_j \frac{dx_j}{dt} = L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1} - L x_j - V y_j
$$

The function file is:

```matlab
function dxdt = columnODE(t, x, params)
    % Unpack parameters
    L  = params.L;  V  = params.V;
    Lb = params.Lb; Vb = params.Vb;
    F  = params.F;  zF = params.zF;
    q  = params.q;  alpha = params.alpha;
    M  = params.M;  N = params.N; D = params.D; B = params.B;
    xD = params.xD; xB = params.xB;
    
    dxdt = zeros(N, 1);
    
    % Equilibrium
    y = alpha*x ./ (1 + (alpha-1)*x);
    
    % Stage 1 (condenser)
    dxdt(1) = (V*y(2) - (V)*y(1)) / M(1);   % No L inflow from above
    
    % Middle stages
    for j = 2:N-1
        dxdt(j) = (L*x(j-1) + V*y(j+1) - L*x(j) - V*y(j)) / M(j);
    end
    
    % Stage N (reboiler)
    dxdt(N) = (Lb*x(N-1) - Lb*x(N) + Vb*y(N) - B*xB) / M(N);
end

% Main script
params = struct('L',84.3,'V',134.3,'Lb',184.3,'Vb',134.3,...
                'F',100,'zF',0.40,'q',1,'alpha',2.0,...
                'M',ones(12,1),'N',12,'D',50,'B',50,...
                'xD',0.95,'xB',0.05);
x0 = 0.5*ones(12,1);  % Initial condition
tspan = [0 50];
[t, sol] = ode45(@(t,x) columnODE(t,x,params), tspan, x0);
plot(t, sol); xlabel('Time (h)'); ylabel('x_j(t)');
title('Dynamic Start-up of Ideal Binary Distillation Column');
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the CMO assumption** before writing the simplified operating-line equations. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong sign on the q-line equation** — students often write $y = (q-1)/q \cdot x + z_F/q$ instead of the standard form. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not including the reboiler and condenser as stages** when counting $N$. The total stage count $N$ includes these. (-1 mark)
> 4. **In MATLAB code, not checking convergence** — leaving a `for` loop with no `break` and no tolerance check loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing the feed stage** with the optimal feed stage in McCabe-Thiele — the stage where stepping crosses from rectifying to stripping line. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Mixing up $\bar{L}$ and $L$**, $\bar{V}$ and $V$ in the stripping line — write the section clearly. (-1 mark)


## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**

- [ ] **MESH acronym** = Mass, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat balances.
- [ ] **Ideal column assumptions:** theoretical stages, CMO, binary, constant $\alpha$.
- [ ] **CMO precondition:** equal latent heats, negligible sensible heats and heat of mixing.
- [ ] **Rectifying operating line:** $y_{j+1} = [R/(R+1)] x_j + [x_D/(R+1)]$, valid for stages 1 to $f-1$.
- [ ] **Stripping operating line:** $y_{j+1} = [\bar{L}/\bar{V}] x_j - [B x_B / \bar{V}]$, valid for stages $f+1$ to $N$.
- [ ] **q-line equation:** $y = [q/(q-1)] x - [z_F/(q-1)]$, passes through $(z_F, z_F)$.
- [ ] **Five standard feed conditions:** saturated liquid ($q=1$), saturated vapor ($q=0$), subcooled liquid ($q>1$), superheated vapor ($q<0$), two-phase feed ($0<q<1$).
- [ ] **Equilibrium relation (binary, constant $\alpha$):** $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha-1)x]$.
- [ ] **Fenske equation:** $N_{\min} = \ln\{ [x_D/(1-x_D)] \cdot [(1-x_B)/x_B] \} / \ln \alpha$.
- [ ] **Underwood equation for $R_{\min}$:** requires iteration if $q \ne 0, 1$.
- [ ] **Gilliland correlation:** links $N, N_{\min}, R, R_{\min}$ via $(X, Y)$ empirical curve.
- [ ] **Material balance identities:** $F = D + B$, $F z_F = D x_D + B x_B$, $V = (R+1)D$.
- [ ] **Tridiagonal matrix form** is the natural form of MESH after substitution; solved by Thomas algorithm in $O(N)$.
- [ ] **Dynamic MESH for `ode45`:** convert algebraic equilibrium into time derivative using holdup $M_j$.
- [ ] **MATLAB functions for column simulation:** `ode45` (dynamic), `fsolve` (steady-state), `ode15s` (stiff).
- [ ] **Numbering convention:** Stage 1 = total condenser (top), Stage $N$ = partial reboiler (bottom), Feed at stage $f$.
- [ ] **Always validate** by checking that $\sum x_j = 1$ at every stage (binary: $x_j + x_{j,\text{heavy}} = 1$).
- [ ] **Stages in McCabe-Thiele count** = number of steps between $x_B$ and $x_D$ on the $y$–$x$ diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacketed Tubular Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Jacketed Tubular Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Jacketed Tubular Reactor (JTR)** is a continuous-flow, non-adiabatic chemical reactor in which a reaction mixture flows through the **annular space (or inner tube)** while a heating/cooling utility fluid flows through an **external concentric jacket** surrounding the tube. Heat is transferred across the tube wall, characterized by an **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**, allowing precise temperature control of an exothermic or endothermic reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Process Modelling & Simulation, the JTR is treated as the canonical *distributed-parameter* coupled system — two interacting ODEs/PDEs (one for the reacting fluid, one for the jacket fluid) that must be solved simultaneously.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Think of the JTR like a **double-walled drinking glass (a thermos flask)**, but with liquid chemicals flowing through both walls in controlled directions:
- **Inner wall (tube):** where the chemical reaction happens — like hot coffee inside the flask.
- **Outer wall (jacket):** where cold water circulates — like the cool outer surface that absorbs heat.
- **Direction of flow:** The cold water can flow in the **same direction as the coffee (co-current)** or in the **opposite direction (counter-current)** — just like a heat exchanger.

This is exactly how industrial **polymerization reactors, tubular reformers, and pharmaceutical flow reactors** are designed for safe, isothermal (or controlled-temperature) operation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Constants

- **Overall heat transfer coefficient:** typically **U = 50 – 1500 W/m²·K**
- **Wall thermal conductivity (steel):** k_w ≈ **45 W/m·K**
- **Specific heat of water (jacket fluid):** C_p,j ≈ **4180 J/kg·K**
- **Characteristic residence time:** τ = L/u ≈ **10 – 600 s**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Damköhler Number (Da):** A dimensionless group that compares reaction rate to convection rate. For JTR: $Da = \dfrac{k\,L}{u}$. When $Da \gg 1$, reaction is fast and jacket cooling must be strong to prevent runaway.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modelling Philosophy: Why and How

The JTR is modelled as **two coupled energy balances** interacting through a heat-transfer term. The model captures:

1. **Reaction heat generation** along the tube length (z-direction).
2. **Heat removal** to the jacket through the tube wall.
3. **Jacket fluid temperature change** as it absorbs/releases heat.
4. **Convective transport** in both streams (flow direction matters).

**Why is the JTR important?**
- Used for **highly exothermic reactions** where temperature runaway can cause explosions (e.g., nitration, polymerization).
- Critical for **selectivity control** in consecutive reactions.
- Forms the basis for **model-predictive control (MPC)** of industrial reactors.

### 2.2 Modelling Assumptions

| # | Assumption | Justification |
|---|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Plug flow in tube and jacket | High Reynolds number ($Re > 10^4$) |
| 2 | Constant physical properties ($\rho, C_p, k$) | Moderate $\Delta T$ |
| 3 | Negligible axial conduction in tube | High Peclet number |
| 4 | Uniform wall temperature | Thin, high-k tube wall |
| 5 | Single overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ | Lumped wall resistance |
| 6 | First-order or pseudo-first-order kinetics | Standard KTU assumption |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | $u\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = -k\,C_A$ | Mass balance (steady-state PFR) |
| 2 | $k = A\,e^{-E/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate law |
| 3 | $\rho\,C_p\,u\,\dfrac{dT}{dz} = (-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A - \dfrac{4U}{D}(T - T_j)$ | Reactor energy balance (co-current) |
| 4 | $\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,\dfrac{dT_j}{dz} = +\dfrac{4U\,D}{D_o^2 - D_i^2}(T - T_j)$ | Jacket energy balance (**co-current**) |
| 5 | $\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,\dfrac{dT_j}{dz} = -\dfrac{4U\,D}{D_o^2 - D_i^2}(T - T_j)$ | Jacket energy balance (**counter-current**) |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} + u\,\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial z} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A}{\rho\,C_p} - \dfrac{4U}{\rho\,C_p\,D}(T - T_j)$ | **Dynamic** reactor energy balance |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)\,C_{A0}}{\rho\,C_p}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta T_{ad}\,C_{A0}}{T_0}\,\dfrac{E}{R\,T_0}$ | Arrhenius sensitivity parameter |
| 9 | $St = \dfrac{U}{\rho\,C_p\,u}$ | Stanton number (heat transfer) |
| 10 | $NTU = \dfrac{U\,A_{wall}}{(\rho\,C_p\,u)_j}$ | Number of Transfer Units (jacket) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** In **counter-current** operation, the jacket energy equation has a **negative sign** because the coolant *enters* at $z = L$ and *exits* at $z = 0$ when the reactor coordinate $z$ is measured from inlet to outlet. Many KTU students lose marks by missing this sign.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymerization (LDPE, PVC):** JTRs with counter-current cooling maintain tight temperature control to control molecular weight distribution.
- **Pharmaceutical flow chemistry:** Enables safe scale-up of exothermic reactions.
- **Petrochemical reformers:** Steam-heated jackets supply endothermic heat for naphtha cracking.
- **Food industry:** Pasteurization in scraped-surface JTRs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Reactor Energy Balance

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume:**
Consider an annular slice of the reactor tube at position $z$ with thickness $\Delta z$.
- Cross-sectional area of tube: $A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Wall heat-transfer area: $A_w = \pi D \Delta z$

**Step 2 — Write the General Energy Balance:**

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Convection in} - \text{Convection out} + \text{Generation} - \text{Transfer to jacket}$$

At steady state, accumulation = 0:

$$0 = \rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c\,T\,\big\vert_z - \rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c\,T\,\big\vert_{z+\Delta z} + (-\Delta H_r)\,r_A\,A_c\,\Delta z - U\,\pi D\,(T - T_j)\,\Delta z$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $\Delta z$ and take limit as $\Delta z \to 0$:**

The convection term becomes a derivative:

$$\lim_{\Delta z \to 0}\frac{T\,\big\vert_z - T\,\big\vert_{z+\Delta z}}{\Delta z} = -\frac{dT}{dz}$$

Substituting:

$$0 = -\rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c\,\frac{dT}{dz} + (-\Delta H_r)\,r_A\,A_c - U\,\pi D\,(T - T_j)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to standard form:**

Divide throughout by $A_c = \pi D^2/4$:

$$\rho\,C_p\,u\,\frac{dT}{dz} = (-\Delta H_r)\,r_A - \frac{4U}{D}(T - T_j)$$

This is the **canonical JTR reactor energy equation** (co-current version; sign of jacket transfer term is **always positive** in the reactor equation regardless of flow direction).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Jacket Energy Balance (Counter-Current Case)

**Step 1 — Control Volume in the Jacket Annulus:**

For the jacket annulus, the flow area is:

$$A_j = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$$

**Step 2 — Coordinate Convention for Counter-Current:**

In counter-current flow, the jacket fluid enters at $z = L$ and leaves at $z = 0$. The convective flux in the $+z$ direction is therefore **negative** of the actual mass flow.

Mass flux entering slice at $z+\Delta z$: $-\rho_j\,u_j\,A_j\,T_j\,\big\vert_{z+\Delta z}$ (negative because of reversed flow)

**Step 3 — Energy Balance on Jacket Slice:**

$$0 = -\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,A_j\,T_j\,\big\vert_{z+\Delta z} - \left[-\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,A_j\,T_j\,\big\vert_z\right] + U\,\pi D\,(T - T_j)\,\Delta z$$

Simplify and divide by $\Delta z$:

$$0 = +\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,A_j\,\frac{dT_j}{dz} + U\,\pi D\,(T - T_j)$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $A_j$ to obtain:**

$$\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,\frac{dT_j}{dz} = -\frac{4U\,D}{D_o^2 - D_i^2}(T - T_j)$$

The **negative sign** confirms counter-current operation.

### 3.3 Complete Coupled System (Steady State, First-Order Reaction)

Combining mass, reactor energy, and jacket energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Mass:} \quad & u\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} = -k\,C_A, \qquad C_A(0) = C_{A0} \\
\text{(ii) Reactor Energy:} \quad & \rho\,C_p\,u\,\frac{dT}{dz} = (-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A - \frac{4U}{D}(T - T_j), \qquad T(0) = T_0 \\
\text{(iii) Jacket Energy:} \quad & \rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,\frac{dT_j}{dz} = -\frac{4U\,D}{D_o^2 - D_i^2}(T - T_j), \qquad T_j(L) = T_{j,L}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **Two-Point Boundary Value Problem (TPBVP)** — three ODEs with mixed boundary conditions at $z = 0$ and $z = L$. It is solved numerically using:
- **Shooting Method** (for simple cases),
- **Collocation / Finite Difference** (for stiff systems),
- **MATLAB `bvp4c`** or **Python `solve_bvp`** (standard KTU tool).

### 3.4 Dimensionless Form (Analytical Insight)

Introduce dimensionless variables:
- $\theta = (T - T_0)/\Delta T_{ad}$, where $\Delta T_{ad} = (-\Delta H_r)\,C_{A0}/(\rho\,C_p)$
- $\theta_j = (T_j - T_{j,0})/\Delta T_{ad}$
- $x = C_A/C_{A0}$, $\zeta = z/L$

The system becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx}{d\zeta} &= -Da\,x \\
\frac{d\theta}{d\zeta} &= Da\,x - N_{cool}(\theta - \theta_j) \\
\frac{d\theta_j}{d\zeta} &= -\frac{N_{cool}}{\eta}(\theta - \theta_j) \quad \text{(counter-current)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $Da = k\,L/u$ — Damköhler number
- $N_{cool} = 4\,U\,L/(\rho\,C_p\,u\,D)$ — cooling intensity
- $\eta = (\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,A_j)/(\rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c)$ — capacity flow ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Temperature runaway occurs when $N_{cool} < \beta$ (where $\beta$ is the Arrhenius sensitivity). For safe design, **always ensure $N_{cool} \geq 2\beta$**.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Steady-State Outlet Temperature)

**Given:** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ in a JTR.
- $L = 5$ m, $D = 0.05$ m, $D_o = 0.10$ m
- $u = 0.005$ m/s, $u_j = 0.01$ m/s
- $C_{A0} = 1000$ mol/m³, $T_0 = 350$ K, $T_{j,L} = 300$ K (counter-current inlet)
- $k = 0.01\,e^{8000(1/350 - 1/T)}$ s⁻¹ (Arrhenius, $E/R = 8000$ K)
- $(-\Delta H_r) = 50{,}000$ J/mol, $\rho = C_p = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4180$ J/kg·K
- $U = 500$ W/m²·K

**Step 1 — Calculate Damköhler number:**
Residence time: $\tau = L/u = 5/0.005 = 1000$ s.
Assume average $T \approx 350$ K, so $k \approx 0.01$ s⁻¹.
$Da = k\,L/u = 0.01 \times 1000 = 10$.

**Step 2 — Concentration profile (analytical for first order):**

$$C_A(z) = C_{A0}\,e^{-k\,z/u}$$

At outlet: $x_A = C_{A,L}/C_{A0} = e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5}$ → **essentially full conversion**.

**Step 3 — Energy balance check (assuming isothermal at 350 K):**

Heat generated: $Q_{gen} = (-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_{A0}\,\tau = 50{,}000 \times 0.01 \times 1000 \times 1000 = 5.0 \times 10^{8}$ W/m³·... (per unit volume integrated).

Heat removed: $Q_{rem} = U\,A_w\,\Delta T_{lm}$ — for design, choose $U$ such that $T_{max} \leq 380$ K.

This confirms that the **cooling duty** must match the **heat release rate** for stable operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Jacketed Tubular Reactor

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **physical flow topology and control interactions** of an industrial JTR. It maps every stream, every energy interaction, and every feedback loop, replacing the need for a complex cross-sectional drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Stream\nCA0 TA0] --> B[Reactor Tube\nPFR Element]
    B --> C[Product Stream\nCAL TAL]
    D[Coolant Inlet\nTj0] --> E[Jacket Annulus\nHeat Exchanger]
    E --> F[Coolant Outlet\nTjL]
    B -.Heat Transfer U.-> E
    E -.Heat Feedback.-> B

    G[Temperature Sensor\nT_z at outlet] -.-> H[Controller PID]
    H -.Valve Position.-> I[Jacket Flow Valve]
    I -.Flowrate.-> D

    J[Concentration Sensor\nCA_z] -.-> K[Reactor Model\nDistributed ODE]
    K -.Realtime Prediction.-> H

    classDef stream fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000;
    classDef unit fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000;
    classDef control fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000;
    class A,C,D,F stream;
    class B,E unit;
    class G,H,I,J,K control;
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Co-current vs Counter-current Flow Paths

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COCURRENT ["Co-current Operation"]
        direction LR
        A1[Reactor Inlet z=0] --> A2[Reactor Outlet z=L]
        B1[Jacket Inlet z=0] --> B2[Jacket Outlet z=L]
        A1 --- B1
        A2 --- B2
    end

    subgraph COUNTERCURRENT ["Counter-current Operation"]
        direction LR
        C1[Reactor Inlet z=0] --> C2[Reactor Outlet z=L]
        D2[Jacket Inlet z=L] --> D1[Jacket Outlet z=0]
        C1 --- D1
        C2 --- D2
    end

    classDef cocur fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000;
    classDef ctrcur fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000;
    class A1,A2,B1,B2 cocur;
    class C1,C2,D1,D2 ctrcur;
```

### 4.3 Schematic of the Coupled ODE Solution Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem Definition:\nReactor + Jacket geometry] --> Q[Write Mass Balance ODE]
    Q --> R[Write Reactor Energy ODE]
    R --> S[Write Jacket Energy ODE]
    S --> T{Counter-current?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Negative sign in jacket ODE]
    T -- No --> V[Positive sign in jacket ODE]
    U --> W[Apply Boundary Conditions:\n3 ODEs mixed at z=0 and z=L]
    V --> W
    W --> X[Numerical Solver:\nbvp4c or solve_bvp]
    X --> Y[Plot T z and Tj z profiles]
    Y --> Z[Compute Conversion XA and Hot Spot Th]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a jacketed tubular reactor. State any **four modelling assumptions** used in deriving its energy balance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A jacketed tubular reactor is a continuous-flow reactor in which the reaction mixture flows through an inner tube surrounded by an annular jacket through which a heating or cooling fluid circulates, enabling controlled heat transfer across the tube wall.

**Four modelling assumptions (½ mark each):**
1. **Plug flow** in both tube and jacket (radial gradients neglected).
2. **Constant physical properties** ($\rho, C_p, k$) over the operating range.
3. **Negligible axial heat conduction** (high Peclet number).
4. **Single overall heat transfer coefficient U** (lumped wall resistance).
5. **Uniform radial temperature** within the tube.
6. **Negligible heat loss** to surroundings.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **co-current** and **counter-current** jacket flow arrangements in a JTR. Mention **one advantage** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Co-current | Counter-current |
|--------|------------|-----------------|
| Flow direction | Both streams flow in the same direction | Streams flow in opposite directions |
| Jacket energy ODE sign | Positive in $dT_j/dz$ term | Negative in $dT_j/dz$ term |
| Log-mean temperature | Smaller driving force at one end | Larger and more uniform driving force |
| Boundary condition | $T_j(0) = T_{j,0}$ | $T_j(L) = T_{j,0}$ |

**Advantages (½ mark each):**
- **Co-current:** Lower jacket outlet temperature → safer for highly exothermic reactions near the inlet.
- **Counter-current:** Higher mean temperature driving force → smaller jacket area required → **preferred industrially**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Develop the **steady-state mathematical model** for a non-isothermal PFR with a counter-current cooling jacket carrying out the reaction $A \to B$ (first-order, exothermic). Clearly state the governing ODEs and boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mass Balance** (1 mark):

$$u\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} = -k\,C_A, \qquad C_A(0) = C_{A0}$$

**Reactor Energy Balance** (3 marks):

At steady state, for a differential element $\Delta z$:

$$\rho\,C_p\,u\,\frac{dT}{dz} = (-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A - \frac{4U}{D}(T - T_j), \qquad T(0) = T_0$$

Derivation:
- Energy in at $z$: $\rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c\,T$
- Energy out at $z+\Delta z$: $\rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c\,(T + \dfrac{dT}{dz}\Delta z)$
- Generation: $(-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A\,A_c\,\Delta z$
- Heat to jacket: $U\,\pi D\,(T - T_j)\,\Delta z$

Equating and dividing by $A_c\,\Delta z$:

$$\rho\,C_p\,u\,\frac{dT}{dz} = (-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A - \frac{4U}{D}(T - T_j)$$

**Jacket Energy Balance (Counter-current)** (3 marks):

For the jacket annulus with reversed flow:

$$\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,\frac{dT_j}{dz} = -\frac{4U\,D}{D_o^2 - D_i^2}(T - T_j), \qquad T_j(L) = T_{j,L}$$

**Summary of Boundary Value Problem** (built into above): The system has three ODEs with mixed boundary conditions at $z = 0$ (mass, reactor temperature) and $z = L$ (jacket inlet temperature).

---

**(b)** A liquid-phase first-order exothermic reaction is carried out in a jacketed tubular reactor operating at steady state with counter-current cooling. Given: $L = 4$ m, $D = 0.04$ m, $u = 0.002$ m/s, $C_{A0} = 800$ mol/m³, $T_0 = 340$ K, $T_{j,L} = 290$ K, $k_{ref} = 0.005$ s⁻¹ at 340 K with $E/R = 7500$ K, $(-\Delta H_r) = 60{,}000$ J/mol, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4000$ J/kg·K, $U = 400$ W/m²·K.

Calculate:
(i) The **Damköhler number** and **conversion** at the reactor outlet assuming isothermal operation at 340 K.
(ii) The **adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{ad}$.
(iii) The **cooling parameter** $N_{cool} = 4UL/(\rho\,C_p\,u\,D)$. Comment on whether the reactor is safe from runaway. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Damköhler number and conversion (2 marks):**

Residence time:

$$\tau = \frac{L}{u} = \frac{4}{0.002} = 2000 \text{ s}$$

Damköhler number:

$$Da = k\,\tau = 0.005 \times 2000 = 10$$

Conversion (for first-order, isothermal):

$$X_A = 1 - e^{-Da} = 1 - e^{-10} \approx 0.99995 \approx 100\%$$

**(ii) Adiabatic temperature rise (2 marks):**

$$\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,C_{A0}}{\rho\,C_p} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 800}{1000 \times 4000} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^7}{4 \times 10^6} = 12 \text{ K}$$

**(iii) Cooling parameter and safety comment (3 marks):**

$$N_{cool} = \frac{4\,U\,L}{\rho\,C_p\,u\,D} = \frac{4 \times 400 \times 4}{1000 \times 4000 \times 0.002 \times 0.04} = \frac{6400}{320} = 20$$

Arrhenius sensitivity:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta T_{ad}}{T_0} \cdot \frac{E}{R\,T_0} = \frac{12}{340} \cdot \frac{7500}{340} \approx 0.0353 \times 22.06 \approx 0.78$$

Safety criterion: $N_{cool} = 20 \gg 2\beta \approx 1.56$. Hence the reactor is **safe from runaway** by a wide margin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the **unsteady-state (dynamic) energy balance** for both the reacting fluid and the jacket fluid in a jacketed tubular reactor. Discuss why this model is essential for **safe start-up and shutdown** procedures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Reactor Dynamic Energy Balance (4 marks):**

For an unsteady-state system, accumulation term $\rho\,C_p\,A_c\,\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t}\,\Delta z$ must be retained:

$$\rho\,C_p\,A_c\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\,\Delta z = \rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c\,T\,\big\vert_z - \rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c\,T\,\big\vert_{z+\Delta z} + (-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A\,A_c\,\Delta z - U\,\pi D\,(T - T_j)\,\Delta z$$

Dividing by $A_c\,\Delta z$ and taking limits:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + u\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial z} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A}{\rho\,C_p} - \frac{4U}{\rho\,C_p\,D}(T - T_j)$$

Initial condition: $T(z,0) = T_{init}(z)$; Boundary condition: $T(0,t) = T_0(t)$.

**Jacket Dynamic Energy Balance (2 marks):**

For counter-current jacket:

$$\frac{\partial T_j}{\partial t} + u_j\,\frac{\partial T_j}{\partial z} = -\frac{4U\,D}{(D_o^2 - D_i^2)\,\rho_j\,C_{pj}}(T - T_j)$$

Initial condition: $T_j(z,0) = T_{j,init}(z)$; Boundary: $T_j(L,t) = T_{j,in}(t)$.

**Engineering Significance for Safe Operation (1 mark):**

During **start-up**, reaction rates accelerate rapidly while jacket fluid is still cold; the dynamic model predicts **hot-spot formation** and ensures operators pre-cool the jacket sufficiently. During **shutdown**, residual heat from exothermic reactions must be safely removed — again requiring the dynamic model for safe coast-down temperature trajectories.

---

**(b)** Using a **dimensional analysis** approach, derive the key dimensionless groups governing the steady-state behaviour of a JTR. Briefly explain how each group influences the reactor performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The governing equations have five fundamental dimensions: length [L], time [t], mass [m], temperature [T], and amount [N]. Using these, **three key dimensionless groups** emerge (7 marks split: 3 marks for derivation, 4 marks for physical interpretation):

**1. Damköhler Number — $Da = k\,L/u$ (2 marks for derivation):**

Normalizing the energy balance by dividing by $u/T_0$ and the concentration by $C_{A0}$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{d\zeta} = Da\,x - N_{cool}(\theta - \theta_j)$$

$Da$ compares **reaction rate** to **convection rate**.
- $Da \ll 1$: Reaction too slow, low conversion.
- $Da \gg 1$: Reaction fast, high conversion, risk of hot-spot.

**2. Cooling Intensity — $N_{cool} = 4\,U\,L/(\rho\,C_p\,u\,D)$ (2 marks):**

Dimensionless heat removal capacity. **Higher $N_{cool}$** means stronger cooling → better temperature control → lower hot-spot temperature.

**3. Capacity Flow Ratio — $\eta = (\rho_j\,C_{pj}\,u_j\,A_j)/(\rho\,C_p\,u\,A_c)$ (1 mark):**

Ratio of jacket to reactor thermal capacity rates. Determines how much the jacket fluid temperature changes along the reactor.
- $\eta \ll 1$: Jacket temperature nearly constant (large utility flow).
- $\eta \gg 1$: Jacket heats up significantly (small utility flow) → poor temperature control.

**4. Arrhenius Sensitivity — $\beta = (E/RT_0)\,(C_{A0}(-\Delta H_r))/(\rho\,C_p\,T_0)$ (2 marks):**

Combines thermodynamics and kinetics. **Runaway criterion:** $N_{cool} < \beta$ → unsafe.

**Performance Summary Table:**

| Dimensionless Group | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on JTR |
|---------------------|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Damköhler | $Da$ | $0.1 - 50$ | Conversion |
| Cooling Intensity | $N_{cool}$ | $1 - 100$ | Hot-spot temperature |
| Capacity Ratio | $\eta$ | $0.1 - 5$ | Jacket $\Delta T$ |
| Arrhenius Sensitivity | $\beta$ | $0.1 - 20$ | Runaway potential |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Sign Error in Counter-current ODE (3 marks lost):** Most students forget the negative sign in the jacket energy balance for counter-current flow. Always re-derive or check physically: coolant entering at high $z$ loses heat as it moves to low $z$, so $T_j$ decreases with $z$ → negative $dT_j/dz$.
> 2. **Confusion between Reactor and Jacket heat-transfer areas (2 marks):** The reactor uses $4U/D$ (per unit cross-sectional area $A_c$), while the jacket uses $4UD/(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$ (per unit jacket area $A_j$). These are different and must not be mixed up.
> 3. **Forgetting Boundary Conditions (1–2 marks):** Always state $C_A(0) = C_{A0}$, $T(0) = T_0$, and the jacket inlet condition at the correct end ($z = 0$ for co-current, $z = L$ for counter-current).
> 4. **Skipping Steady-State Assumption:** If the question says "dynamic" or "transient", the partial time derivative $\partial T/\partial t$ must be included.
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** Always carry SI units in derivations (W, J, mol, K, m, s) — mixing CGS/English loses marks.
> 6. **Not commenting on Runaway Safety:** In numerical problems, examiners expect a one-line conclusion on whether $N_{cool} > 2\beta$ for safe operation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **JTR = Distributed-parameter coupled system** (PFR + heat exchanger).
>
> ✅ **Three coupled ODEs:** mass, reactor energy, jacket energy.
>
> ✅ **Reactor energy ODE always has $-4U/D\,(T - T_j)$** (regardless of flow direction).
>
> ✅ **Jacket energy ODE sign = + for co-current, − for counter-current.**
>
> ✅ **TPBVP:** Boundary conditions split between $z = 0$ and $z = L$ — solved by `bvp4c` / `solve_bvp`.
>
> ✅ **Key dimensionless numbers:** $Da$, $N_{cool}$, $\eta$, $\beta$.
>
> ✅ **Runaway criterion:** $N_{cool} > 2\beta$ for safe operation.
>
> ✅ **Dynamic model uses partial derivatives** $\partial T/\partial t + u\,\partial T/\partial z$.
>
> ✅ **Counter-current preferred industrially** (uniform $\Delta T_{lm}$).
>
> ✅ **Assumptions:** Plug flow, constant properties, lumped $U$, no axial conduction.
>
> ✅ **Typical applications:** Polymerization, nitration, flow chemistry, reformer tubes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counter-Current Liquid-Liquid Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Counter-Current Liquid-Liquid Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Counter-Current Liquid-Liquid Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger (CCLDPHE):** A double-pipe (concentric pipe) heat exchanger in which the **hot liquid stream** and the **cold liquid stream** flow in **opposite axial directions** through the annular and inner pipe regions, respectively, enabling continuous heat transfer across the cylindrical wall separating the two streams. The governing design parameters are the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**, the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**, the heat transfer area **A**, and the **Capacity Rate Ratio (C_r)**. Mathematically, the steady-state energy transfer is expressed as: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$ where $\Delta T_{LM}$ is the counter-current **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** evaluated at the two ends of the exchanger. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Way Escalator in a Mall:** > Imagine a shopping mall where customers moving **UP** on one escalator (the hot stream losing heat) and customers moving **DOWN** on the parallel escalator (the cold stream gaining heat) are crossing paths. Because they are traveling in **opposite directions**, at every single floor (every differential slice dA), the temperature difference between the upward and downward crowd remains reasonably high. This is **counter-current flow**. > > If both escalators went **UP** (co-current), the crowd temperatures would quickly meet in the middle, and the temperature difference would collapse — meaning less heat transfer. The opposite-direction arrangement is therefore the **most thermally efficient** arrangement for liquid-liquid heat exchangers. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Engineering Metrics - **Thermal conductivity of typical carbon steel pipe wall:** $k \approx 45\ \text{W/(m·K)}$ - **Thermal conductivity of typical stainless steel pipe wall:** $k \approx 16\ \text{W/(m·K)}$ - **Standard specific heat of water:** $C_p \approx 4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ - **Standard specific heat of typical organic liquids:** $C_p \approx 1.5 - 2.5\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ - **Typical U values for liquid-liquid double-pipe exchangers:** $U \approx 150 - 1500\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ - **Standard inlet/outlet pressure range:** $1 - 10\ \text{bar (gauge)}$ > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In double-pipe exchangers, the inner pipe carries one fluid, and the annulus (the space between the inner pipe outer wall and the outer pipe inner wall) carries the other fluid. The thin cylindrical wall is the **dominant thermal resistance** for liquid-liquid systems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Hot fluid entry:** The hot liquid enters at one end of the exchanger (say, the right end) with inlet temperature $T_{h,i}$ and mass flow rate $m_h$. 2. **Cold fluid entry (opposite end):** The cold liquid enters at the left end with inlet temperature $T_{c,i}$ and mass flow rate $m_c$, moving in the **opposite direction**. 3. **Wall-mediated conduction:** Heat flows radially outward (or inward) through the cylindrical pipe wall, which is characterized by thermal conductivity $k$ and thickness $x$. 4. **Convective boundary layers:** Inside the pipe, a thermal boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ forms. The fluid-side convection coefficients are $h_i$ (inner pipe fluid) and $h_o$ (annulus fluid). 5. **Temperature profile:** The hot stream temperature decreases monotonically along its flow direction; the cold stream temperature increases monotonically in its flow direction. The two profiles **cross** each other if the streams are not balanced. 6. **Steady-state assumption:** Accumulation of energy inside the control volume is zero; energy in = energy out. ### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Design Equations - **Why counter-current?** Because the temperature difference between the two fluids at the **cold-end** (where the cold fluid exits) can be maintained larger than in co-current flow, the **LMTD is larger** for the same inlet/outlet conditions, meaning **more heat transfer per unit area**. - **How is LMTD derived?** By integrating a differential energy balance over the length of the exchanger, which yields a logarithmic mean rather than an arithmetic mean (because temperature profiles are exponential in nature). - **Why use the LMTD method?** It allows decoupling the heat transfer rate $Q$ from the temperatures, and uses an experimentally measurable $U$ (overall heat transfer coefficient) that accounts for **all resistances** in series. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description / Application | |---|----------|--------------------------| | 1 | $Q = m_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = m_c C_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ | Steady-state energy balance on both streams | | 2 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ | Core design equation (LMTD method) | | 3 | $\Delta T_{LM,CC} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-current LMTD | | 4 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o};\quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ | End-point temperature differences (CC) | | 5 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (thin wall) | | 6 | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}}$ | Heat capacity rate ratio (0 < $C_r \le 1$) | | 7 | $C_{\min} = \min(m_h C_{p,h},\ m_c C_{p,c})$ | Minimum capacity rate | | 8 | $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{\min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units (dimensionless) | | 9 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$ | Effectiveness | | 10 | $\varepsilon_{CC} = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\big[-NTU(1 - C_r)\big]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\big[-NTU(1 - C_r)\big]}$ | Counter-current effectiveness | | 11 | $\varepsilon_{CC} = \dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$ when $C_r = 1$ | Balanced streams special case | | 12 | $\Delta P_f = 4 f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Fanning-friction pressure drop | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL UNITS CHECK:** If $U$ is in $\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, and $\Delta T_{LM}$ in K (or °C, since these are intervals), then $Q$ comes out in **watts (W)**. For $Q$ in **kW**, divide by 1000. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Counter-current liquid-liquid double-pipe exchangers are extensively used in: - **Petrochemical refineries** — pre-heating crude oil using hot diesel fractions. - **Dairy and food industries** — pasteurization and milk cooling. - **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** — temperature control of solvent mixtures. - **Solar thermal plants** — pre-heating thermal oil using hot water. - **Process cooling** — cooling of organic solvents using chilled water. Their simplicity, ease of cleaning, and ability to handle viscous slurries make them ideal for **small-to-medium scale** heat duties (typically $Q \le 500\ \text{kW}$). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Balance on a Differential Element Consider a differential heat transfer area $dA$ at axial position $x$ from the cold-fluid inlet (see SECTION 4 for schematic). **Step 1:** The rate of heat transferred from the hot fluid to the cold fluid across $dA$ is: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$ **Step 2:** For the hot fluid, this heat loss corresponds to a temperature drop: $$dQ = -m_h C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$$ The negative sign indicates $T_h$ decreases as $x$ increases along the hot-fluid direction. **Step 3:** For the cold fluid, this heat gain corresponds to a temperature rise (note: in counter-current, the cold fluid moves in the $-x$ direction, so $dT_c/dx$ is negative relative to its own flow direction, but positive relative to the hot-fluid coordinate): $$dQ = m_c C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$ **Step 4:** Define the capacity rates: $$C_h = m_h C_{p,h},\qquad C_c = m_c C_{p,c}$$ **Step 5:** Divide the two energy equations to eliminate $dQ$: $$U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c) = -C_h \cdot dT_h = C_c \cdot dT_c$$ **Step 6:** Take the differential of $(T_h - T_c)$: $$d(T_h - T_c) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)}{C_h} - \frac{U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)}{C_c}$$ $$d(T_h - T_c) = -U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$ **Step 7:** Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Then: $$\frac{d\Delta T}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) dA$$ **Step 8:** Integrate from the hot-fluid inlet ($A = 0$, $\Delta T = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$) to the hot-fluid outlet ($A = A$, $\Delta T = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$): $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d\Delta T}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) \int_0^A dA$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$ **Step 9:** Solve for $Q$. Using $Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})$ and $Q = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$, we have $\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o} + T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$. **Step 10:** Substitute back: $$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -\frac{U A (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{Q}$$ **Step 11:** Rearrange to obtain the **LMTD form**: $$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = U A \cdot \Delta T_{LM,CC}$$ This is the **master counter-current heat exchanger equation**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Counter-Current Effectiveness **Step 1:** Define the dimensionless maximum possible heat transfer: $$Q_{max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$ **Step 2:** Define effectiveness: $$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{Q_{max}}$$ **Step 3:** Express $Q$ in terms of effectiveness and the capacity rates. Let $T_{h,i} - T_{c,i} = \Delta T_{max}$. **Step 4:** Using $Q = C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ and $\varepsilon = Q/(C_{\min}\Delta T_{max})$: $$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \varepsilon \frac{C_{\min}}{C_h} \Delta T_{max}$$ $$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \varepsilon \frac{C_{\min}}{C_c} \Delta T_{max}$$ **Step 5:** Compute $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$: $$\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_{max}\left(1 - \varepsilon \frac{C_{\min}}{C_c}\right)$$ $$\Delta T_2 = \Delta T_{max}\left(1 - \varepsilon \frac{C_{\min}}{C_h}\right)$$ **Step 6:** Substitute into the LMTD master equation $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ with $Q = \varepsilon C_{\min} \Delta T_{max}$: $$\varepsilon C_{\min} \Delta T_{max} = \frac{U A}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \cdot \Delta T_{max}\left[\varepsilon \frac{C_{\min}}{C_c} - \varepsilon \frac{C_{\min}}{C_h}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$ **Step 7:** After algebraic manipulation (see textbook derivations) with $NTU = U A / C_{\min}$ and $C_r = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$: $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{CC} = \frac{1 - \exp\!\big[-NTU(1 - C_r)\big]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\big[-NTU(1 - C_r)\big]}}$$ **Step 8 — Special Case ($C_r = 1$):** Apply L'Hôpital's rule as $C_r \to 1$: $$\varepsilon_{CC} = \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** The effectiveness-NTU method is **preferred** when outlet temperatures are unknown but the heat exchanger area and flow rates are known. The LMTD method is **preferred** when all four end temperatures are specified. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CC vs. Co-Current | Property | Counter-Current (CC) | Co-Current (CoC) | |----------|---------------------|------------------| | Maximum possible heat transfer | $C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ | $C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ | | Outlet temperature crossing | **Allowed** (if $C_{\min}$ is hot stream) | **Not allowed** | | $\Delta T_{LM}$ for same $A$, $U$ | **Larger** | Smaller | | Effectiveness at same NTU | **Higher** | Lower | | Suitable for viscous fluids | Yes (uniform $\Delta T$ profile) | Less preferred | | Typical $\varepsilon$ range | $0.5 - 0.95$ | $0.3 - 0.6$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** In counter-current flow, the **cold-stream outlet temperature can exceed the hot-stream outlet temperature** if the hot stream has the smaller capacity rate. This is **thermodynamically valid** and is the key advantage of counter-current configuration. Students often mistakenly flag this as an error. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid) The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the counter-current heat exchanger modelling workflow — how the inlet boundary conditions, differential element, and outlet stream properties interact. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Hot Inlet
Th_i, mh, Cph"] --> B["Differential
Element dA"] C["Cold Inlet
Tc_i, mc, Cpc"] --> D["Differential
Element dA"] B --> E["Hot Outlet
Th_o, mh, Cph"] D --> F["Cold Outlet
Tc_o, mc, Cpc"] B <-->|"Q = U dA (Th - Tc)"| D G["Overall U
1/U = 1/hi + x/k + 1/ho"] --> B G --> D H["Boundary
Conditions"] --> A H --> C I["Solver:
LMTD or
e-NTU"] --> B I --> D E --> J["Output:
Q, To_h, To_c"] F --> J ``` ### 4.2 Temperature Profile Along Axial Length (Mermaid Qualitative Plot) ```mermaid graph TD K["x = 0: Cold Inlet End
Cold enters at Tc_i
Hot leaves at Th_o"] L["x = L/2: Middle
Both temperatures
approach crossing point"] M["x = L: Hot Inlet End
Hot enters at Th_i
Cold leaves at Tc_o"] K --> L --> M N["Hot Stream Profile
Th: starts HIGH at x=L
decreases linearly to Th_o at x=0"] O["Cold Stream Profile
Tc: starts LOW at x=0
increases linearly to Tc_o at x=L"] N -.overlaps.-> L O -.overlaps.-> L ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description of the Physical Hardware (Text-Based) A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger consists of the following physical components, arranged coaxially: | Component | Function | Material (typical) | |-----------|----------|---------------------| | **Inner pipe** | Carries one of the two liquids (often the more corrosive) | Stainless steel | | **Outer pipe (shell)** | Forms the annulus for the second liquid | Carbon steel | | **End fittings (return bends / bonnets)** | Allow the outer pipe to turn 180° at each end | Cast iron / steel | | **Inlet and outlet nozzles** | Direct stream entry and exit | Flanged connections | | **Support sheets / baffles** | Hold the inner pipe concentric within the outer pipe | Steel rod + spider | | **Insulation jacket** | Minimize ambient heat loss | Mineral wool / calcium silicate | > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw a neat sketch," always label the **flow directions with arrows**, indicate **all four end temperatures** ($T_{h,i}, T_{h,o}, T_{c,i}, T_{c,o}$), and show the **inner pipe** and **annulus** with distinct cross-hatching. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define LMTD. Why is the logarithmic mean used in preference to the arithmetic mean temperature difference in a heat exchanger?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **LMTD Definition:** The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) is defined as the temperature difference that, if applied uniformly across the entire heat transfer area $A$, would yield the same total heat transfer rate as the actual variable temperature difference in the heat exchanger. **Mathematical expression (counter-current):** $$\Delta T_{LM,CC} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$ **Why logarithmic mean?** The temperature profile along the length of a counter-current heat exchanger is **exponential**, not linear. This arises directly from the differential energy balance which yields $\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) dA$. Integrating an exponential function produces a natural logarithm. The arithmetic mean would **overestimate** the true driving force and is therefore non-physical for this configuration. For a constant $\Delta T$ (e.g., a phase-change condenser), the arithmetic mean and logarithmic mean coincide. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is the effectiveness of a heat exchanger? Write the effectiveness equation for a counter-current heat exchanger when the capacity rate ratio $C_r = 1$.** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Effectiveness Definition:** Effectiveness $(\varepsilon)$ is the ratio of the **actual heat transfer rate** to the **maximum possible heat transfer rate** in a heat exchanger. $$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$ For counter-current flow with $C_r = 1$ (balanced capacity rates), the effectiveness expression simplifies (via L'Hôpital's rule) to: $$\varepsilon_{CC} = \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU},\quad \text{where } NTU = \frac{U A}{C_{\min}}$$ This is the key design relationship when the two streams have **equal capacity rates** — a common scenario in liquid-liquid applications such as oil-to-water heat recovery. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing the **end-point $\Delta T$ definitions** for counter-current vs. co-current. For counter-current, $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ (the hot inlet and cold outlet are at the **same end**), not the way it is for co-current. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the LMTD unit-conversion factor ($1\ \text{K} = 1\ °C$ for intervals, but $1\ \text{K} \neq 1\ °F$). > - **Common Mistake #3:** When $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (within 1%), the LMTD collapses to the arithmetic mean; the L'Hôpital limit must be used. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Treating both streams as having equal capacity rates without computing $C_h$ and $C_c$ explicitly. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Misapplying the effectiveness formula — students often write the **co-current** formula when the question states **counter-current**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 3 | Modified for PECHT631]** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool **2.0 kg/s of oil** ($C_{p,\text{oil}} = 2.1\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$) from **150 °C to 80 °C** using **3.5 kg/s of water** ($C_{p,\text{water}} = 4.18\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$) entering at **30 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **$U = 800\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$**. **(a)** Determine the **water outlet temperature** and the **log mean temperature difference (LMTD)**. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the **heat transfer area** required. Also compute the **effectiveness** of the heat exchanger and the **NTU**. (7 marks) **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1 — Compute heat duty from the oil side:** $$Q = m_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 2.0 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 80) = 2.0 \times 2.1 \times 70 = 294\ \text{kW}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for water outlet temperature:** $$Q = m_c C_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$$ $$294 = 3.5 \times 4.18 \times (T_{c,o} - 30)$$ $$T_{c,o} - 30 = \frac{294}{14.63} = 20.10\ \text{°C}$$ $$\boxed{T_{c,o} = 50.10\ \text{°C}}$$ **Step 3 — Identify end-point temperature differences (counter-current convention):** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} -
