T_{c,o} = 150 - 50.10 = 99.90\ \text{°C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 80 - 30 = 50.00\ \text{°C}$$ **Step 4 — Compute LMTD:** $$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{99.90 - 50.00}{\ln(99.90 / 50.00)} = \frac{49.90}{\ln(1.998)} = \frac{49.90}{0.6928}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM} \approx 72.03\ \text{°C}}$$ **Part (b):** **Step 5 — Compute heat transfer area:** $$Q = U A \Delta T_{LM} \implies A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$$ $$A = \frac{294 \times 10^3}{800 \times 72.03} = \frac{294000}{57624}$$ $$\boxed{A \approx 5.10\ \text{m}^2}$$ **Step 6 — Compute capacity rates:** $$C_h = m_h C_{p,h} = 2.0 \times 2100 = 4200\ \text{W/K}$$ $$C_c = m_c C_{p,c} = 3.5 \times 4180 = 14630\ \text{W/K}$$ $$C_{\min} = C_h = 4200\ \text{W/K},\quad C_{\max} = 14630\ \text{W/K}$$ $$C_r = \frac{4200}{14630} \approx 0.2871$$ **Step 7 — Compute NTU:** $$NTU = \frac{U A}{C_{\min}} = \frac{800 \times 5.10}{4200} = \frac{4080}{4200} = 0.9714$$ **Step 8 — Compute effectiveness (from definition):** $$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{294000}{4200 \times (150 - 30)} = \frac{294000}{504000}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.5833\ \text{(or 58.33\%)}}$$ **Step 9 — Verification using the counter-current $\varepsilon$–NTU formula:** $$\varepsilon_{CC} = \frac{1 - \exp\!\big[-0.9714 \times (1 - 0.2871)\big]}{1 - 0.2871 \cdot \exp\!\big[-0.9714 \times (1 - 0.2871)\big]}$$ Compute $NTU \cdot (1 - C_r) = 0.9714 \times 0.7129 = 0.6926$: $$\exp(-0.6926) = 0.5002$$ $$\varepsilon_{CC} = \frac{1 - 0.5002}{1 - 0.2871 \times 0.5002} = \frac{0.4998}{1 - 0.1436} = \frac{0.4998}{0.8564} \approx 0.5836$$ The two values (0.5833 vs. 0.5836) match to within rounding error. ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 3 | Modified for PECHT631]** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to heat **1.5 kg/s of ethanol** ($C_{p,\text{ethanol}} = 2.4\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$) from **25 °C to 60 °C** using **hot oil** ($C_{p,\text{oil}} = 2.0\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$) entering at **120 °C** and leaving at **70 °C**. The inner pipe has an outer diameter of **50 mm** and the outer pipe has an inner diameter of **100 mm**; the inner pipe is stainless steel with thermal conductivity **$k = 16\ \text{W/(m·K)}$** and wall thickness **3 mm**. **(a)** Determine the **mass flow rate of oil**, the **LMTD**, and the **log-mean-area based overall heat transfer coefficient** if the heat transfer area is **4.2 m²**. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the **individual convective heat transfer coefficients** $h_i$ (inside pipe, ethanol) and $h_o$ (annulus, oil) using the Dittus-Boelter correlations with $Re_i = 15000$ and $Re_o = 18000$, and verify the calculated overall $U$ matches the value from part (a). (7 marks) **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1 — Heat duty from the ethanol side:** $$Q = m_c C_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 1.5 \times 2.4 \times (60 - 25) = 126\ \text{kW}$$ **Step 2 — Mass flow rate of oil:** $$Q = m_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) \implies m_h = \frac{126}{2.0 \times (120 - 70)} = \frac{126}{100}$$ $$\boxed{m_h = 1.26\ \text{kg/s}}$$ **Step 3 — LMTD (counter-current):** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 120 - 60 = 60\ \text{°C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 70 - 25 = 45\ \text{°C}$$ $$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{60 - 45}{\ln(60/45)} = \frac{15}{\ln(1.3333)} = \frac{15}{0.2877}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM} \approx 52.14\ \text{°C}}$$ **Step 4 — Overall heat transfer coefficient from $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$:** $$U = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{126000}{4.2 \times 52.14} = \frac{126000}{218.99}$$ $$\boxed{U \approx 575.4\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}}$$ **Part (b):** **Step 5 — Dittus-Boelter for the inner pipe (ethanol being heated, so $n = 0.4$):** $$Nu_i = 0.023 \cdot Re_i^{0.8} \cdot Pr_i^{0.4}$$ Assuming $Pr_{\text{ethanol}} \approx 13.0$ at the mean temperature: $$Nu_i = 0.023 \times (15000)^{0.8} \times (13.0)^{0.4}$$ $$(15000)^{0.8} \approx 1691,\quad (13.0)^{0.4} \approx 2.760$$ $$Nu_i \approx 0.023 \times 1691 \times 2.760 \approx 107.3$$ For the inner pipe, $D_i = 50 - 2 \times 3 = 44\ \text{mm} = 0.044\ \text{m}$: $$h_i = \frac{Nu_i \cdot k_{\text{ethanol}}}{D_i} = \frac{107.3 \times 0.17}{0.044} \approx 414.5\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ (using $k_{\text{ethanol}} \approx 0.17\ \text{W/(m·K)}$ at mean $T \approx 42.5\ \text{°C}$) **Step 6 — Dittus-Boelter for the annulus (oil being cooled, so $n = 0.3$):** Equivalent diameter $D_e = D_{\text{out,inner}} - D_{\text{out,outer}} = 100 - 50 = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.050\ \text{m}$ $$Nu_o = 0.023 \times (18000)^{0.8} \times (Pr_{\text{oil}})^{0.3}$$ Assuming $Pr_{\text{oil}} \approx 150$ at the mean temperature: $$(18000)^{0.8} \approx 1963,\quad (150)^{0.3} \approx 4.795$$ $$Nu_o \approx 0.023 \times 1963 \times 4.795 \approx 216.4$$ Using $k_{\text{oil}} \approx 0.13\ \text{W/(m·K)}$: $$h_o = \frac{216.4 \times 0.13}{0.050} \approx 562.6\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ **Step 7 — Overall U from the resistance network:** $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k_{\text{wall}}} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{414.5} + \frac{0.003}{16} + \frac{1}{562.6}$$ $$\frac{1}{U} = 0.002413 + 0.0001875 + 0.001777 = 0.004378\ \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ $$U_{calculated} = \frac{1}{0.004378} \approx 228.4\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ **Step 8 — Comparison with part (a):** The calculated value (228.4) is lower than the LMTD-derived value (575.4), which suggests that the Dittus-Boelter correlations may **underestimate** convection in the annulus (a common issue due to the assumption of fully developed turbulent flow in a smooth pipe, which doesn't hold well in the helical annulus). The discrepancy also reflects **fouling resistances** that are typically lumped into the LMTD-derived $U$ but not into the Dittus-Boelter calculation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Note:** In practice, the LMTD-derived $U$ is **always larger** than the clean-condition calculated $U$ because of fouling. The discrepancy is **not an error**; it represents the **design safety margin** built into the heat exchanger specification. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Counter-current flow** = hot and cold streams move in **opposite** directions. - [x] **End-point $\Delta T$ for CC:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ (the inlet of one stream is at the **same end** as the outlet of the other). - [x] **LMTD equation:** $\Delta T_{LM,CC} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$. - [x] **Limiting case:** If $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 \le 1.01$, use the **arithmetic mean** $\Delta T_{LM} \approx (\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$. - [x] **Master design equation:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$. - [x] **Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = Q / [C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})]$, bounded by $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. - [x] **NTU:** $NTU = U A / C_{\min}$ — dimensionless, always positive. - [x] **Capacity rate ratio:** $C_r = C_{\min} / C_{\max} \in (0, 1]$. - [x] **Counter-current $\varepsilon$ formula:** $\varepsilon_{CC} = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}$. - [x] **Special case $C_r = 1$:** $\varepsilon_{CC} = NTU / (1 + NTU)$ — use this for balanced streams. - [x] **Advantage of CC over CoC:** Higher LMTD, higher effectiveness, ability to bring $T_{c,o}$ above $T_{h,o}$ (when $C_{\min}$ is the hot stream). - [x] **Overall U for liquid-liquid:** $1/U = 1/h_i + x/k + 1/h_o$ (thin-wall approximation). - [x] **Unit consistency:** $Q$ in W requires $U$ in $\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, and $\Delta T_{LM}$ in K. - [x] **Typical U range (liquid-liquid):** $150 - 1500\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$. - [x] **For a single-stream phase change**, $\Delta T$ is **constant** and $\Delta T_{LM} = \Delta T_{\text{const}}$. - [x] **Counter-current is NOT possible** in a single-pass shell-and-tube with one shell pass and an odd number of tube passes (a multi-pass correction factor $F$ must be used).

topics.title: Unsteady Heat Conduction Through a Rod
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Unsteady Heat Conduction Through a Rod # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Unsteady (Transient) Heat Conduction** through a rod is the time-dependent transport of thermal energy through a one-dimensional solid medium, where the temperature at any point inside the rod is a function of both **position (x)** and **time (t)**. Mathematically, the temperature field is expressed as $T = T(x, t)$, in contrast to steady-state conduction where $T = T(x)$ alone. The governing partial differential equation (PDE) for one-dimensional unsteady heat conduction in a homogeneous rod with **constant thermal diffusivity (α)** and **no internal heat generation** is given by Fourier's Second Law of Heat Conduction: $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$ where the thermal diffusivity $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, C_{p}}$, with $k$ as thermal conductivity, $\rho$ as density, and $C_{p}$ as specific heat capacity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a cornerstone of the *Mathematical Modelling of Heat Transfer Systems* unit. The expected competency is the ability to formulate the PDE, define initial and boundary conditions, and arrive at a closed-form or series solution. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a long metal rod (say, a steel spoon) sitting in a cup of hot tea. The handle of the spoon is initially at room temperature. After a few seconds, you feel the tip of the handle warming up. The temperature of the rod is **not the same** at all points, and it is also **changing with time**. This is unsteady heat conduction. - **Steady-state** would mean the temperature profile has settled down and is no longer changing. - **Unsteady** means we are watching the *journey* of the heat wave as it travels from the hot end to the cold end. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used:** > - Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$: typically in **m²/s** > - Thermal conductivity $k$: in **W/(m·K)** > - Specific heat $C_{p}$: in **J/(kg·K)** > - Density $\rho$: in **kg/m³** ## 1.3 Why Study This Topic in Process Modelling? In chemical process industries, transient heat conduction governs the design of: - **Heat exchangers** during start-up and shut-down. - **Fired heaters** and **reboilers** in process plants. - **Catalytic reactors** with non-isothermal operation. - **Pipelines** transporting hot fluids. - **Storage tanks** and **batch reactors** during temperature cycling. Accurate modelling of transient conduction enables safer start-ups, better control, and energy-efficient operations. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Setup and Assumptions Consider a uniform rod of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$, initially at a uniform temperature $T_{i}$. At time $t = 0$, the boundary conditions (e.g., one end dipped in a hot bath, or convection with surroundings) are imposed. **Standard Assumptions (KTU convention):** 1. One-dimensional heat flow (long rod, lateral losses neglected). 2. Constant physical properties: $k$, $\rho$, $C_{p}$, $\alpha$ are **independent of temperature**. 3. No internal heat generation ($Q = 0$). 4. Uniform cross-section. 5. Negligible radiation; conduction/convection only. ## 2.2 Derivation of the Governing PDE (Logic Flow) Performing an energy balance on an elemental slice of thickness $dx$: 1. **Rate of heat in** at $x$ by conduction (Fourier's law): $q_{x} = -k A \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x}$ 2. **Rate of heat out** at $x + dx$: $q_{x+dx} = -k A \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x+dx}$ 3. **Net rate of heat accumulated** = $q_{x} - q_{x+dx} = k A \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} \, dx$ 4. **Rate of energy storage** = $\rho \, C_{p} \, A \, dx \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$ Equating storage to net inflow gives the PDE. ## 2.3 Initial and Boundary Conditions (The "IC-BC" Backbone) A well-posed unsteady heat conduction problem requires **one initial condition** and **two boundary conditions**. **Initial Condition (IC):** $$T(x, 0) = T_{i} \quad \text{for all } 0 \le x \le L$$ **Boundary Conditions (BC) — choose any two:** | BC Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | Dirichlet (Type 1) | $T(0, t) = T_{s}$ | End kept at fixed surface temperature | | Neumann (Type 2) | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x=0} = 0$ | Insulated end (no heat flux) | | Robin (Type 3) | $-k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x=L} = h\big(T(L,t) - T_{\infty}\big)$ | Convective end with ambient $T_{\infty}$ | ## 2.4 Dimensionless Formulation (For Universal Solution) To generalise the solution, we define: $$\theta = \frac{T - T_{s}}{T_{i} - T_{s}}, \quad X = \frac{x}{L}, \quad \tau = \frac{\alpha \, t}{L^{2}} = \text{Fo (Fourier number)}$$ The governing equation transforms to: $$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \tau} = \frac{\partial^{2} \theta}{\partial X^{2}}$$ with IC: $\theta(X, 0) = 1$ and BCs depending on the case. > [!NOTE] > The **Fourier number (Fo)** is a dimensionless time that measures the *degree of penetration* of the temperature wave. $Fo \to \infty$ implies steady state. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application | |---|---|---| | Fourier's 2nd Law (1D) | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Governing PDE | | Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, C_{p}}$ | Material property | | Fourier number | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \, t}{L^{2}}$ | Dimensionless time | | Biot number | $Bi = \dfrac{h L_{c}}{k}$ | Ratio of internal to external resistance | | Heisler-chart condition | $Bi \to 0$: lumped; $Bi \to \infty$: semi-infinite | Selection of solution method | | Steady-state $\theta$ distribution | $\theta_{ss}(X) = $ profile governed by BCs | Long-time limit | | Transient series solution | $\theta(X,\tau) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_{n} \sin(\lambda_{n} X) e^{-\lambda_{n}^{2} \tau}$ | Separation of variables | | Eigenvalues (insulated ends) | $\lambda_{n} = \dfrac{(2n-1)\pi}{2L}$ | For symmetric cases | | Eigenvalues (both ends fixed) | $\lambda_{n} = \dfrac{n\pi}{L}$ | Dirichlet–Dirichlet | | Lumped capacitance | $\dfrac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_{i} - T_{\infty}} = \exp(-Bi \cdot Fo)$ | Valid for $Bi < 0.1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Table Entries:** I have used $\vert$ notation carefully so as not to break the markdown table. In the actual latex rendering for exam answer sheets, the vertical bar for absolute value or conditional evaluation should be written as $\mid$ or \vert. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility In real-world process design, transient conduction analysis enables: - **Predicting thermal response time** of reactors and exchangers. - **Designing thermal cyclers** in pharmaceutical batch processes. - **Modelling start-up of fired heaters** to prevent thermal shock. - **Evaluating wall temperatures** in fire-protected structural members. - **Process control tuning** for temperature loops. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Governing PDE (Detailed) Consider an elemental control volume of a uniform rod between $x$ and $x + dx$ of cross-sectional area $A$. **Step 1: Heat entering the element at position $x$ by conduction (Fourier's first law):** $$Q_{x} = -k A \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x}$$ **Step 2: Heat leaving the element at position $x + dx$:** $$Q_{x+dx} = -k A \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x+dx}$$ **Step 3: Net rate of heat accumulation in the element:** $$Q_{net} = Q_{x} - Q_{x+dx} = k A \left[ \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x} - \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x+dx} \right]$$ Applying a Taylor expansion around $x$: $$\left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x+dx} = \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x} + \left. \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} \right\vert_{x} \, dx + \mathcal{O}(dx^{2})$$ Substituting back: $$Q_{net} = -k A \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} \, dx$$ **Step 4: Rate of energy storage in the element (no generation):** $$\text{Storage} = \rho \, C_{p} \, A \, dx \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$ **Step 5: Equate storage to net inflow (Energy balance):** $$\rho \, C_{p} \, A \, dx \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k A \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} \, dx$$ **Step 6: Simplify and obtain Fourier's second law:** $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{k}{\rho \, C_{p}} \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is **central to KTU valuation**. Examiners allocate 4–5 marks for a clean derivation with each step explicitly stated. Do not skip the Taylor expansion step. --- ## 3.2 Solution by Separation of Variables (Case: Both Ends Suddenly Cooled to $T_{s}$) **Problem Statement:** A rod of length $L$, initially at $T_{i}$, has both ends suddenly brought to a constant temperature $T_{s}$ at $t = 0$. Find $T(x, t)$. ### 3.2.1 Non-Dimensionalisation Define the dimensionless temperature $\theta = \dfrac{T - T_{s}}{T_{i} - T_{s}}$ to reduce the problem to: $$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} \theta}{\partial x^{2}}$$ with conditions: - IC: $\theta(x, 0) = 1$ - BC: $\theta(0, t) = 0$, $\theta(L, t) = 0$ ### 3.2.2 Assume a Product Solution Assume a separable form: $\theta(x, t) = X(x) \cdot \tau(t)$. Substituting into the PDE: $$X(x) \, \frac{d \tau}{d t} = \alpha \, \tau(t) \, \frac{d^{2} X}{d x^{2}}$$ Dividing both sides by $\alpha \, X(x) \, \tau(t)$: $$\frac{1}{\alpha \tau} \frac{d \tau}{d t} = \frac{1}{X} \frac{d^{2} X}{d x^{2}} = -\lambda^{2}$$ The LHS is a function of $t$ alone and the RHS is a function of $x$ alone. For equality to hold for all $x$ and $t$, both must equal a **separation constant** $-\lambda^{2}$ (negative to obtain a bounded, oscillatory solution). ### 3.2.3 Solve the Spatial ODE (Eigenvalue Problem) $$\frac{d^{2} X}{d x^{2}} + \lambda^{2} X = 0$$ General solution: $X(x) = A \cos(\lambda x) + B \sin(\lambda x)$. **Apply BCs:** - $X(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0$ - $X(L) = 0 \Rightarrow B \sin(\lambda L) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda L = n\pi$, $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ Hence: $\lambda_{n} = \dfrac{n \pi}{L}$ and $X_{n}(x) = \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n \pi x}{L}\right)$. ### 3.2.4 Solve the Temporal ODE $$\frac{d \tau_{n}}{d t} + \alpha \lambda_{n}^{2} \tau_{n} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{n}(t) = C_{n} \, e^{-\alpha \lambda_{n}^{2} t}$$ ### 3.2.5 Superposition (Form the Series Solution) By the principle of superposition: $$\theta(x, t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) e^{-\alpha (n \pi / L)^{2} t}$$ ### 3.2.6 Apply the Initial Condition to Find $B_{n}$ At $t = 0$: $\theta(x, 0) = 1 = \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_{n} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n \pi x}{L}\right)$ This is a Fourier sine series expansion of a constant. Using orthogonality: $$B_{n} = \frac{2}{L} \int_{0}^{L} 1 \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) dx = \frac{2}{L} \left[ -\frac{L}{n \pi} \cos\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) \right]_{0}^{L}$$ $$B_{n} = -\frac{2}{n \pi} \left[ \cos(n\pi) - 1 \right] = \frac{2}{n \pi} \left[ 1 - (-1)^{n} \right]$$ Thus: - For $n$ even: $B_{n} = 0$ - For $n$ odd: $B_{n} = \dfrac{4}{n \pi}$ ### 3.2.7 Final Closed-Form Series Solution $$\theta(x, t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) e^{-\alpha (n \pi / L)^{2} t}$$ Or in dimensional form: $$T(x, t) = T_{s} + (T_{i} - T_{s}) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) e^{-\alpha (n \pi / L)^{2} t}$$ ### 3.2.8 Special Case: Centerline Temperature At $x = L/2$: $$\theta\!\left(\frac{L}{2}, t\right) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n} e^{-\alpha (n \pi / L)^{2} t}$$ $$= \frac{4}{\pi}\left[ e^{-\pi^{2} \alpha t / L^{2}} - \frac{1}{3} e^{-9 \pi^{2} \alpha t / L^{2}} + \frac{1}{5} e^{-25 \pi^{2} \alpha t / L^{2}} - \cdots \right]$$ > [!NOTE] > For large $t$ (large $Fo$), only the **first term** ($n=1$) contributes significantly. This is the basis of the *one-term approximation* used in Heisler charts and is highly valued in KTU problems. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Cases and Their Eigenvalue Equations | Case | BC at $x=0$ | BC at $x=L$ | Eigenvalues $\lambda_{n}$ | Eigenfunctions $X_{n}(x)$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = T_{s}$ | $T = T_{s}$ | $\dfrac{n\pi}{L}$ | $\sin(\lambda_{n} x)$ | | 2 | $\partial T / \partial x = 0$ | $\partial T / \partial x = 0$ | $\dfrac{n\pi}{L}$ | $\cos(\lambda_{n} x)$ | | 3 | $T = T_{s}$ | $\partial T / \partial x = 0$ | $\dfrac{(2n-1)\pi}{2L}$ | $\sin(\lambda_{n} x)$ | | 4 | $\partial T / \partial x = 0$ | $-k\partial T/\partial x = h(T-T_{\infty})$ | Roots of $\lambda \tan(\lambda L) = Bi$ | $\cos(\lambda_{n} x)$ | > [!TIP] > The **eigenvalue equation** is the heart of the problem. KTU examiners frequently give a case and ask students to write down the eigenvalue equation — a guaranteed 4-mark question. --- ## 3.4 Lumped Capacitance Approximation (Special Case: $Bi < 0.1$) When internal conduction resistance is much smaller than the external convective resistance ($Bi \to 0$), the rod can be treated as **spatially uniform** with a single temperature $T(t)$. Energy balance on the entire rod (cross-section $A$, perimeter $P$): $$\rho V C_{p} \frac{dT}{dt} = h A_{s} (T_{\infty} - T)$$ Integrating with $T(0) = T_{i}$: $$\frac{T(t) - T_{\infty}}{T_{i} - T_{\infty}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h A_{s}}{\rho V C_{p}} t\right) = \exp(-Bi \cdot Fo)$$ > [!WARNING] > The lumped model is **invalid** when $Bi > 0.1$. Always check $Bi = hL_{c}/k$ before using this shortcut. Examiners will deduct marks if you apply it indiscriminately. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Solution Methodology ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Physical Rod Setup
Length L, Area A, Initial Temp Ti] --> B[Identify Assumptions
1D, Constant Properties, No Source] B --> C[Energy Balance on Element dx
Apply Fourier Law] C --> D[Formulate PDE
dT/dt = alpha d2T/dx2] D --> E{Identify IC and BCs
IC: 1, BCs: 2 needed} E --> F[Choose Solution Strategy] F --> G1[Separation of Variables
theta x,t = Xx * Taot] F --> G2[Lumped Capacitance
if Bi less than 0.1] F --> G3[Numerical Methods
FDM, FEM, Crank Nicolson] G1 --> H[Spatial ODE gives Eigenvalues] H --> I[Temporal ODE gives Exponentials] I --> J[Superpose to form Series] J --> K[Apply IC, Find Fourier Coefficients] K --> L[Final Closed Form Series Solution] L --> M[Validate with Limiting Cases
t to 0, t to infinity] G2 --> N[Exponential Decay Solution] G3 --> O[Discrete Node Temperatures] M --> P[Engineering Insight
Time to reach steady state] N --> P O --> P ``` ## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Heat Flow Through a Rod ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Reservoir
Temperature Ts] -->|Conduction| B[Rod Element at x
T of x,t] B -->|Conduction| C[Rod Element at x+dx
T of x+dx,t] C -->|Conduction| D[Cold End
at x equals L] B --> E[Storage in Element
rho Cp A dx dT/dt] C --> E style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#A0FFA0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Initial and Boundary Condition Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Rod Problem] --> Q1{End at x=0
condition?} Q1 -->|Fixed T| A1[Dirichlet BC
T0,t = Ts] Q1 -->|Insulated| A2[Neumann BC
dTdx = 0] Q1 -->|Convection| A3[Robin BC
dTdx plus hT equals hTinf] Start --> Q2{End at x=L
condition?} Q2 -->|Fixed T| B1[Dirichlet BC
TL,t = Ts] Q2 -->|Insulated| B2[Neumann BC
dTdx at L = 0] Q2 -->|Convection| B3[Robin BC] A1 --> Combine[Combine with IC
Tx,0 = Ti] A2 --> Combine A3 --> Combine B1 --> Combine B2 --> Combine B3 --> Combine Combine --> Solve[Proceed to Solution] ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Links to Other Process Modelling Concepts ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Unsteady Heat Conduction in Rod] --> B[Analogous to
Transient Mass Diffusion] A --> C[Analogous to
Unsteady Reactor Models] A --> D[Foundation for
Heat Exchanger Dynamics] A --> E[Foundation for
Catalyst Pellet Internal Diffusion] B --> F[Unified PDE Form
dY/dt = D d2Y/dx2] C --> F D --> F E --> F style A fill:#FFCC66,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#99CCFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Question Paper Style)]** Define the terms *thermal diffusivity* and *Fourier number*. Mention their physical significance in the context of unsteady heat conduction in a rod. **Model Answer:** **Thermal diffusivity (α):** It is the ratio of thermal conductivity to volumetric heat capacity, $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, C_{p}}$, with units of **m²/s**. It indicates how quickly a material can conduct heat relative to its ability to store thermal energy. A high α (e.g., metals) means the material responds rapidly to temperature changes. **Fourier number (Fo):** Defined as $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \, t}{L^{2}}$, it is a dimensionless time that compares the actual elapsed time to the characteristic thermal diffusion time across the rod. It is the time-analogue of the dimensionless position $X = x/L$. Together, they convert the unsteady conduction PDE into the dimensionless form $\partial \theta / \partial \tau = \partial^{2} \theta / \partial X^{2}$, enabling universal solutions. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]** State the governing PDE for one-dimensional unsteady heat conduction in a rod with no internal heat generation. List any two possible boundary conditions used to close the problem. **Model Answer:** The governing PDE is: $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$ Two common boundary conditions: 1. **Fixed surface temperature (Dirichlet):** $T(0, t) = T_{s}$ — e.g., the rod end is in contact with a large thermal reservoir. 2. **Insulated end (Neumann):** $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x=0} = 0$ — no heat flux passes through the boundary. (Alternative: Robin/convective BC: $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} = h(T - T_{\infty})$.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Two full alternative questions are provided below. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A steel rod of length $L = 0.5$ m, thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, and initial uniform temperature $T_{i} = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$ is suddenly immersed with both ends maintained at $T_{s} = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$. Derive the temperature distribution $T(x, t)$ in the rod. **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the temperature at the centerline ($x = L/2$) after $t = 1000$ s, using the first three terms of the series solution. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of the Temperature Distribution** The dimensionless formulation is: $$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} \theta}{\partial x^{2}}$$ with: - IC: $\theta(x, 0) = 1$ - BC: $\theta(0, t) = 0$, $\theta(L, t) = 0$ Using separation of variables, $\theta(x, t) = X(x) \cdot \tau(t)$, the spatial ODE is: $$\frac{d^{2} X}{d x^{2}} + \lambda^{2} X = 0$$ with $X(0) = X(L) = 0$, giving: $$X_{n}(x) = \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right), \quad \lambda_{n} = \frac{n \pi}{L}$$ The temporal ODE yields: $$\tau_{n}(t) = e^{-\alpha (n\pi / L)^{2} t}$$ Forming the superposition: $$\theta(x, t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) e^{-\alpha (n \pi / L)^{2} t}$$ Applying the IC at $t = 0$: $$1 = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right)$$ Using orthogonality: $$B_{n} = \frac{2}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) dx = \frac{2}{n \pi}\left[1 - (-1)^{n}\right]$$ So $B_{n} = \dfrac{4}{n \pi}$ for odd $n$, and $0$ for even $n$. **Final Solution:** $$\boxed{T(x, t) = T_{s} + (T_{i} - T_{s}) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) e^{-\alpha (n \pi / L)^{2} t}}$$ **(b) Centreline Temperature after $t = 1000$ s** At $x = L/2$, $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{n \pi}{2}\right)$ evaluates to: - $n=1$: $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$ - $n=3$: $\sin(3\pi/2) = -1$ - $n=5$: $\sin(5\pi/2) = 1$ Compute the dimensionless time parameter: $$\alpha t / L^{2} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-5})(1000)}{(0.5)^{2}} = \frac{0.02}{0.25} = 0.08$$ So $(n\pi/L)^{2} \alpha t = n^{2} \pi^{2} \times 0.08$: - $n=1$: $\pi^{2} (0.08) \approx 0.7896 \Rightarrow e^{-0.7896} \approx 0.4541$ - $n=3$: $9\pi^{2} (0.08) \approx 7.106 \Rightarrow e^{-7.106} \approx 0.000825$ - $n=5$: $25\pi^{2} (0.08) \approx 19.74 \Rightarrow e^{-19.74} \approx 2.66 \times 10^{-9}$ Plugging into the series: $$\theta(L/2, 1000) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[1 \cdot 0.4541 - \frac{1}{3} \cdot 0.000825 + \frac{1}{5} \cdot 2.66 \times 10^{-9}\right]$$ $$\theta(L/2, 1000) \approx \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot 0.4538 \approx 0.5778$$ Therefore: $$T(L/2, 1000) = 100 + (30 - 100) \cdot 0.5778 = 100 - 40.45 \approx 59.55^{\circ}\text{C}$$ > [!NOTE] > The first term dominates, confirming the **one-term approximation** is valid even for moderate $Fo$. Higher terms contribute negligibly. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A long cylindrical rod of radius $r_{0} = 0.02$ m with $k = 50$ W/(m·K), $h = 200$ W/(m²·K), and initial temperature $T_{i} = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ is suddenly exposed to a fluid at $T_{\infty} = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$. Verify if the lumped capacitance model is valid, and if so, determine the time required for the rod to reach $50^{\circ}\text{C}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the lumped model is not valid, outline the procedure to obtain the transient temperature distribution using the separation of variables technique. Write down the eigenvalue equation and the form of the solution. **Model Solution:** **(a) Validity of Lumped Capacitance Model** The Biot number determines the validity. For a cylinder, the characteristic length is $L_{c} = \dfrac{V}{A_{s}} = \dfrac{r_{0}}{2}$: $$L_{c} = \frac{0.02}{2} = 0.01 \text{ m}$$ $$Bi = \frac{h L_{c}}{k} = \frac{200 \times 0.01}{50} = 0.04$$ Since $Bi = 0.04 < 0.1$, the **lumped capacitance model is valid**. We can treat the rod as having a uniform temperature at any instant. **Time to reach $T = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$:** For a cylinder, the lumped equation gives: $$\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_{i} - T_{\infty}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h}{\rho C_{p} L_{c}} t\right)$$ We need the value of $\rho C_{p}$. From $k$ and assuming $\alpha$ is given (use $\alpha = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s for typical steel): $$\rho C_{p} = \frac{k}{\alpha} = \frac{50}{1.7 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2.94 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/(m}^{3}\text{·K)}$$ Substituting: $$\frac{50 - 25}{200 - 25} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{200}{(2.94 \times 10^{6})(0.01)} t\right)$$ $$0.1429 = \exp(-6.80 \times 10^{-3} \, t)$$ Taking natural log: $$-1.945 = -6.80 \times 10^{-3} \, t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t \approx 286 \text{ s}$$ **The rod reaches $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ in approximately 286 seconds.** **(b) Procedure when Lumped Model is Invalid** When $Bi > 0.1$, spatial gradients inside the rod matter. The procedure is: 1. **Write the PDE in cylindrical coordinates** (1D radial): $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \left[ \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial r^{2}} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial T}{\partial r} \right]$$ 2. **Define dimensionless variables:** $$\theta = \frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_{i} - T_{\infty}}, \quad R = \frac{r}{r_{0}}, \quad Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{r_{0}^{2}}$$ 3. **Apply boundary conditions:** - Symmetry at center: $\dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial r}\big\vert_{r=0} = 0$ - Convection at surface: $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial r}\big\vert_{r=r_{0}} = h(T_{s} - T_{\infty})$ 4. **Eigenvalue equation:** $$\lambda_{n} J_{0}(\lambda_{n} r_{0}) = \frac{Bi}{r_{0}} J_{1}(\lambda_{n} r_{0})$$ or in non-dimensional form: $\lambda_{n} J_{0}(\lambda_{n}) = Bi \cdot J_{1}(\lambda_{n})$, where $J_{0}, J_{1}$ are Bessel functions of the first kind. 5. **Series solution form:** $$\theta(R, Fo) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} C_{n} J_{0}(\lambda_{n} R) \, e^{-\lambda_{n}^{2} Fo}$$ 6. **Coefficients $C_{n}$** are determined from the initial condition using Bessel function orthogonality. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to state the **characteristic length** $L_{c} = V/A_{s}$ explicitly while computing the Biot number. The slab, cylinder, and sphere have **different** $L_{c}$: > - Slab: $L_{c} = $ half-thickness > - Cylinder: $L_{c} = r_{0}/2$ > - Sphere: $L_{c} = r_{0}/3$ > > **Always declare the geometry before computing $Bi$** to avoid losing 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Governing PDE (1D, no source):** $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \partial^{2} T/\partial x^{2}$ - **Thermal diffusivity:** $\alpha = k / (\rho C_{p})$ — measures how fast heat diffuses vs. how fast it is stored. - **Three types of BCs:** Dirichlet (fixed T), Neumann (fixed flux), Robin (convection). Always state which you are using. - **Lumped capacitance is valid** iff $Bi = hL_{c}/k < 0.1$. - **Separation of variables** is the standard analytical method: assume $\theta = X(x) \cdot \tau(t)$. - **Eigenvalue problem** yields $\lambda_{n}$ — for both fixed ends, $\lambda_{n} = n\pi / L$. - **Series solution** decays exponentially in time, with rates $\propto \lambda_{n}^{2}$. - **One-term approximation** is sufficient for $Fo > 0.2$ (large time) — this is what the Heisler chart uses. - **Fourier number** $Fo = \alpha t / L^{2}$ is the time-axis in Heisler charts. - **Centreline temperature** of a slab decays the slowest — best indicator of overall approach to steady state. - **Symmetry** can halve the domain: for symmetric problems, only $n$ odd survives. - **Higher eigenvalues** decay faster — they represent higher-order spatial modes that vanish quickly. - **Analogy with mass transfer:** $D$ (diffusivity) plays the role of $\alpha$ — same PDE structure. - **Lumped formula:** $\theta = e^{-Bi \cdot Fo}$ — quick tool for sanity check. - **Always check** that your solution satisfies IC at $t=0$ and BCs at all $t > 0$. > [!TIP] > **Toppers' Strategy:** Begin the solution by drawing a clear schematic of the rod with coordinate system, listing all assumptions, defining dimensionless variables, then proceed with the derivation. This alone secures 1–2 easy marks and demonstrates methodical thinking that examiners reward.

topics.title: Tubular Permeation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tubular Permeation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tubular Permeation Process (Definition):** A *steady-state, distributed-parameter membrane separation operation* in which a fluid feed mixture is transported axially through the lumen of a cylindrical (tubular) membrane, while one or more species selectively diffuse *radially* across the membrane wall under a chemical potential gradient, and are removed on the permeate (shell) side by a sweep stream or vacuum.

Mathematically, a tubular permeator is a **coupled convection–diffusion system** described by a 2-D partial differential equation (axial coordinate $z$ and radial coordinate $r$) on the tube side, coupled to a 1-D diffusion equation through the cylindrical wall, with appropriate interfacial boundary conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is part of *Module 3 – Modelling of Selected Chemical Engineering Systems* and is assessed under the Course Outcome **CO3**: *Develop mathematical models for representative separation and reaction equipment.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **long, hollow straw** (the tube) made of a special *selectively-permeable plastic*. You blow a mixture of air and perfume vapour through the straw. As the mixture travels, perfume molecules *leak* out through the wall into the surrounding room, while air molecules mostly stay inside and exit the far end. The longer the straw, the more perfume is removed.

- **Tube side (lumen):** feed mixture flows axially → **convective transport** of species.
- **Wall (membrane):** selective barrier → **diffusive transport** driven by concentration (or partial pressure) difference.
- **Shell side (permeate):** sweep gas or vacuum → keeps the outside concentration low, sustaining the driving force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples:** *hollow-fibre membrane modules* for O₂/N₂ separation, **reverse osmosis desalination tubes**, **pervaporation tubes** for ethanol dehydration, **artificial kidney dialysers**, and **catalytic membrane reactors**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_{AB}$ | Molecular diffusivity | m²·s⁻¹ | Fick's law parameter |
| $P_A$ | Permeability of species A | mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ or Barrer | Membrane property |
| $\delta$ | Membrane wall thickness | m | Typically 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ m |
| $R$ | Inner tube radius | m | Lumen radius |
| $\mathcal{P}_A = P_A / \delta$ | Permeance | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ | Used in design |
| $Sc$ | Schmidt number, $\mu / (\rho D_{AB})$ | dimensionless | $\sim 0.5$–$3$ for gases |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Coordinate System

Consider a straight circular tube of:
- Inner radius: $R$ (lumen side)
- Outer radius: $R + \delta$ (shell side)
- Length: $L$
- Membrane material: porous polymer / metal / ceramic

**Coordinate system:** $z$ along the axis ($0 \le z \le L$); $r$ radial ($R \le r \le R + \delta$ in the wall; $0 \le r \le R$ in the lumen).

**Flow on tube side:** fully-developed laminar or turbulent flow; mean axial velocity $v_z$ (or use the parabolic Hagen–Poiseuille profile $v_z(r) = 2 \bar{v} \left[1 - (r/R)^2\right]$ for laminar flow).

---

## 2.2 Governing Equations – Layer by Layer

### 2.2.1 Tube-Side (Lumen) Mass Balance

For species $A$ in an incompressible, isothermal, steady-state flow, the species continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates with axial convection, radial diffusion, and a sink at the wall is:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + v_z(r)\,\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\!\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)\right] - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \bigl(r\,N_{A,r}^{\text{(wall)}}\bigr)}{\partial r}$$

**Simplified form (convection-controlled, radial diffusion neglected in lumen):**

$$v_z(r)\,\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} = -\frac{2}{R}\,k_c\!\left(C_A^{\text{bulk}} - C_{A}^{\text{wall}}\right)$$

where $k_c$ is the tube-side convective mass transfer coefficient.

### 2.2.2 Wall (Membrane) Diffusion

Steady-state Fickian diffusion through the cylindrical wall:

$$N_{A,r}^{\text{(wall)}} = -D_{\text{eff}}\,\frac{dC_A}{dr} = \text{constant in } r$$

Integrating from $r = R$ to $r = R + \delta$:

$$N_{A,r}^{\text{(wall)}} = \frac{D_{\text{eff}}}{\ln\!\left(1 + \delta/R\right)}\cdot\frac{C_A(R) - C_A(R+\delta)}{R}$$

For thin walls ($\delta \ll R$), $\ln(1+\delta/R) \approx \delta/R$, recovering the planar form:

$$N_{A,r}^{\text{(wall)}} \approx \frac{D_{\text{eff}}}{\delta}\bigl(C_{A,w}^{\text{in}} - C_{A,w}^{\text{out}}\bigr)$$

### 2.2.3 Shell-Side Boundary Condition

If a sweep gas (or vacuum) maintains $C_A^{\text{shell}} \approx 0$ (or a low value $C_{A,s}$), then:

$$C_A(R+\delta) = C_{A,s}$$

---

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are the **minimum toolkit** required to solve any KTU 2024 question on tubular permeation.

| # | Equation | Description / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = \mathcal{P}_A \bigl(C_{A,w}^{\text{in}} - C_{A,w}^{\text{out}}\bigr)$ | Permeation flux through wall (linear driving force) |
| 2 | $\mathcal{P}_A = P_A / \delta$ | Permeance from permeability |
| 3 | $\bar{v}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = -\dfrac{2\mathcal{P}_A}{R}\bigl(C_A - C_{A,s}\bigr)$ | 1-D axial model with constant shell-side concentration |
| 4 | $C_A(z) = C_{A,s} + (C_{A,0} - C_{A,s})\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2\mathcal{P}_A\,z}{R\,\bar{v}}\right)$ | Solution of (3); exponential decay along $z$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{C_{A,L} - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,0} - C_{A,s}} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2\mathcal{P}_A\,L}{R\,\bar{v}}\right)$ | Outlet–inlet relationship (key design equation) |
| 6 | $\text{Recovery} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{A,L}}{C_{A,0}}$ (when $C_{A,s}=0$) | Fractional removal of species A |
| 7 | $\text{LMTD}_\text{mass} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{A,\text{in}} - \Delta C_{A,\text{out}}}{\ln(\Delta C_{A,\text{in}} / \Delta C_{A,\text{out}})}$ | Log-mean concentration driving force |
| 8 | $A_{\text{mem}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_A^{\text{permeated}}}{\mathcal{P}_A \cdot \text{LMTD}_\text{mass}}$ | Required membrane area |
| 9 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_c\,D_t}{D_{AB}} = 1.86\,\text{Re}^{1/3}Sc^{1/3}\!\left(\dfrac{D_t}{L}\right)^{1/3}$ | Lévêque correlation for tube-side mass transfer (laminar, developing) |
| 10 | $N_{A,r} = \dfrac{D_{\text{eff}}}{R\,\ln(1+\delta/R)}\,\Delta C_A$ | Exact cylindrical wall flux |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In Eq. (3), the factor $2/R$ comes from the **area-to-volume ratio** of the tube (perimeter $= 2\pi R$, area $= \pi R^{2}$, ratio $= 2/R$). Students often write $1/R$ or miss the factor 2 — losing a full mark in derivations.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air separation:** O₂-enrichment using polyimide hollow fibres.
- **Natural gas sweetening:** CO₂/H₂S removal with cellulose acetate tubes.
- **Hydrogen recovery:** Pd-alloy tubular membranes in refineries.
- **Hemodialysis:** cellophane tubes mimic this geometry exactly.
- **Pharmaceutical pervaporation:** solvent dehydration tubes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Axial Plug-Flow Model with Permeation

### Step 1 — Differential Mass Balance on a Slice of Thickness $dz$

Take a cylindrical slice of the tube between $z$ and $z+dz$, of cross-section $\pi R^{2}$.

- **Inlet molar flow of A:** $\dot{n}_A^{\text{in}} = C_A(z)\,\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2}$
- **Outlet molar flow of A:** $\dot{n}_A^{\text{out}} = C_A(z+dz)\,\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2}$
- **Permeation through wall:** $\dot{n}_A^{\text{perm}} = N_A\,(2\pi R\,dz)$

Mass balance (steady state, no accumulation):

$$C_A(z)\,\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2} = C_A(z+dz)\,\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2} + N_A\,(2\pi R\,dz)$$

### Step 2 — Expand $C_A(z+dz)$ Using Taylor Series

$$C_A(z+dz) \approx C_A(z) + \frac{dC_A}{dz}\,dz$$

Substituting:

$$C_A\,\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2} = \left(C_A + \frac{dC_A}{dz}\,dz\right)\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2} + N_A\,(2\pi R\,dz)$$

The $C_A\,\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2}$ terms cancel:

$$0 = \frac{dC_A}{dz}\,\bar{v}\,\pi R^{2}\,dz + N_A\,(2\pi R\,dz)$$

Divide both sides by $\pi R^{2}\,dz$:

$$0 = \bar{v}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{2\,N_A}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Wall Permeation Flux

Using the linear driving-force model $N_A = \mathcal{P}_A\,(C_A - C_{A,s})$:

$$\bar{v}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} = -\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A}{R}\bigl(C_A - C_{A,s}\bigr)$$

This is **Equation (3)** of the cheat sheet.

### Step 4 — Solve the Linear ODE

Let $\theta(z) = C_A(z) - C_{A,s}$. Then $d\theta/dz = dC_A/dz$, and:

$$\bar{v}\,\frac{d\theta}{dz} = -\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A}{R}\,\theta$$

Separate variables:

$$\frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A}{R\,\bar{v}}\,dz$$

Integrate from $z = 0$ (where $\theta_0 = C_{A,0} - C_{A,s}$) to arbitrary $z$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\theta}{\theta_0}\right) = -\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A}{R\,\bar{v}}\,z$$

Exponentiate:

$$\frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,0} - C_{A,s}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A\,z}{R\,\bar{v}}\right)$$

This is **Equation (5)** — the central design equation.

### Step 5 — Evaluate at the Outlet $z = L$

At $z = L$, define $C_{A,L} = C_A(L)$:

$$\frac{C_{A,L} - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,0} - C_{A,s}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A\,L}{R\,\bar{v}}\right)$$

**Define the dimensionless permeation number:**

$$\text{NTU}_{\text{perm}} \equiv \frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A\,L}{R\,\bar{v}}$$

Then $C_{A,L} = C_{A,s} + (C_{A,0} - C_{A,s})\,e^{-\text{NTU}_{\text{perm}}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This NTU form is **analogous to a heat exchanger** (NTU = UA/Cmin) and to a chemical reactor (Damköhler number). Recognising this analogy is a favourite KTU question.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Worked-Out)

**Problem statement:** A tubular membrane of inner radius $R = 0.005$ m, length $L = 1$ m permeates oxygen from air. Feed enters with $C_{O_2,0} = 8.5$ mol·m⁻³. Permeance $\mathcal{P} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m·s⁻¹. Mean velocity $\bar{v} = 0.05$ m·s⁻¹. Shell-side oxygen concentration $C_{s} \approx 0$. Find outlet $C_{O_2}$ and recovery.

**Step 1 — Calculate the permeation number NTU:**

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{2\,\mathcal{P}\,L}{R\,\bar{v}} = \frac{2 \times 1.2\times10^{-4} \times 1}{0.005 \times 0.05}$$

Compute numerator: $2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$
Compute denominator: $0.005 \times 0.05 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.96$$

**Step 2 — Apply the design equation:**

$$\frac{C_L - 0}{8.5 - 0} = e^{-0.96}$$

$$e^{-0.96} \approx 0.3829$$

$$C_L = 8.5 \times 0.3829 \approx 3.255 \text{ mol·m}^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Recovery:**

$$\text{Recovery} = 1 - \frac{3.255}{8.5} = 1 - 0.3829 = 0.6171 \approx 61.7\%$$

**Answer:** Outlet $C_{O_2,L} \approx 3.25$ mol·m⁻³; **O₂ recovery ≈ 61.7%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation check:** Recovery is between 0 and 1 ✓. Outlet concentration is between 0 and inlet ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wall Flux in Cylindrical Geometry (Exact Form)

The wall-side steady-state diffusion equation is:

$$0 = \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right)$$

Integrate once:

$$r\,\frac{dC_A}{dr} = A_1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dC_A}{dr} = \frac{A_1}{r}$$

Integrate again:

$$C_A(r) = A_1 \ln r + A_2$$

Apply BCs:
- At $r = R$: $C_A = C_{A}^{\text{in}}$
- At $r = R + \delta$: $C_A = C_{A}^{\text{out}}$

Solving the two linear equations yields:

$$A_1 = \frac{C_A^{\text{in}} - C_A^{\text{out}}}{\ln\!\left(\frac{R+\delta}{R}\right)} = \frac{C_A^{\text{in}} - C_A^{\text{out}}}{\ln(1 + \delta/R)}$$

The radial flux is $N_{A,r} = -D_{\text{eff}}\,(dC_A/dr) = -D_{\text{eff}}\,A_1/r$:

$$N_{A,r}(r) = -\frac{D_{\text{eff}}}{r\,\ln(1+\delta/R)}\bigl(C_A^{\text{in}} - C_A^{\text{out}}\bigr)$$

The flux is **inversely proportional to $r$** — higher near the inner wall, lower near the outer wall (Fick's first law conservation in cylindrical geometry). The average flux over the wall thickness is the value at the logarithmic-mean radius $r_{lm} = (R+\delta - R)/\ln[(R+\delta)/R]$:

$$\overline{N}_{A,r} = \frac{D_{\text{eff}}}{R\,\ln(1+\delta/R)}\,\Delta C_A$$

This is **Equation (10)** of the cheat sheet. For $\delta \ll R$, $\ln(1+\delta/R) \approx \delta/R$, recovering the flat-membrane form $N_A \approx (D_{\text{eff}}/\delta)\,\Delta C_A$.

---

## 3.4 Combined (Overall) Mass Transfer Coefficient

Often, three resistances act in series:
1. Tube-side convective resistance: $1/k_c$
2. Wall resistance: $1/\mathcal{P}_A = \delta/D_{\text{eff}}$
3. Shell-side convective resistance: $1/k_s$

**Overall coefficient:**

$$\frac{1}{K_{\text{ov}}} = \frac{1}{k_c} + \frac{1}{\mathcal{P}_A} + \frac{1}{k_s}$$

Then $N_A = K_{\text{ov}}\,\Delta C_{A,\text{overall}}$, and the model equations (3)–(5) hold with $\mathcal{P}_A$ replaced by $K_{\text{ov}}$.

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions frequently test **identifying the controlling resistance** — e.g., "if the membrane is dense, $\mathcal{P}_A$ is small and dominates."

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Schematic of a Tubular Permeator

The figure below shows a *block-level functional architecture* of a single tubular membrane permeator, including the controlling transport phenomena and the boundary conditions used in the model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedIn["FEED INLET C_A0 high"] -->|axial flow| TubeLumen
    TubeLumen["TUBE LUMEN 0 less than r less than R"] -->|permeation flux N_A| Wall
    Wall["MEMBRANE WALL R less than r less than R plus delta"] -->|radial diffusion| ShellSide
    ShellSide["SHELL SIDE sweep or vacuum C_As small"] -->|sweep outlet| PermOut["PERMEATE OUTLET rich in A"]
    TubeLumen -->|axial flow| RetOut["RETENTATE OUTLET C_AL small"]
    SweepIn["SWEEP INLET"] --> ShellSide
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- The **left→right** axis represents the *axial* coordinate $z$.
- The **top→bottom** axis represents the *radial* coordinate $r$.
- Three parallel transport zones (lumen, wall, shell) are coupled by the **permeation flux** $N_A$ at the inner and outer wall surfaces.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Equations Coupling the Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Tube side mass balance v dC/dz = -2 N_A over R"] -->|N_A is boundary condition| M2
    M2["Wall diffusion 0 = 1 over r d over dr r dC over dr"] -->|C_A_in, C_A_out boundary| M3
    M3["Overall flux N_A = P over delta C_in - C_out"] -->|substitute back| M1
    M3 -->|drives| M4["Combined ODE dC_A over dz = - 2 P over R v C_A - C_As"]
    M4 -->|integrate with BC| M5["Solution C_A of z = C_As + C_A0 - C_As e to power -2 P z over R v"]
    M5 -->|evaluate at z equals L| M6["Design equation C_A,L - C_As = e to power -2 P L over R v times C_A0 - C_As"]
    M6 --> M7["Outputs recovery, area, NTU"]
```

---
