# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "permeance" of a membrane. How is it related to permeability?**

**Model Answer:**
> Permeance $\mathcal{P}_A$ of a membrane for species A is defined as the **steady-state molar flux of A per unit driving force** (concentration or partial pressure difference) across the membrane. Mathematically,
> $$\mathcal{P}_A = \frac{N_A}{\Delta C_A} \quad [\text{m·s}^{-1}]$$
> It is related to the intrinsic permeability $P_A$ by $\mathcal{P}_A = P_A / \delta$, where $\delta$ is the membrane thickness. Thus permeance is a *practical* quantity that combines material property ($P_A$) with geometry ($\delta$).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Fick's first law of diffusion. Write the expression for steady-state molar flux through a cylindrical membrane wall of inner radius $R$ and outer radius $R+\delta$.**

**Model Answer:**
> Fick's first law states that the molar flux of a species by molecular diffusion is proportional to the negative gradient of its concentration: $N_A = -D_{AB}\,\nabla C_A$.
> For a cylindrical membrane wall, applying Fick's law in the radial direction and integrating between $r = R$ and $r = R+\delta$ gives the average flux:
> $$\overline{N}_{A,r} = \frac{D_{\text{eff}}}{R\,\ln(1+\delta/R)}\bigl(C_A^{\text{in}} - C_A^{\text{out}}\bigr)$$
> For thin walls ($\delta \ll R$), this reduces to $\overline{N}_{A,r} \approx (D_{\text{eff}}/\delta)\,\Delta C_A$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A tubular membrane permeator is used to recover species **A** from a gas mixture. The following data are given:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Inner tube radius $R$ | 0.01 m |
| Wall thickness $\delta$ | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m |
| Permeability $P_A$ | $3 \times 10^{-9}$ mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ |
| Feed inlet concentration $C_{A,0}$ | 10 mol·m⁻³ |
| Shell-side concentration $C_{A,s}$ | 0.5 mol·m⁻³ |
| Mean axial velocity $\bar{v}$ | 0.10 m·s⁻¹ |
| Length $L$ | 2 m |
| Total molar flow $\dot{n}_{\text{tot}}$ | 0.05 mol·s⁻¹ |

Assume isothermal, steady-state, plug flow, ideal gas behaviour.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the governing differential equation for $C_A(z)$ along the tube and obtain its analytical solution. Clearly state all assumptions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state: $\partial/\partial t = 0$
2. Isothermal operation
3. Plug flow on tube side (uniform $\bar{v}$)
4. Constant permeance $\mathcal{P}_A$
5. Constant shell-side concentration $C_{A,s}$
6. Negligible radial gradients in the lumen (well-mixed radially)

**Mass balance on a slice $dz$:** as derived in Section 3.1, we obtain

$$\bar{v}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} = -\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A}{R}\bigl(C_A - C_{A,s}\bigr)$$

with $\mathcal{P}_A = P_A / \delta = 3\times 10^{-9}/5\times 10^{-5} = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ (treated as a lumped permeance with consistent units; for KTU we accept the value as given).

Setting $\theta = C_A - C_{A,s}$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dz} = -\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A}{R\,\bar{v}}\,\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta(z) = \theta_0\,e^{-2\mathcal{P}_A z/(R\bar{v})}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_A(z) = C_{A,s} + (C_{A,0} - C_{A,s})\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A\,z}{R\,\bar{v}}\right)\;}$$

**Marks split:** Assumptions (2), differential balance (2), integration (2), final form (1).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute (i) the outlet concentration $C_{A,L}$, (ii) the fractional recovery of A, and (iii) the molar flow rate of A permeated.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Outlet concentration:**

The permeation number:
$$\text{NTU} = \frac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A\,L}{R\,\bar{v}} = \frac{2 \times 6\times10^{-5} \times 2}{0.01 \times 0.10}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$
Denominator: $0.01 \times 0.10 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{2.4\times10^{-4}}{1.0\times10^{-3}} = 0.24$$

$$C_{A,L} = 0.5 + (10 - 0.5)\,e^{-0.24} = 0.5 + 9.5 \times 0.7866 = 0.5 + 7.473 = 7.973 \text{ mol·m}^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{C_{A,L} \approx 7.97 \text{ mol·m}^{-3}}$$

**(ii) Fractional recovery (relative to inlet):**

$$\text{Recovery} = \frac{C_{A,0} - C_{A,L}}{C_{A,0} - C_{A,s}} = \frac{10 - 7.973}{10 - 0.5} = \frac{2.027}{9.5} = 0.2133$$

$$\boxed{\text{Recovery} \approx 21.3\%}$$

**(iii) Molar flow of A permeated:**

Assuming total flow ≈ 0.05 mol·s⁻¹ and inlet mole fraction $y_0 = C_{A,0}/C_{\text{tot}}$. We interpret the question as the *molar rate of A removed from the tube*:

$$\dot{n}_{A,\text{perm}} = \dot{n}_{\text{tot}} \cdot (C_{A,0} - C_{A,L})/C_{\text{tot,ref}} \cdot \text{volumetric correction}$$

At KTU level, take the simple per-stream interpretation:
$$\dot{n}_{A,\text{perm}} = 0.05 \times 0.2133 = 0.01066 \text{ mol·s}^{-1}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{n}_{A,\text{perm}} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol·s}^{-1}}$$

**Marks split:** NTU (2), outlet concentration (2), recovery (1.5), molar flow (1.5).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A **cylindrical porous membrane tube** of inner radius $R = 0.008$ m and outer radius $R_o = 0.0085$ m is used for oxygen separation. The effective diffusivity in the wall is $D_{\text{eff}} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m²·s⁻¹. The inner wall oxygen concentration is $C_{A}^{\text{in}} = 9$ mol·m⁻³ and the outer wall concentration is $C_{A}^{\text{out}} = 1$ mol·m⁻³.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the steady-state concentration profile $C_A(r)$ inside the cylindrical membrane wall and the average permeation flux $\overline{N}_{A,r}$.**

**Model Solution:**

Governing equation: $0 = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dC_A}{dr}\right)$

First integration: $r\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dr} = A_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{dC_A}{dr} = \dfrac{A_1}{r}$

Second integration: $C_A(r) = A_1 \ln r + A_2$

Apply BCs:
- $C_A(R) = 9$: $\quad 9 = A_1 \ln R + A_2$
- $C_A(R_o) = 1$: $\quad 1 = A_1 \ln R_o + A_2$

Subtract:
$$9 - 1 = A_1\,(\ln R - \ln R_o) = -A_1\,\ln(R_o/R)$$
$$A_1 = -\frac{8}{\ln(R_o/R)} = -\frac{8}{\ln(0.0085/0.008)} = -\frac{8}{\ln(1.0625)} = -\frac{8}{0.0606} \approx -132.0$$

$$A_2 = 9 - A_1 \ln R = 9 - (-132.0)\,\ln(0.008) = 9 + 132.0 \times 4.8283 \approx 9 + 637.4 = 646.4$$

$$\boxed{C_A(r) = -132.0\,\ln r + 646.4 \quad (R \le r \le R_o)}$$

Average flux: $\overline{N}_{A,r} = \dfrac{D_{\text{eff}}}{R\,\ln(1+\delta/R)}\,\Delta C_A$

With $\delta = R_o - R = 0.0005$ m, $\delta/R = 0.0625$, $\ln(1.0625) = 0.0606$:

$$\overline{N}_{A,r} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.008 \times 0.0606} \times (9 - 1)$$

$$= \frac{2\times 10^{-6}}{4.85 \times 10^{-4}} \times 8 = 4.12 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 = 0.0330 \text{ mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{N}_{A,r} \approx 3.30 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}}$$

**Marks split:** Wall ODE (2), integration (2), BC application (1.5), average flux (1.5).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Define the overall mass transfer coefficient $K_{\text{ov}}$ for a tubular permeator with three resistances in series. If the tube-side coefficient $k_c = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m·s⁻¹, the wall permeance $\mathcal{P}_A = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}$ m·s⁻¹, and the shell-side coefficient $k_s = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ m·s⁻¹, identify the controlling resistance and compute $K_{\text{ov}}$.**

**Model Solution:**

The overall coefficient is defined by the resistances-in-series addition:

$$\frac{1}{K_{\text{ov}}} = \frac{1}{k_c} + \frac{1}{\mathcal{P}_A} + \frac{1}{k_s}$$

Compute each term:
- $1/k_c = 1 / (5 \times 10^{-4}) = 2000$ s·m⁻¹
- $1/\mathcal{P}_A = 1 / (1.6 \times 10^{-4}) = 6250$ s·m⁻¹
- $1/k_s = 1 / (8 \times 10^{-4}) = 1250$ s·m⁻¹

Sum: $1/K_{\text{ov}} = 2000 + 6250 + 1250 = 9500$ s·m⁻¹

$$K_{\text{ov}} = \frac{1}{9500} \approx 1.053 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m·s}^{-1}$$

**Controlling resistance:** $1/\mathcal{P}_A = 6250$ s·m⁻¹ is the largest individual term (≈ 65.8% of total). Therefore **the wall (membrane) resistance is rate-controlling**.

**Insight:** A thicker or less-permeable membrane would make this worse. Design improvement: use a thinner, higher-permeability membrane, OR increase $k_c$ by using a turbulent promoter.

**Marks split:** Definition (2), numerical evaluation (2), identification of controlling resistance (2), design insight (1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on Tubular Permeation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2/R** in the axial ODE — students often write $1/R$ or $4/R$. Remember: area-to-volume ratio of a cylinder is $\mathbf{2/R}$.
> 2. **Using $C_{A,s} = 0$ without justification** — KTU examiners expect a statement like "shell-side sweep gas at high flowrate maintains $C_{A,s} \approx 0$."
> 3. **Confusing permeability $P_A$ with permeance $\mathcal{P}_A$** — units differ. $P_A$ has an extra factor of length; $\mathcal{P}_A = P_A/\delta$.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption list** — KTU 2024 Scheme weights the *modelling* step (assumptions + differential balance) at **≥ 30%** of the 14 marks.
> 5. **Sign errors in cylindrical wall flux** — Fick's law is $N = -D\,\nabla C$; in cylindrical coordinates with $r$ increasing outward, the flux is negative (outward). Be careful with sign convention.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify units in the final answer** — always state mol·m⁻³ for concentration, mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ for flux.
> 7. **Using $L$ (length) where $z$ (variable) is needed** in the design equation — these are different.
> 8. **Ignoring radial diffusion in the lumen** — at very low $Pe$, this assumption fails. If asked, mention Lévêque (Eq. 9 in cheat sheet) for the more accurate tube-side mass transfer coefficient.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tubular Permeation Process**

- [ ] Tubular permeation = **convective axial flow + radial diffusion through a cylindrical wall**.
- [ ] **Tube-side governing equation:** $\bar{v}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = -\dfrac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A}{R}\,(C_A - C_{A,s})$.
- [ ] **Wall-side governing equation:** $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dr}\right) = 0$ → $C_A(r) = A_1 \ln r + A_2$.
- [ ] **Solution on tube side:** $C_A(z) = C_{A,s} + (C_{A,0} - C_{A,s})\,e^{-2\mathcal{P}_A z/(R\bar{v})}$.
- [ ] **Design number:** $\text{NTU}_{\text{perm}} = \dfrac{2\,\mathcal{P}_A\,L}{R\,\bar{v}}$.
- [ ] **Cylindrical wall average flux:** $\overline{N}_{A,r} = \dfrac{D_{\text{eff}}}{R\,\ln(1+\delta/R)}\,\Delta C_A$.
- [ ] **Overall resistance:** $\dfrac{1}{K_{\text{ov}}} = \dfrac{1}{k_c} + \dfrac{1}{\mathcal{P}_A} + \dfrac{1}{k_s}$.
- [ ] **Planar-wall limit:** for $\delta \ll R$, $\ln(1+\delta/R) \approx \delta/R$, recover $N_A = (D_{\text{eff}}/\delta)\,\Delta C_A$.
- [ ] **Key analogy:** tubular permeator ↔ co-current heat exchanger / PFR with first-order sink.
- [ ] **Units to remember:** $[P_A] = $ Barrer = $3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹.
- [ ] **Boundary conditions to write in every exam:** $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$; $C_A(R) = C_{A}^{\text{in}}$; $C_A(R+\delta) = C_{A,s}$.
- [ ] **Recovery formula:** $\eta = (C_{A,0} - C_{A,L}) / (C_{A,0} - C_{A,s})$.
- [ ] **Lévêque correlation** (tube-side $k_c$): $Sh = 1.86\,Re^{1/3}\,Sc^{1/3}(D_t/L)^{1/3}$ — write it down when "tube-side mass transfer" is asked.
- [ ] **Controlling-resistance rule:** largest of $\{1/k_c, 1/\mathcal{P}_A, 1/k_s\}$ dictates the rate.
- [ ] **Final numerical answer must always carry correct units** — examiner's rule #1 in PECHT631.

> **End of Notes — Tubular Permeation Process | Module 3 | PECHT631**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Absorber - Simulation Using MATLAB/SciLab or Any Other Process Simulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Absorber - Simulation Using MATLAB/SciLab or Any Other Process Simulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Absorption (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Gas absorption is a **diffusion-controlled mass transfer unit operation** in which one or more soluble components of a gas mixture are selectively dissolved into a *non-volatile liquid solvent*. The process is governed by simultaneous **interphase mass transfer** and **thermodynamic phase equilibrium**, and is industrially applied in CO₂ capture, H₂S removal (amine treating), SO₂ scrubbing, and natural gas sweetening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Simulation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Process simulation is the **mathematical modelling of a chemical process** using a set of governing equations (material, energy, equilibrium, and rate balances — collectively termed the *MESH equations*), followed by their **numerical solution** on a digital computer (MATLAB / SciLab / Aspen Plus) to predict steady-state or dynamic column profiles of composition, temperature, and flow rate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **wet sponge in a smoky room**. The smoke (soluble gas) drifts into the sponge (liquid solvent) and gets trapped inside its damp fibers (absorbed). The fresher the sponge and the longer the contact, the more smoke is removed. A **gas absorber** works identically: a *lean* solvent enters at the top, a *dirty* gas enters at the bottom, and they flow counter-currently across multiple trays, transferring the pollutant (e.g., CO₂) from the gas into the liquid. The "sponge" becomes "spent" (rich solvent) and the gas becomes "clean" (treated gas).

| Engineering Quantity | Sponge Analogy | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Inlet gas flow | Smoky air entering | $G$ |
| Solvent flow | Fresh water poured on sponge | $L$ |
| Solute (CO₂) in gas | Smoke particles | $y$ |
| Solute in liquid | Dirt absorbed in sponge | $x$ |
| Number of trays | Layers of sponge | $N$ |

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Must Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard reference values** appear frequently in KTU numerical problems and university examinations:
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
> - **Standard pressure:** $P^{\circ} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$
> - **Standard temperature:** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \, \text{K}$
> - **Henry's constant for CO₂ in water at 25 °C:** $H_{CO_2} = 29.4 \, \text{atm} \cdot \text{kg-mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^{3}$
> - **Universal-Quasi-Chemical (UNIQUAC) / NRTL models** are the standard thermodynamic options for non-ideal systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The MESH Equation Framework (Foundational Theory)

Every tray (or stage) of a gas absorber is described by **four** sets of conservation laws, collectively known as the **MESH equations** — the bedrock of KTU Module 3.

### 2.1.1 M — Material Balance (Component $i$ on stage $j$)

For a **non-reactive absorption** of a single transferable component on tray $j$:

$$L_{j-1} x_{j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{j+1} - L_j x_j - V_j y_j = 0$$

where $L$ is the liquid molar flow and $V$ is the vapour molar flow.

### 2.1.2 E — Phase Equilibrium Relation

At thermodynamic equilibrium, the gas and liquid compositions are linked by an **equilibrium curve** $y^{*}_j = f(x_j)$. For dilute systems, **Henry's Law** applies:

$$y^{*}_j = m \, x_j = \frac{P}{H} \, x_j$$

> **Note:** The **Henry's constant** $H$ has units of **atm** and $m$ is dimensionless.

### 2.1.3 S — Summation Equations (Total Flow Balances)

On each tray, the mole fractions must sum to unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{i,j} = 1 \quad ; \quad \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j} = 1$$

### 2.1.4 H — Heat (Energy) Balance

$$L_{j-1} h_{j-1} + V_{j+1} H_{j+1} - L_j h_j - V_j H_j = 0$$

For **isothermal absorbers** (the typical KTU 2024 Module 3 assumption), this equation is decoupled and ignored.

## 2.2 Two-Film Theory & Mass Transfer Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Two-Film Theory (Lewis & Whitman, 1923)** is the standard rate model in KTU 2024 Process Modelling. It assumes **stagnant films** of thickness $\delta$ on both sides of the gas–liquid interface, with mass transfer occurring by **steady-state molecular diffusion**.

The flux of solute across the interface is expressed as:

$$N_A = K_y (y - y^{*}) = K_x (x^{*} - x)$$

where the **overall mass transfer coefficients** are related to individual-film coefficients via the *additivity-of-resistance* principle:

$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$$

## 2.3 Kremser Equation (Dilute / Shortcut Method)

> [!NOTE]
> For **dilute, isothermal, constant $L$ and $V$** absorbers, the **Kremser equation** allows a direct closed-form solution without solving MESH numerically. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark short question** topic.

For an absorber with $N$ theoretical stages, solvent-to-gas ratio $A = L / mV$, and absorption factor:

$$\text{Absorption} = \frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

## 2.4 Numerical Solution Approach (Simulation Strategy)

Since MESH equations are **coupled, non-linear, and algebraic**, KTU 2024 Module 3 mandates that students simulate using:

1. **Stage-by-stage calculation** (Lewis-Matheson) — for ideal systems.
2. **ODE formulation** (relaxation/equation-oriented method) — for rigorous, general cases.
3. **Matrix methods** (Thomas algorithm for tridiagonal systems).
4. **MATLAB ode45 / SciLab ode** for dynamic or staged ODE systems.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following **master table** is the minimum every KTU 2024 student must memorise for the ESE.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material balance on stage $j$ | $L_{j-1} x_{j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{j+1} = L_j x_j + V_j y_j$ | mol/s |
| 2 | Henry's Law | $y = m x$ with $m = P / H$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Two-film overall coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | s/m |
| 4 | Mass transfer flux | $N_A = K_y (y - y^{*})$ | mol/(m²·s) |
| 5 | Kremser absorption | $\dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Absorption factor | $A = L / (mV)$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Stage efficiency (Murphree) | $E_{M,j} = \dfrac{y_{j} - y_{j+1}}{y^{*}_{j} - y_{j+1}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Operating line (top section) | $y_{j+1} = \dfrac{L}{V} x_j + \dfrac{V_1 y_1 - L_0 x_0}{V}$ | mol frac |
| 9 | Total condenser/dummy stage | $y_1 = K x_1$ | mol frac |
| 10 | Ideal-gas volumetric flow | $V = \dfrac{n_{total} RT}{P}$ | m³/s |

> **Real-world utility:** These equations form the *backbone* of column design in **Aspen Plus, HYSYS, CHEMCAD, ProMax, and gPROMS** — the simulators used by every refinery, petrochemical, and carbon-capture plant in the world.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stage Material Balance Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — A Generic Equilibrium Stage

Consider an **equilibrium stage $j$** of a gas absorber as shown in MESH framework. The streams entering tray $j$ are:

- **Vapour** $V_{j+1}$ from tray $j+1$ below, with mole fraction $y_{j+1}$ of solute.
- **Liquid** $L_{j-1}$ from tray $j-1$ above, with mole fraction $x_{j-1}$ of solute.

The streams leaving tray $j$ are:

- **Vapour** $V_j$ leaving upward, with mole fraction $y_j$.
- **Liquid** $L_j$ leaving downward, with mole fraction $x_j$.

### 3.1.2 Component Mass Balance

> **Step 1:** Apply the steady-state **component material balance** for the solute (call it $i = A$, e.g. CO₂):

$$
\text{Input rate of A} = \text{Output rate of A}
$$

> **Step 2:** Write the fluxes in terms of flow and composition:

$$V_{j+1} y_{j+1} + L_{j-1} x_{j-1} = V_j y_j + L_j x_j$$

> **Step 3:** Rearrange by collecting input terms on the left and output terms on the right:

$$L_{j-1} x_{j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{j+1} - L_j x_j - V_j y_j = 0$$

> **Step 4:** For the **total molar balance** (sum over all components), since $\sum x = \sum y = 1$:

$$L_{j-1} + V_{j+1} = L_j + V_j$$

This is the **Total Material Balance (M-T)** which, combined with the component balance, defines the **operating line** of the stage.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Operating Line

### 3.2.1 Starting Point — Solve the Two Balances

> **Step 1:** From the total balance, express $V_j$:

$$V_j = L_{j-1} + V_{j+1} - L_j$$

> **Step 2:** Substitute into the component balance:

$$L_{j-1} x_{j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{j+1} = x_j (L_{j-1} + V_{j+1} - L_j) + y_j V_j$$

> **Step 3:** Assume **constant molar overflow (CMO)** — i.e. $L_j = L$ and $V_j = V$ for all $j$. This decouples the energy balance and is the KTU 2024 standard assumption for isothermal absorbers.

> **Step 4:** Under CMO, the operating line for an **envelope** taken from the top of the column to stage $j$ becomes:

$$y_{j+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_j + \frac{V_1 y_1 - L_0 x_0}{V}$$

> **Step 5:** Recognise this as a **straight line** in the $(x, y)$ plane with slope $L/V$ and intercept $\dfrac{V_1 y_1 - L_0 x_0}{V}$.

> **Step 6:** For the **entire column envelope** (top of gas $V_1, y_1$ to bottom of gas $V_{N+1}, y_{N+1}$):

$$V_{N+1} - V_1 = L_N - L_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{N+1} = V_1$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Kremser Equation (Rigorous)

### 3.3.1 Recursive Stage-by-Stage Substitution

> **Step 1:** Write the material balance for the *top stage* under CMO:

$$L(x_0 - x_1) = V(y_1 - y_2) \quad \text{or} \quad y_2 = y_1 - A(x_1 - x_0)$$

where $A = L/(mV)$ and we used Henry's law $y_1^* = m x_1$, with the assumption that $y_1 = y_1^*$ at equilibrium.

> **Step 2:** For stage 2, the analogous relation is:

$$y_3 = y_1 - A(x_1 - x_0) - A(x_2 - x_1)$$

> **Step 3:** Substitute $x_2 = y_2 / m$ and $x_1 = y_1 / m$:

$$y_3 = y_1 - A\left(\frac{y_2 - y_1}{m}\right) - A\left(\frac{y_1 - y_1}{m}\right)$$

> **Step 4:** Continue this recursion down to stage $N$, yielding the **closed-form Kremser solution**:

$$y_{N+1} - y_1 = A \left(\frac{y_N - m x_0}{m}\right)$$

> **Step 5:** Solving this geometric series rigorously:

$$y_{N+1} - y_1 = A^{N+1} (y_1 - m x_0) - A (y_1 - m x_0)$$

> **Step 6:** Rearranging gives the canonical **Kremser absorption formula**:

$$
\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}
$$

> **Step 7:** This expression is the **fraction of the maximum possible absorption** actually achieved. As $A \to \infty$ (infinite solvent), the RHS $\to 1$ (complete absorption). As $N \to \infty$, the RHS $\to 1$ (infinite stages).

## 3.4 ODE Formulation for Dynamic Simulation (KTU 2024 Module 3 Special)

### 3.4.1 Continuous Differential Mass Balance

> **Step 1:** Replace the discrete tray index $j$ with a continuous height $z$ (height of packing, in metres). The gas and liquid flow become differential.

> **Step 2:** Write the **differential component balance** for the gas phase (assuming liquid flow is continuous and molar fluxes are $G$ and $L$ per unit area):

$$-G \frac{dy}{dz} = K_y a (y - y^{*})$$

> **Step 3:** Use Henry's law $y^{*} = m x$, but $x$ is related to $y$ via a gas-phase material balance from the *top* of the column:

$$L(x_{in} - x) = G(y - y_{out})$$

> **Step 4:** Solve the two equations simultaneously. Substituting $x$ in terms of $y$:

$$x = x_{in} - \frac{G}{L}(y - y_{out})$$

> **Step 5:** Substituting into the differential equation:

$$-G \frac{dy}{dz} = K_y a \left[ y - m x_{in} + m \frac{G}{L}(y - y_{out}) \right]$$

> **Step 6:** Grouping the $y$ terms:

$$-\frac{dy}{dz} = \frac{K_y a}{G} \left[ y \left( 1 + \frac{mG}{L} \right) - m x_{in} - \frac{mG}{L} y_{out} \right]$$

> **Step 7:** Define the **NTU** (Number of Transfer Units):

$$NTU = \int_0^H \frac{K_y a}{G} dz = \frac{K_y a H}{G}$$

> **Step 8:** The **separation factor** $\beta = mG / L = 1/A$ enters. The final **integrated form** is the *Colburn analogy*:

$$NTU = \int_{y_{in}}^{y_{out}} \frac{(1-y)_{lm} \, dy}{(1-y)(y - y^*)}$$

where $(1-y)_{lm}$ is the **log-mean correction** for high concentrations. This integral is solved numerically using **MATLAB's `ode45` or SciLab's `ode` solver**, marching from $z = 0$ to $z = H$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A gas absorber has 4 theoretical stages, $L = 80 \, \text{kmol/h}$, $V = 100 \, \text{kmol/h}$, $m = 0.8$, $y_{N+1} = 0.04$, $x_0 = 0.001$. Compute $y_1$ using Kremser.

> **Step 1:** Compute absorption factor:

$$A = \frac{L}{mV} = \frac{80}{0.8 \times 100} = 1.0$$

> **Step 2:** Compute the numerator $A^{N+1} - A$:

$$A^{N+1} - A = 1^{5} - 1 = 0$$

> **Step 3:** This indicates $A = 1$ is the *break-even* condition. The gas leaves unchanged. We need $A > 1$ for absorption. So the **minimum solvent flow** is $L_{min} = mV = 80 \, \text{kmol/h}$. Use $L = 120 \, \text{kmol/h}$:

$$A = \frac{120}{0.8 \times 100} = 1.5$$

> **Step 4:** Recompute:

$$A^{N+1} - A = 1.5^{5} - 1.5 = 7.5938 - 1.5 = 6.0938$$

$$A^{N+1} - 1 = 7.5938 - 1 = 6.5938$$

$$\text{Absorption} = \frac{6.0938}{6.5938} = 0.9241$$

> **Step 5:** Compute $y_1$:

$$y_1 = y_{N+1} - 0.9241 \times (y_{N+1} - m x_0) = 0.04 - 0.9241 \times (0.04 - 0.0008) = 0.04 - 0.03624 = 0.00376$$

> **Conclusion:** The CO₂ in the gas leaving the top is reduced from **4 mol%** to **0.376 mol%**, i.e. a **90.6% removal** efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Gas Absorber Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Gas V_N+1 y_N+1] -->|Bottom Inlet| B[Stage N Tray N]
    B --> C[Stage N-1]
    C --> D[Stage N-2]
    D --> E[Stage 2]
    E --> F[Stage 1 Top Tray]
    F -->|Treated Gas Outlet| G[Exit Gas V1 y1]
    H[Lean Solvent L0 x0] -->|Top Inlet| F
    F -->|Rich Solvent Downflow| E
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B -->|Rich Solvent Outlet| I[Exit Liquid LN xN]
    J[MESH Solver MATLAB ode45] -.->|Reads States| B
    J -.->|Reads States| C
    J -.->|Reads States| D
    J -.->|Reads States| E
    J -.->|Reads States| F
    K[Equilibrium Model ystar = m times x] -.->|Provides ystar| J
    L[Mass Transfer Kya Kxa] -.->|Provides Rates| J
```

## 4.2 Simulation Algorithm Flowchart (MATLAB / SciLab Procedure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Simulation] --> S2[Input Parameters L V N m yN1 x0 H Kya]
    S2 --> S3[Initialize Vectors y and x size N+1]
    S3 --> S4[Set Boundary Conditions y at N+1 and x at 0]
    S4 --> S5{Choose Solver ode45 vs fsolve}
    S5 -->|Dynamic| S6[Formulate ODE dy dz equals f y z]
    S5 -->|Steady| S7[Formulate MESH as F y x equals 0]
    S6 --> S8[Integrate from z equals 0 to H]
    S7 --> S9[Newton Raphson Iteration]
    S8 --> S10[Check Convergence y tolerance 1e-6]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 -->|Converged| S11[Plot Composition Profiles y versus stage and x versus stage]
    S10 -->|Not Converged| S5
    S11 --> S12[Compute Removal Efficiency and NTU]
    S12 --> S13[Print Report and Stop]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Simulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GUI Input Panel] --> B[Thermodynamic Property Database]
    A --> C[Column Geometry Module]
    B --> D[MESH Equation Builder]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Numerical Solver Core]
    E --> F[Output Plot Module]
    F --> G[Composition Profile Plot]
    F --> H[NTU versus H Curve]
    F --> I[Removal Efficiency Bar Chart]
    E --> J[Convergence Monitor]
    J -->|Residual 1e-6| E
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a "block diagram of a process simulator", always include the **three core blocks**: *Property Database → Equation Builder → Numerical Solver → Output Module*. Examiners award full marks only if all four blocks are labelled clearly with arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the MESH equations used in simulating a gas absorber. State the significance of each letter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The MESH equations are the **four fundamental sets of equations** solved at every stage of a separation column to determine composition and flow profiles:

- **M — Material Balance:** Conservation of mass for each component on each tray. (1 Mark)
- **E — Equilibrium Relation:** Thermodynamic link between the vapour and liquid leaving an equilibrium stage, $y^{*} = f(x)$. (1 Mark)
- **S — Summation Equations:** Constraint that mole fractions of all components sum to unity in each phase. (0.5 Marks)
- **H — Heat (Energy) Balance:** Conservation of enthalpy across each stage, which is decoupled under the isothermal / CMO assumption. (0.5 Marks)

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the Kremser equation for an isothermal gas absorber and explain the physical meaning of the absorption factor $A$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Kremser equation for an isothermal absorber with $N$ theoretical stages is:

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

where **A = L / (mV)** is the **absorption factor** (1 Mark). Physically, $A$ represents the **ratio of the slope of the operating line to the slope of the equilibrium line** in the McCabe-Thiele diagram (1 Mark). When $A > 1$, the operating line lies above the equilibrium curve, and absorption is feasible; when $A = 1$, the two lines coincide (pinch point); when $A < 1$, the operating line lies below the equilibrium curve and absorption is impossible (1 Mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and the student must answer **either Option A or Option B** (internal choice). Each option is divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

> **Option A (a) [7 Marks]:** *Derive the MESH equations for a generic equilibrium stage of a gas absorber operating under the constant molar overflow (CMO) assumption. Clearly state the role of Henry's law in the E equation.*

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Draw a generic stage $j$ with streams $L_{j-1}, x_{j-1}$ entering from above and $V_{j+1}, y_{j+1}$ entering from below, with $L_j, x_j$ and $V_j, y_j$ leaving.

> **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Apply component material balance (the M equation):

$$L_{j-1} x_{j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{j+1} = L_j x_j + V_j y_j$$

> **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply the E equation using Henry's law for the solute at equilibrium:

$$y_j^{*} = m x_j, \quad m = \frac{P}{H}$$

> **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Write the S equations $\sum y_{i,j} = 1$ and $\sum x_{i,j} = 1$.

> **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Under CMO, the H equation reduces to $L_j = L$ and $V_j = V$, decoupling the energy balance.

> **Step 6 (1 Mark):** Substitute into the M equation to obtain the operating line:

$$y_{j+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_j + \left( y_1 - \frac{L}{V} x_0 \right)$$

The role of Henry's law is to *close* the MESH system by providing a thermodynamic relationship between the unknown $x_j$ and $y_j$.

---

> **Option A (b) [7 Marks]:** *A gas absorber is to remove 95% of CO₂ from a flue gas stream using a monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent. The gas flow is $V = 1000 \, \text{kmol/h}$ with $y_{CO_2, in} = 0.15$, the lean solvent flow is $L = 5000 \, \text{kmol/h}$ with $x_{CO_2, in} = 0.005$, and the Henry's-law slope is $m = 0.82$. Calculate (i) the minimum number of stages for a 95% removal using the Kremser equation, and (ii) the exit gas composition for $N = 4$ stages.*

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute the target exit composition. 95% removal implies $y_1 = 0.15 \times 0.05 = 0.0075$.

> **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the absorption factor:

$$A = \frac{L}{mV} = \frac{5000}{0.82 \times 1000} = 6.0976$$

> **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Invert Kremser to solve for the minimum stages. We require:

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} = \frac{19}{20} = 0.95$$

Substitute:

$$\frac{0.15 - 0.0075}{0.15 - 0.82 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.1425}{0.1459} = 0.9767$$

Set this equal to the Kremser RHS and solve numerically:

$$0.9767 = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

Iterating with $N = 3$ gives $0.9496$; with $N = 4$ gives $0.9874$. So **$N_{min} = 4$ stages** (rounded up).

> **Step 4 (2 Marks):** For $N = 4$ with $A = 6.0976$:

$$A^{N+1} - A = 6.0976^{5} - 6.0976 = 8393.6 - 6.0976 = 8387.5$$

$$A^{N+1} - 1 = 6.0976^{5} - 1 = 8392.6$$

$$\text{Absorption} = \frac{8387.5}{8392.6} = 0.99944$$

> **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute $y_1$:

$$y_1 = y_{N+1} - 0.99944 \times (y_{N+1} - m x_0) = 0.15 - 0.99944 \times 0.1459 = 0.15 - 0.1458 = 0.00416$$

> **Conclusion (Mark):** $y_{CO_2, exit} = 0.00416$ i.e. **97.2% removal** with 4 stages.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option**

> **Option B (a) [7 Marks]:** *Explain the Two-Film Theory of mass transfer. Derive the expression for the overall mass transfer coefficient $K_y$ in terms of individual-film coefficients $k_y$ and $k_x$ and the equilibrium slope $m$.*

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (2 Marks):** Describe the **Two-Film Theory (Lewis-Whitman, 1923)**: A gas-liquid interface is flanked by a **stagnant gas film** of thickness $\delta_G$ and a **stagnant liquid film** of thickness $\delta_L$. Mass transfer occurs by **steady-state molecular diffusion** through these films; the bulk phases are perfectly mixed.

> **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Write the individual flux expressions:

$$N_A = k_y (y - y_i) = k_x (x_i - x)$$

> **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Use the equilibrium relation at the interface, $y_i = m x_i$, to eliminate $x_i$:

$$x_i = \frac{y_i}{m} \Rightarrow N_A = k_x \left( \frac{y_i}{m} - x \right) = \frac{k_x}{m}(y_i - m x)$$

> **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Express the flux in terms of the **overall driving force** $y - y^{*}$ where $y^{*} = m x$:

$$N_A = K_y (y - y^{*}) = K_y (y - m x)$$

> **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Combine the three flux expressions. From the individual-film equations:

$$y - y_i = \frac{N_A}{k_y} \quad ; \quad y_i - m x = \frac{m N_A}{k_x}$$

Adding:

$$y - m x = N_A \left( \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} \right)$$

> **Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}}$$

This is the **additivity of resistances** — the total resistance to mass transfer is the sum of gas-film and liquid-film resistances, with $m$ acting as a "weight factor" that converts the liquid-film resistance to gas-phase units.

---

> **Option B (b) [7 Marks]:** *Formulate the steady-state simulation of a 5-stage gas absorber as a set of equations suitable for solution by `fsolve` in MATLAB. Write the pseudocode and describe the boundary conditions.*

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (2 Marks):** Define state variables. Each stage $j$ has unknowns $(x_j, y_j)$ for the solute, plus a third unknown for the total flow (decoupled under CMO, fixed). So we have $2N = 10$ unknowns.

> **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Write the residual vector $F(y, x)$ of length 10. For each internal stage $j = 1, \ldots, 5$:

$$F_{2j-1} = L x_{j-1} + V y_{j+1} - L x_j - V y_j = 0 \quad \text{(M equation)}$$

$$F_{2j} = y_j - m x_j = 0 \quad \text{(E equation)}$$

> **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply **boundary conditions**:
- **Bottom:** $y_6 = y_{N+1} = 0.15$ (known inlet gas composition).
- **Top:** $x_0 = x_{in} = 0.005$ (known lean-solvent composition).

> **Step 4 (2 Marks):** MATLAB pseudocode:

```matlab
% Gas Absorber Simulation - KTU 2024 Module 3
L  = 5000;     % kmol/h, liquid flow
V  = 1000;     % kmol/h, gas flow
m  = 0.82;     % Henry slope
N  = 5;        % number of stages
y_in  = 0.15;  % bottom gas inlet
x_in  = 0.005; % top liquid inlet

% Initial guess vector [x1, y1, x2, y2, ..., x5, y5]
x0 = 0.05 * ones(2*N, 1);

% Residual function
F = @(z) residuals(z, L, V, m, N, y_in, x_in);

% Solve using fsolve
options = optimoptions('fsolve', 'Display', 'iter', 'TolFun', 1e-9);
z_sol = fsolve(F, x0, options);

% Extract profiles
x_prof = z_sol(1:2:end);
y_prof = z_sol(2:2:end);

% Plot results
figure;
subplot(1,2,1); plot(1:N, y_prof, '-o'); xlabel('Stage'); ylabel('y');
title('Vapour Composition Profile'); grid on;
subplot(1:2, 2); plot(1:N, x_prof, '-s'); xlabel('Stage'); ylabel('x');
title('Liquid Composition Profile'); grid on;
```

> **Step 5 (Mark):** The `residuals` function enforces the **M and E equations** at each stage plus the two boundary conditions (overdetermined system with 10 equations, 10 unknowns, consistent).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $A$ (absorption factor) with $\alpha$ (relative volatility):** Many students interchange these. $A = L/(mV)$ is the slope ratio; $\alpha = K_i/K_{ref}$ is the volatility. **(−1 Mark penalty)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of Henry's constant $H$:** When $H$ is in **atm**, you MUST convert all pressures to atm. Writing $m = P/H$ with $P$ in kPa is a silent error. **(−1 Mark)**
>
> 3. **Sign convention violation in the H equation:** Energy outflow is positive in the KTU convention. Reversing signs loses **1–2 marks** in derivations.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the constant molar overflow (CMO) assumption:** If you assume variable molar overflow without justification, you must write a full energy balance — partial assumptions earn **0 marks** for the H section.
>
> 5. **In Kremser problems, the student often writes $A^{N+1}$ instead of $A^{N+1} - A$ in the numerator.** Read the equation three times before submitting.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to state boundary conditions in `fsolve` problems:** KTU examiners explicitly check for $y_{N+1}$ and $x_0$ being declared. **(−1 to −2 Marks)**
>
> 7. **Confusing Murphree vapour efficiency with Murphree liquid efficiency** in tray-by-tray problems. The two are related by $E_{Mv} = E_{ML} \cdot [1 + E_{ML}(mV/L - 1)]^{-1}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (MUST MEMORISE):**

- **MESH** stands for **Material, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat** balances at every stage.
- **Henry's Law** $y = mx$ is the linearised E equation for dilute systems; $m = P/H$.
- **Kremser equation:** $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ where $A = L/(mV)$.
- **Absorption feasibility:** Operating line must lie **above** equilibrium curve, i.e. $L/V > m$, i.e. $A > 1$.
- **Two-film theory** gives the resistance-in-series formula: $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$.
- **Constant Molar Overflow (CMO)** is the standard KTU assumption that decouples H from M and E.
- **Minimum solvent flow** is $L_{min} = mV$ (i.e. $A = 1$, pinch at the top).
- **Minimum number of stages** is found by the Kremser inversion (numerical) or graphically (McCabe-Thiele stepping).
- **Simulation approach:** (1) MESH assembly → (2) `fsolve` or `ode45` → (3) Plot composition profiles → (4) Compute removal efficiency and NTU.
- **Boundary conditions for column simulation:** Top liquid ($x_0$) and bottom gas ($y_{N+1}$) are known; the unknowns are all internal $(x_j, y_j)$ plus the top vapour $y_1$ and bottom liquid $x_N$.
- **Murphree efficiency** $E_{Mv,j} = (y_j - y_{j+1})/(y_j^{*} - y_{j+1})$ accounts for non-ideal stages; KTU 2024 typically assumes $E = 1$ unless stated.
- **Industrial relevance:** CO₂ capture (MEA, MDEA), Claus process H₂S removal, ammonia scrubbing, and natural gas sweetening all use the MESH framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithm for Solving Continuous Flow Tank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Algorithm for Solving Continuous Flow Tank

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Continuous Flow Tank (CFT)**, more commonly known as a **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)**, is a process vessel into which reactants are fed continuously and from which products are withdrawn continuously, while the contents are maintained in a perfectly mixed state by mechanical agitation. In the context of **Process Modelling and Simulation**, the system is described by a set of coupled **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** derived from the conservation laws of mass, energy, and momentum applied to a control volume.

An **Algorithm for Solving a Continuous Flow Tank** is the formalized, step-by-step computational procedure used to obtain the dynamic or steady-state response of the tank's state variables (concentration, temperature, liquid level) by numerically integrating the governing ODEs from a known initial state, following a chosen discretization scheme (Euler, Runge-Kutta, etc.) until a specified convergence or terminal condition is achieved.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a kitchen washbasin with a continuously running tap and an open drain.**
> - **Inflow ($F_{in}$)** = water from the tap.
> - **Outflow ($F_{out}$)** = water leaving through the drain.
> - **Volume in the basin ($V$)** = water level.
> - **Concentration of a dissolved dye ($C_A$)** = how dark the water is.
>
> If you suddenly drop a pinch of dark dye into the inflow, the basin water will gradually turn dark, then slowly fade as fresh water dilutes it. The **algorithm** is the recipe a computer follows to *predict exactly how dark the water will be at every second*, without actually doing the experiment.

### 1.3 System Parameters and Standard Metrics

The standard engineering metrics and physical parameters tracked in a continuous flow tank are:

- **Volumetric Flow Rate ($F$)** in **m³/s** or **L/min**
- **Tank Volume ($V$)** in **m³**
- **Space Time / Residence Time ($\tau = V/F$)** in **seconds (s)**
- **Reactant Concentration ($C_A$)** in **kmol/m³**
- **Reaction Rate ($-r_A$)** in **kmol/(m³·s)**
- **Density ($\rho$)** in **kg/m³**, taken as **1000 kg/m³** for dilute aqueous systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is on the *algorithm itself* — the systematic sequence of mathematical and logical operations that transform a physical process model into a numerically computable form. Mastery of this topic is a prerequisite for any computer-based simulation exercise in the KTU lab and the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need an Algorithm?

A continuous flow tank is governed by differential equations that, except in trivial cases, **do not have a closed-form analytical solution** when reaction kinetics are nonlinear. We therefore require a *discrete-time numerical algorithm* that approximates the continuous solution by stepping forward in time using small increments.

### 2.2 The Modelling Hierarchy (Operational Logic Steps)

1. **Identify the Control Volume** — fix the tank as the system boundary.
2. **Write the Total Mass Balance** — leads to liquid level dynamics.
3. **Write the Component Mass Balance** — leads to concentration dynamics.
4. **Write the Energy Balance** (if non-isothermal) — leads to temperature dynamics.
5. **Specify Initial Conditions (IC)** — values of all state variables at $t = 0$.
6. **Select a Numerical Integration Method** — e.g., explicit Euler, 4th-order Runge-Kutta.
7. **Choose Integration Step Size ($h$)** — balances accuracy vs. computational cost.
8. **Iterate Forward in Time** — update state variables at each step.
9. **Check Convergence / Termination** — stop when $t$ reaches $t_{final}$ or steady state.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Step 1–4:** Convert the real tank into a system of ODEs.
- **Step 5:** ODEs need a starting point; without IC the solution is non-unique.
- **Step 6:** A *discretization formula* (e.g., Euler or RK4) replaces derivatives with finite differences.
- **Step 7:** A smaller $h$ improves accuracy but increases CPU time.
- **Step 8:** Each iteration produces the state at a new time instant.
- **Step 9:** Prevents the algorithm from running infinitely.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` symbols inside tables to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}$ | Total mass balance (constant $\rho$) | m³/s |
| 2 | $V\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = F_{in} C_{A,in} - F_{out} C_A - V\,k\,C_A$ | Component A balance with 1st-order reaction | kmol/s |
| 3 | $C_{A,ss} = \dfrac{C_{A,in}}{1 + k\tau}$ | Steady-state exit concentration | kmol/m³ |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{F}$ | Space / residence time | s |
| 5 | $C_A^{n+1} = C_A^n + h\,f(t_n, C_A^n)$ | Generic explicit Euler update | kmol/m³ |
| 6 | $k_1 = h\,f(t_n, C_A^n)$ | RK4: stage 1 slope | kmol/m³·s |
| 7 | $k_2 = h\,f\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, C_A^n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)$ | RK4: stage 2 slope | kmol/m³·s |
| 8 | $k_3 = h\,f\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, C_A^n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right)$ | RK4: stage 3 slope | kmol/m³·s |
| 9 | $k_4 = h\,f\!\left(t_n + h, C_A^n + k_3\right)$ | RK4: stage 4 slope | kmol/m³·s |
| 10 | $C_A^{n+1} = C_A^n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$ | RK4: state update | kmol/m³ |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_{ss} = \vert C_A^{n+1} - C_A^{n} \vert$ | Steady-state convergence criterion | kmol/m³ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Continuous flow tank algorithms are the backbone of:

- **Chemical plant startup/shutdown sequence design** — predicting how fast composition and temperature approach operating point.
- **Wastewater treatment plant simulation** — biological CSTRs for BOD removal.
- **Pharmaceutical batch-to-continuous transition** — semi-batch modeling.
- **Polymerization reactor control** — tracking monomer conversion.
- **Disturbance rejection analysis** — sensor noise, feed composition changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Governing ODE (Constant Volume Isothermal CSTR)

**Step 1 — State the General Mass Balance:**

$$
\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Rate In} - \text{Rate Out} + \text{Rate of Generation}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to Component A (with 1st-order reaction $-r_A = k C_A$):**

$$
\frac{d(VC_A)}{dt} = F_{in} C_{A,in} - F_{out} C_A + V(-r_A)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Left Side Using the Product Rule:**

$$
V\frac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A\frac{dV}{dt} = F_{in} C_{A,in} - F_{out} C_A - V k C_A
$$

**Step 4 — Impose the Constant-Volume Constraint $F_{in} = F_{out} = F$:**

Since the inflow equals the outflow, $dV/dt = 0$ and the volume remains fixed. Substituting $F_{in} = F_{out} = F$ gives:

$$
V\frac{dC_A}{dt} = F C_{A,in} - F C_A - V k C_A
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into Standard ODE Form:**

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(C_{A,in} - C_A) - k C_A
$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the Residence Time $\tau = V/F$:**

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = \frac{C_{A,in} - C_A}{\tau} - k C_A
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Initial Condition $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$:**

The complete **Initial Value Problem (IVP)** for the continuous flow tank is:

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = f(t, C_A) = \frac{C_{A,in}}{\tau} - C_A\left(\frac{1}{\tau} + k\right), \quad C_A(0) = C_{A,0}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Solution (for Verification Only)

Because the right-hand side is linear in $C_A$, the ODE has a closed-form solution:

$$
C_A(t) = C_{A,ss} + \left(C_{A,0} - C_{A,ss}\right) e^{-(k + 1/\tau)\,t}
$$

where the steady-state value is:

$$
C_{A,ss} = \frac{C_{A,in}}{1 + k\tau}
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Solution via the Algorithm

We now treat the tank equation as an IVP and discretize time into uniform steps of size $h$.

**Step 8 — Discretize the Time Domain:**

$$
t_n = n\,h, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

**Step 9 — Define the Right-Hand-Side Function:**

$$
f(C_A) = \frac{C_{A,in}}{\tau} - C_A\left(\frac{1}{\tau} + k\right)
$$

**Step 10 — Explicit (Forward) Euler Update Rule:**

$$
C_A^{n+1} = C_A^n + h\, f(C_A^n)
$$

Substituting $f(C_A^n)$:

$$
C_A^{n+1} = C_A^n + h\left[\frac{C_{A,in}}{\tau} - C_A^n\left(\frac{1}{\tau} + k\right)\right]
$$

**Step 11 — 4th-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4) Update Rule (Higher Accuracy):**

$$
k_1 = h\,f(C_A^n)
$$

$$
k_2 = h\,f\!\left(C_A^n + \frac{k_1}{2}\right)
$$

$$
k_3 = h\,f\!\left(C_A^n + \frac{k_2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
k_4 = h\,f\!\left(C_A^n + k_3\right)
$$

$$
C_A^{n+1} = C_A^n + \frac{1}{6}\left(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4\right)
$$

**Step 12 — Convergence Check (Steady-State Stopping Criterion):**

$$
\varepsilon = \vert C_A^{n+1} - C_A^n \vert < \text{TOL} \quad (\text{typically } \text{TOL} = 10^{-6})
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Full Detail)

**Given Data:**

$$
C_{A,in} = 2.0 \text{ kmol/m}^3, \quad C_{A,0} = 0.0 \text{ kmol/m}^3, \quad k = 0.1 \text{ s}^{-1}, \quad \tau = 10 \text{ s}, \quad h = 2 \text{ s}
$$

**Compute Steady State (Analytical Truth):**

$$
C_{A,ss} = \frac{2.0}{1 + (0.1)(10)} = \frac{2.0}{2.0} = 1.0 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

**Numerical Hand Calculation at $n = 0 \rightarrow n = 1$ (Euler):**

$$
f(C_A^0) = \frac{2.0}{10} - 0.0 \left(\frac{1}{10} + 0.1\right) = 0.2 - 0.0 = 0.2
$$

$$
C_A^1 = 0.0 + 2(0.2) = 0.4 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

**At $n = 1 \rightarrow n = 2$:**

$$
f(C_A^1) = 0.2 - 0.4(0.2) = 0.2 - 0.08 = 0.12
$$

$$
C_A^2 = 0.4 + 2(0.12) = 0.64 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

**At $n = 2 \rightarrow n = 3$:**

$$
f(C_A^2) = 0.2 - 0.64(0.2) = 0.2 - 0.128 = 0.072
$$

$$
C_A^3 = 0.64 + 2(0.072) = 0.784 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

**Continuing yields the convergence sequence:**
$C_A^0 = 0.00$, $C_A^1 = 0.40$, $C_A^2 = 0.64$, $C_A^3 = 0.784$, $C_A^4 = 0.8704$, … $\rightarrow C_{A,ss} = 1.00$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical solution **monotonically approaches** the analytical steady state of **1.0 kmol/m³**, confirming the algorithm is **stable** for $h = 2$ s (stability requires $h(1/\tau + k) < 2$, i.e., $h < 10$ s).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm-to-Equation Mapping

| Algorithm Step | Mathematical Operation | Output Variable |
|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Read inputs | Assign $F, V, C_{A,in}, k, h$ | Parameters |
| Initialize | Set $C_A \leftarrow C_{A,0}$, $t \leftarrow 0$ | State |
| Compute RHS | Evaluate $f(C_A)$ | Slope |
| Update state | $C_A \leftarrow C_A + h\,f(C_A)$ | New $C_A$ |
| Increment time | $t \leftarrow t + h$ | New $t$ |
| Convergence test | Check $\vert C_A^{n+1} - C_A^n \vert < \text{TOL}$ | Boolean |
| Loop / Exit | Repeat or stop | Termination |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the CSTR Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Parameters F, V, k, CAin, h, tFinal] --> B[Initialize State: CA = CA0, t = 0]
    B --> C{Is t less than tFinal?}
    C -- No --> Z[Output Final CA Profile and Stop]
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute RHS Function fCA = CAin/tau - CA * 1/tau + k]
    D --> E[Choose Integration Method: Euler or RK4]
    E --> F[Compute Intermediate Slopes k1, k2, k3, k4 if RK4]
    F --> G[Update State CA new = CA old + delta CA]
    G --> H[Increment Time t = t + h]
    H --> I[Check Convergence: epsilon less than TOL]
    I -- Not Converged --> C
    I -- Converged --> J[Flag Steady State Reached]
    J --> K[Output CA steady and Continue or Stop]
    K --> C
```

### 4.2 Physical Schematic of the Continuous Flow Tank (Block-Adapted)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Stream: Fin, CAin] --> B[CSTR Tank: Volume V, Stirrer]
    B --> C[Product Stream: Fout, CA]
    B --> D[Reaction: First Order, Rate Constant k]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Sensor: Measure CA and T]
    E --> F[Controller / Algorithm]
    F -- Adjust F or T --> B
```

### 4.3 Algorithm Decision Tree (Method Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Have ODE dCA/dt = fCA] --> Q1{Need High Accuracy?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Use RK4 Method]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Stiffness suspected?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use Implicit Backward Euler]
    Q2 -- No --> R3[Use Explicit Forward Euler]
    R1 --> END[Output Numerical Solution]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
```

### 4.4 Convergence Behavior Plot (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph time_domain[Time Evolution of CA]
        direction LR
        T0[t = 0] --> T1[t = 10 s]
        T1 --> T2[t = 30 s]
        T2 --> T3[t = 60 s]
        T3 --> T4[t = infinity, Steady State]
    end
    T0 --- V0[CA = 0.0]
    T1 --- V1[CA = 0.40]
    T2 --- V2[CA = 0.64]
    T3 --- V3[CA = 0.78]
    T4 --- V4[CA = 1.00]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *State the general component mass balance for a constant-volume continuous flow tank carrying out a first-order reaction. Identify each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
V\frac{dC_A}{dt} = F\,C_{A,in} - F\,C_A - V\,k\,C_A
$$

- **LHS ($V\,dC_A/dt$):** Rate of accumulation of A inside the tank (kmol/s).
- **First term on RHS ($F\,C_{A,in}$):** Rate at which A enters with the feed.
- **Second term on RHS ($F\,C_A$):** Rate at which A leaves with the outlet.
- **Third term ($-V\,k\,C_A$):** Rate of disappearance of A by chemical reaction (consumption, hence negative sign).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Compare the Explicit Euler method and the 4th-order Runge-Kutta method with respect to accuracy, computational cost, and stability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Explicit Euler | Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4) |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Accuracy (local error) | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h^5)$ |
| Computational cost per step | 1 function evaluation | 4 function evaluations |
| Stability region | Small (conditionally stable) | Larger (more robust) |
| Recommended use | Quick prototyping, stiff analysis with small $h$ | Production-grade simulation |

**Conclusion:** RK4 is the **default choice** for non-stiff CSTR simulation; Euler is used for **pedagogical clarity** in KTU theory exams.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> A constant-volume isothermal CSTR operates at steady state with feed concentration $C_{A,in} = 1.5$ kmol/m³, volumetric flow rate $F = 0.5$ m³/min, tank volume $V = 2$ m³, and a first-order reaction rate constant $k = 0.2$ min⁻¹. Initially, the tank is empty of A ($C_{A,0} = 0$). Starting at $t = 0$, the feed is switched on.
>
> **(a)** Develop the governing ODE and derive the steady-state concentration $C_{A,ss}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the complete algorithm (Euler method) to compute the transient concentration profile and compute the values of $C_A$ at $t = 0, 5, 10, 15, 20$ min using step size $h = 5$ min. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Mass balance (1 mark):**
$$
V\frac{dC_A}{dt} = F\,C_{A,in} - F\,C_A - V\,k\,C_A
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $dC_A/dt$ (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(C_{A,in} - C_A) - k\,C_A
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute given data (1 mark):**
- $\tau = V/F = 2/0.5 = 4$ min
- $1/\tau + k = 0.25 + 0.2 = 0.45$ min⁻¹

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = \frac{1.5}{4} - 0.45\,C_A = 0.375 - 0.45\,C_A
$$

**Step 4 — At steady state, $dC_A/dt = 0$ (1 mark):**

$$
0 = 0.375 - 0.45\,C_{A,ss}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C_{A,ss}$ (1 mark):**

$$
C_{A,ss} = \frac{0.375}{0.45} = 0.833 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check with formula (1 mark):**
$$
C_{A,ss} = \frac{C_{A,in}}{1 + k\tau} = \frac{1.5}{1 + (0.2)(4)} = \frac{1.5}{1.8} = 0.833 \text{ kmol/m}^3 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — State the IVP (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = 0.375 - 0.45\,C_A, \quad C_A(0) = 0
$$

#### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

**Euler update rule (1 mark):**
$$
C_A^{n+1} = C_A^n + h\,(0.375 - 0.45\,C_A^n), \quad h = 5
$$

**Algorithm steps (1 mark):**
1. Read parameters: $C_{A,in}, F, V, k, h, C_{A,0}$
2. Initialize: $C_A = 0$, $t = 0$
3. Loop: compute slope $f = 0.375 - 0.45\,C_A$
4. Update: $C_A \leftarrow C_A + 5f$
5. Increment $t$ and repeat

**Iteration 1: $t = 0 \rightarrow t = 5$ (1 mark):**
- $f = 0.375 - 0.45(0) = 0.375$
- $C_A(5) = 0 + 5(0.375) = 1.875$ kmol/m³

**Iteration 2: $t = 5 \rightarrow t = 10$ (1 mark):**
- $f = 0.375 - 0.45(1.875) = 0.375 - 0.84375 = -0.46875$
- $C_A(10) = 1.875 + 5(-0.46875) = 1.875 - 2.34375 = -0.469$ kmol/m³

**Iteration 3: $t = 10 \rightarrow t = 15$ (1 mark):**
- The result becomes **negative** — physically impossible.

**Iteration 4: $t = 15 \rightarrow t = 20$ (1 mark):**
- The solution **diverges** / oscillates wildly.

**Diagnosis (1 mark):**
- The explicit Euler step $h = 5$ min violates the stability condition: $h(1/\tau + k) < 2$ → $5 \times 0.45 = 2.25 > 2$. The algorithm is **unstable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students routinely lose **2–3 marks** by failing to **check numerical stability** after computing the iterations. A step size that produces physically impossible negative concentrations is a clear sign of instability. The correct professional answer is to **reduce $h$ (e.g., $h = 1$ min)** or **switch to RK4**, not to "force" the result to be positive.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 4 Choice]**
>
> A non-isothermal CSTR with constant volume $V = 1$ m³ carries out an irreversible first-order exothermic reaction $A \rightarrow B$. The feed enters with $C_{A,in} = 3$ kmol/m³, $T_{in} = 350$ K, $F = 1$ m³/min, and $k = 10^{13} \exp(-10000/T)$ min⁻¹ (with $T$ in K). Initial conditions: $C_A(0) = 3$ kmol/m³, $T(0) = 350$ K. Liquid density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4.2$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta H = -2.5 \times 10^5$ kJ/kmol.
>
> **(a)** Derive the coupled ODE system for $C_A$ and $T$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Draw the flowchart of the algorithm (RK4) to solve this coupled system and discuss why RK4 is preferred over Euler here. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

**Component balance (2 marks):**
$$
V\frac{dC_A}{dt} = F(C_{A,in} - C_A) - V k C_A
$$

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(C_{A,in} - C_A) - k C_A
$$

**Energy balance (3 marks):**
$$
V\rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F\rho C_p(T_{in} - T) + (-\Delta H) V k C_A
$$

Dividing by $V\rho C_p$:

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(T_{in} - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H)\,k\,C_A}{\rho\,C_p}
$$

**Numerical substitution (1 mark):**
- $F/V = 1$ min⁻¹
- $(-\Delta H)/(\rho C_p) = (2.5 \times 10^5)/(1000 \times 4.2) = 59.52$ K·m³/kmol

**Final coupled IVP (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = (3 - C_A) - k C_A, \quad C_A(0) = 3
$$

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = (350 - T) + 59.52\,k\,C_A, \quad T(0) = 350
$$

where $k = 10^{13}\exp(-10000/T)$.

#### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

**RK4 Algorithm for coupled system (4 marks):**
For a state vector $\mathbf{y} = [C_A, T]^T$ and $f_1, f_2$ the two RHS functions:

$$
k_1^{(1)} = h f_1(y^n), \quad k_1^{(2)} = h f_2(y^n)
$$

$$
k_2^{(1)} = h f_1\!\left(y^n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right), \quad k_2^{(2)} = h f_2\!\left(y^n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)
$$

(and similarly $k_3, k_4$)

$$
y^{n+1} = y^n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)
$$

**Flowchart description (2 marks):** Read parameters → Initialize $[C_A, T]$ → Loop: compute $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$ for **both** ODEs → Update state → Check convergence → Output.

**Why RK4 over Euler (1 mark):** The Arrhenius term $10^{13}\exp(-10000/T)$ makes the RHS **highly nonlinear and stiff**. Euler's tiny stability radius forces impractically small $h$; RK4's larger stability region permits a reasonable step size while keeping truncation error $O(h^5)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> In coupled systems, students frequently **forget to propagate the intermediate stages $k_2, k_3$ correctly** for both equations simultaneously. Each stage must use the **same $t_n + h/2$ or $t_n + h$** for both ODEs. Mixing time points across the two equations is the single most common error in KTU answer scripts and costs **2 marks** outright.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Algorithm for Solving Continuous Flow Tank**

- **Core ODE (constant volume, 1st order):** $V\,dC_A/dt = F(C_{A,in} - C_A) - V k C_A$
- **Steady-state formula:** $C_{A,ss} = C_{A,in}/(1 + k\tau)$, where $\tau = V/F$
- **Explicit Euler update:** $C_A^{n+1} = C_A^n + h\,f(C_A^n)$ — first-order accurate
- **RK4 update:** uses four slope evaluations $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$; fourth-order accurate
- **Stability limit for Euler:** $h(1/\tau + k) < 2$ (must always be checked!)
- **Initial conditions are mandatory** — the IVP is incomplete without $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$
- **Convergence test:** stop when $\vert C_A^{n+1} - C_A^n \vert < 10^{-6}$
- **For coupled systems** (e.g., non-isothermal CSTR with $C_A$ and $T$): each RK4 stage must evaluate **all** ODEs at the same intermediate state.
- **Physical sanity check:** concentration must remain $\geq 0$ at every step; negative values = instability.
- **Algorithm must include** parameter input, initialization, iterative loop, update rule, time advance, and termination logic.
- **Default method for KTU board problems:** Euler for short 3-mark derivations; RK4 for full 14-mark numerical simulations.
- **Watch out for the $\tau$ vs. $V/F$ notation** — they are identical; examiners will accept either.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isothermal CSTR in Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT631 - PROCESS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Isothermal CSTR in Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Isothermal CSTR in Series (Cascade of Equal-Sized CSTRs):**
> A reactor configuration in which **N** continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTRs) of **equal volume (V)** are connected in a sequential flow path, operating at a **constant temperature (T)**, where the effluent of reactor (i−1) becomes the feed to reactor *i*. The total residence time is the arithmetic sum of the individual reactor residence times.

Mathematically, the cascade is governed by the **species material balance** applied independently to each stage:

$$F_{A,i-1} - F_{A,i} + r_{A,i}\,V_i = 0$$

where $F_{A,i}$ is the molar flow rate of species *A* leaving reactor *i*, $r_{A,i}$ is the rate of reaction evaluated at the exit conditions of reactor *i* (this is the defining assumption of a CSTR — perfect mixing, so the exit concentration equals the concentration inside), and $V_i$ is the volume of reactor *i*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Pill Bottle Filling Line"**
> Imagine a pharmaceutical line where coloured pills dissolve in water. If you have **one giant mixing tank** (single CSTR), some pills spend a long time and some leave almost immediately — the *conversion* is mediocre. Now, instead, split that giant tank into **five smaller tanks in a row**, with water flowing from one to the next. Each pill must pass through *all five* tanks before exiting, ensuring much longer and more uniform exposure. The total volume is the same, but the *overall conversion* is much higher. **CSTRs in series = staged dilution of unreacted material with progressively less unreacted material available downstream.**

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Volumetric flow rate (v₀):** typically in **m³·s⁻¹** or **L·s⁻¹**
- **Reactor volume per stage (V):** in **m³**
- **Space time (τ):** $\tau = V/v_0$ in **seconds (s)**
- **Rate constant (k):** units depend on reaction order — **s⁻¹** (1st order), **m³·(kmol·s)⁻¹** (2nd order)
- **Total volume of cascade (V_T):** $V_T = N \cdot V$

## 1.4 Why Study CSTRs in Series?

> [!NOTE]
> CSTRs in series act as the **mathematical bridge** between a single CSTR (lowest conversion for a given total volume, for positive-order reactions) and a PFR (highest conversion). As **N → ∞**, the cascade mathematically approaches a PFR, while **N = 1** recovers a single CSTR. This is a classic **KTU 2024 outcome** for understanding the **extremes of reactor design**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

For an isothermal cascade of **N equal-volume CSTRs** processing a single reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ with rate $-r_A = k\,C_A^n$:

- **Step 1: Write the steady-state molar balance for each stage *i*.**
  The steady-state operating assumption means **no accumulation**, so:
  $$F_{A,i-1} - F_{A,i} + r_{A,i}\,V = 0$$

- **Step 2: Express in terms of concentration and space time.**
  Using $F_A = v_0 C_A$ and $\tau = V/v_0$, this simplifies to:
  $$C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i} + r_{A,i}\,\tau = 0$$

- **Step 3: Substitute the rate law.**
  For a first-order reaction ($n = 1$): $-r_A = k C_A$
  $$C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i} = k\,C_{A,i}\,\tau$$

- **Step 4: Solve for the exit concentration of each stage.**
  Rearranging the first-order expression:
  $$C_{A,i} = \frac{C_{A,i-1}}{1 + k\tau}$$
  This is a **geometric decay** relationship across stages.

- **Step 5: Generalise across all N stages.**
  The exit concentration of the final stage is:
  $$C_{A,N} = \frac{C_{A,0}}{(1 + k\tau)^N}$$

- **Step 6: Convert to overall conversion.**
  Using $X_N = 1 - C_{A,N}/C_{A,0}$:
  $$X_N = 1 - \frac{1}{(1 + k\tau)^N}$$

- **Step 7: Engineering interpretation.**
  The term $(1 + k\tau)^N$ is the **amplification factor** — as N increases, conversion increases monotonically (for $k\tau > 0$).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield equations** for the topic. Master these before attempting any numerical.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Applicable Reaction | Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | Space time per stage | $\tau = V/v_0$ | All | s |
| 2 | Stage material balance | $C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i} = -r_{A,i}\,\tau$ | All | kmol·m⁻³ |
| 3 | 1st-order exit concentration | $C_{A,i} = C_{A,i-1}/(1 + k\tau)$ | $-r_A = kC_A$ | kmol·m⁻³ |
| 4 | 1st-order overall conversion | $X_N = 1 - (1 + k\tau)^{-N}$ | $-r_A = kC_A$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | 2nd-order ($2A \to P$) stage balance | $k\tau\,C_{A,i}^{2} + C_{A,i} - C_{A,i-1} = 0$ | $-r_A = kC_A^{2}$ | varies |
| 6 | 2nd-order $C_{A,i}$ (quadratic solution) | $C_{A,i} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4k\tau\,C_{A,i-1}}}{2k\tau}$ | $-r_A = kC_A^{2}$ | kmol·m⁻³ |
| 7 | Total volume for given $X_N$ (1st order) | $V_T = \frac{N\,v_0}{k}\left[\left(\frac{1}{1-X_N}\right)^{1/N} - 1\right]$ | $-r_A = kC_A$ | m³ |
| 8 | CSTR → PFR limit | $\displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty} X_N = 1 - e^{-k\tau_T}$ | $-r_A = kC_A$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Single CSTR limit (N=1) | $X_1 = k\tau/(1 + k\tau)$ | $-r_A = kC_A$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Overall conversion (multi-stage) | $X_N = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - X_i)$ | All orders | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Convention Used:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in formula (1) above denotes absolute magnitude. In this entire sheet, $C_{A,i}$ is the exit concentration of stage *i* and $C_{A,i-1}$ is its inlet (which equals the exit of stage *i-1*).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Petrochemical Industry:** Hydrocrackers and hydrotreaters are staged CSTR-like reactors where gradual conversion prevents hot spots and runaway reactions.
> - **Polymerisation Reactors:** Polymerisation reactions are often highly exothermic; isothermal staging prevents thermal runaway.
> - **Wastewater Treatment:** Activated sludge reactors are commonly configured as 3–4 CSTRs in series to achieve progressive BOD reduction.
> - **Pharma & Biotech:** Multi-stage continuous-flow reactors enable precise control over intermediate concentrations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Reaction in N Equal CSTRs in Series

**Starting Equation (general species balance for stage i):**

$$F_{A,i-1} - F_{A,i} + r_{A,i}\,V = 0$$

**Substitute $F_A = v_0 C_A$ and divide through by $v_0$:**

$$C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i} + \frac{r_{A,i}\,V}{v_0} = 0$$

**Recognise $\tau = V/v_0$:**

$$C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i} + r_{A,i}\,\tau = 0$$

**Apply the first-order rate law $-r_A = k C_A$. Since the CSTR is perfectly mixed, the rate is evaluated at the exit composition $C_{A,i}$:**

$$C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i} + (-k C_{A,i})\,\tau = 0$$

**Group terms containing $C_{A,i}$:**

$$C_{A,i-1} = C_{A,i} + k\tau\,C_{A,i}$$

**Factor $C_{A,i}$ on the right-hand side:**

$$C_{A,i-1} = C_{A,i}(1 + k\tau)$$

**Solve for $C_{A,i}$:**

$$C_{A,i} = \frac{C_{A,i-1}}{1 + k\tau}$$

**Apply this recurrence relation N times. Reactor 1:**

$$C_{A,1} = \frac{C_{A,0}}{1 + k\tau}$$

**Reactor 2 (using $C_{A,1}$ as input):**

$$C_{A,2} = \frac{C_{A,1}}{1 + k\tau} = \frac{C_{A,0}}{(1 + k\tau)^2}$$

**Reactor 3:**

$$C_{A,3} = \frac{C_{A,2}}{1 + k\tau} = \frac{C_{A,0}}{(1 + k\tau)^3}$$

**Generalise for the N-th reactor:**

$$C_{A,N} = \frac{C_{A,0}}{(1 + k\tau)^N}$$

**Convert to overall conversion using the definition $X_N = (C_{A,0} - C_{A,N})/C_{A,0}$:**

$$X_N = 1 - \frac{C_{A,N}}{C_{A,0}} = 1 - \frac{1}{(1 + k\tau)^N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important derivation in this module.** KTU examiners frequently set 7–14 mark problems testing this result.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Volume of N Equal CSTRs for Specified Conversion

We are required to find $V_T = N V$ given a desired $X_N$ for a first-order reaction.

**Start with the conversion expression:**

$$X_N = 1 - \frac{1}{(1 + k\tau)^N}$$

**Isolate the $(1 + k\tau)^N$ term:**

$$1 - X_N = \frac{1}{(1 + k\tau)^N}$$

**Take the N-th root of both sides:**

$$(1 - X_N)^{1/N} = \frac{1}{1 + k\tau}$$

**Invert:**

$$\frac{1}{(1 - X_N)^{1/N}} = 1 + k\tau$$

**Subtract 1 from both sides:**

$$\frac{1}{(1 - X_N)^{1/N}} - 1 = k\tau$$

**Substitute $\tau = V/v_0$:**

$$\frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{1}{k}\left[\frac{1}{(1 - X_N)^{1/N}} - 1\right]$$

**Multiply by N to obtain the total volume $V_T = N V$:**

$$V_T = \frac{N\,v_0}{k}\left[\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_N}\right)^{1/N} - 1\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting case check:** As $N \to \infty$, the term $\left[(1-X_N)^{-1/N}\right]^N$ can be analysed using the standard limit $\lim_{N\to\infty}(1 + a/N)^N = e^a$. Setting $a = -\ln(1-X_N)$ confirms the PFR result $V_T = (v_0/k)\ln[1/(1-X_N)]$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Second-Order Reaction ($2A \to$ Products) in Two CSTRs in Series

For the reaction $-r_A = k C_A^2$, the stage balance becomes:

$$C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i} = k C_{A,i}^2 \tau$$

**Re-arrange to a standard quadratic form $a C_{A,i}^2 + b C_{A,i} + c = 0$:**

$$k\tau C_{A,i}^2 + C_{A,i} - C_{A,i-1} = 0$$

**Apply the quadratic formula $C_{A,i} = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = k\tau$, $b = 1$, $c = -C_{A,i-1}$:**

$$C_{A,i} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 4k\tau C_{A,i-1}}}{2k\tau}$$

**Reject the negative root (physically inadmissible for concentration). Take the positive root:**

$$C_{A,i} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4k\tau C_{A,i-1}}}{2k\tau}$$

**For Reactor 1, substitute $C_{A,0}$:**

$$C_{A,1} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4k\tau C_{A,0}}}{2k\tau}$$

**For Reactor 2, substitute $C_{A,1}$:**

$$C_{A,2} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4k\tau C_{A,1}}}{2k\tau}$$

**Overall conversion for two equal CSTRs:**

$$X_2 = 1 - \frac{C_{A,2}}{C_{A,0}}$$

This must be solved **iteratively** or numerically for the second-order case — no closed-form solution exists for $N \geq 2$ in second order.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (First Order, 3-Stage Cascade)

**Given:** $C_{A,0} = 2$ kmol·m⁻³, $k = 0.5$ min⁻¹, $\tau = 2$ min per stage, $N = 3$.

**Stage 1:**

$$C_{A,1} = \frac{2}{1 + (0.5)(2)} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0 \text{ kmol·m}^{-3}$$

**Stage 2:**

$$C_{A,2} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ kmol·m}^{-3}$$

**Stage 3:**

$$C_{A,3} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ kmol·m}^{-3}$$

**Overall conversion using the closed-form formula:**

$$X_3 = 1 - \frac{0.25}{2.0} = 1 - \frac{1}{(1+0.5\times 2)^3} = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = 0.875 \text{ or } 87.5\%$$

**Comparison with a single CSTR of total volume $V_T = 3V$ (i.e., $\tau_T = 6$ min):**

$$X_1^{\text{equivalent}} = \frac{k\tau_T}{1 + k\tau_T} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \text{ or } 75\%$$

**Conclusion:** Three equal CSTRs in series achieve **87.5% conversion** versus **75%** for a single CSTR of equal total volume — a clear **12.5 percentage-point improvement**, illustrating the engineering motivation for staging.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 3-Stage Isothermal CSTR Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FEED STREAM"] --> B["CSTR 1"]
    B --> C["CSTR 2"]
    C --> D["CSTR 3"]
    D --> E["PRODUCT STREAM"]
    F["FRESH FEED FA0"] --> A
    G["CA0 INLET CONC"] --> A
    H["VOLUME V PER STAGE"] --> B
    H --> C
    H --> D
    I["SPACE TIME TAU"] --> B
    I --> C
    I --> D
    J["EXIT CONC CA1"] --> B
    K["EXIT CONC CA2"] --> C
    L["EXIT CONC CA3"] --> D
    M["OVERALL CONVERSION XN"] --> E
```

## 4.2 Detailed Process Flow with Mass Balance Annotations

```mermaid

    Mid1 --> R2["REACTOR 2\nV\ntau = V by v0"]:::reactor
    R2 --> Mid2["v0 CA2"]:::mid
    Mid2 --> R3["REACTOR 3\nV\ntau = V by v0"]:::reactor
    R3 --> Out3["v0 CA3\nFINAL EXIT"]:::out
    Sink1["Rate rA1 V"]:::sink --> R1
    Sink2["Rate rA2 V"]:::sink --> R2
    Sink3["Rate rA3 V"]:::sink --> R3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["N equals 1"]:::n1 --> Q1["Single CSTR\nX = k tau by 1 + k tau"]:::n1
    Start2["N equals 2"]:::n2 --> Q2["Two CSTRs in series\nX = 1 - 1 by 1 + k tau squared"]:::n2
    Start3["N equals 3"]:::n3 --> Q3["Three CSTRs in series\nX = 1 - 1 by 1 + k tau cubed"]:::n3
    StartN["N large"]:::nn --> QN["Limit N infinity\nX approaches 1 - exp minus k tauT\nPFR EQUIVALENT"]:::nn
```
